title: The Problem of Pain in Rheumatology: Variations in Case Definitions Derived from Chronic Pain Phenotyping Algorithms using Electronic Health Records
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pain is a significant burden in autoimmune rheumatic diseases (ARDs) such as systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren's syndrome, and systemic sclerosis, affecting up to 65% of patients who require long-term opioid therapies and substantially reducing health-related quality of life and disability. Using electronic health records, this study developed multiple phenotyping algorithms to identify chronic pain cases based on pain scores, diagnostic codes, analgesic prescriptions, and pain interventions, revealing considerable variation in their correlations (ranging from 0.23 to 0.50). Notable demographic disparities emerged: females exhibited markedly higher prevalence and intensity of chronic pain across all phenotyping measures compared to males, and Black patients showed substantially higher chronic pain prevalence (42%) than Asian patients (27%), with publicly insured individuals also disproportionately affected. The research underscores how ARD patients frequently experience co-occurring chronic pain from non-rheumatologic conditions including migraine, endometriosis, irritable bowel syndrome, and fibromyalgia, managed through diverse therapeutic approaches including NSAIDs, anticonvulsants, antidepressants, physical therapy, and acupuncture. These findings highlight critical gaps in pain case definition standardization and document pronounced sex and racial disparities in both the burden of chronic pain and treatment patterns within rheumatologic populations.

title: The Problem of Pain in Lupus: Epidemiological Profiles of Patients Attending Multidisciplinary Pain Clinics
journal: Pain management nursing : official journal of the American Society of Pain Management Nurses
summary:
# Summary

Systemic lupus erythematosus causes diverse pain manifestations including musculoskeletal pain, headaches, abdominal pain, pleuritis, secondary fibromyalgia, and Raynaud's phenomenon, with pain frequently serving as an initial disease symptom. More than half of SLE patients develop concomitant chronic overlapping pain conditions, most commonly chronic low back pain (36.6%) and fibromyalgia (30.7%). Patients with SLE requiring specialized pain clinic care represent a more severely affected population, characterized by higher rates of mental health comorbidities (76.7%), depression, anxiety, mood disorders, and substance/alcohol use disorders compared to non-pain clinic SLE patients. Pain clinic attendees also demonstrate greater overall disease burden, including significantly more comorbidities, higher body mass index, and greater reliance on public health insurance. The co-occurrence of pain with depression in SLE patients leads to worse clinical outcomes and elevated healthcare costs, underscoring the importance of multidisciplinary pain management approaches that address both physical and psychiatric dimensions of this autoimmune disease.

title: Preliminary Validation of a General Factor Model of Chronic Overlapping Pain Conditions
summary:
# Summary

This study validates a bifactor model of chronic overlapping pain conditions (COPCs) that reveals distinct yet interconnected contributors to pain and comorbidity patterns. The model identifies a general factor representing broad liability for COPCs, alongside specific factors for somatic symptoms and neck/shoulder pain. The general COPC factor demonstrates strong criterion validity, with each unit increase associated with 1.71-fold higher odds of clinically diagnosed COPC, increased pain-related healthcare utilization, elevated risks for depression and bipolar disorder, and significantly increased all-cause mortality (aOR=1.25) and fatal overdose risk. The somatic symptoms specific factor shows the strongest associations with adverse mental health outcomes and medication receipt, including opioids and benzodiazepines, and independently predicts overdose risk (aOR=1.94 for fatal overdose). In contrast, the neck/shoulder pain specific factor demonstrates weak or protective associations with most outcomes, including lower mortality risk. These findings suggest central sensitization may underlie the general factor, offering a neurobiological explanation for why COPCs cluster together and highlighting distinct pathways through which different pain manifestations influence psychiatric comorbidity, substance use, and mortality risk.

title: Pleiotropy and genetically inferred causality linking multisite chronic pain to substance use disorders
summary: This study reveals a bidirectional genetic relationship between multisite chronic pain and substance use disorders, with multisite chronic pain demonstrating stronger causal effects on alcohol use disorder, cannabis use disorder, and problematic tobacco use than the reverse. Genome-wide analysis identified pleiotropic genetic variants—particularly in the IHO1 and CADM2 genes—that influence both chronic pain and multiple substance use disorders, suggesting shared genetic architecture underlying these comorbid conditions. The IHO1 rs7652746 variant associates with cerebellar and amygdala imaging phenotypes, brain regions implicated in both pain perception and addiction-related emotion dysregulation, while CADM2 encodes a synaptic adhesion protein involved in neuronal organization and impulsivity—a trait genetically correlated with both chronic pain and substance use vulnerability. Additional pleiotropic variants in SLC39A8 and NCAM1 further support the shared genetic basis across pain and specific substance use phenotypes. The findings suggest that substance use may paradoxically increase pain sensitivity through biological mechanisms involving alcohol, cannabis, and tobacco, while simultaneously chronic pain patients may self-medicate with these substances, creating a reinforcing cycle. These results highlight the neurobiological underpinnings of pain-addiction comorbidity and have implications for understanding the opioid epidemic and developing integrated treatment approaches for patients with concurrent chronic pain and substance use disorders.

title: Subgroups of pelvic pain are differentially associated with endometriosis and inflammatory comorbidities: a latent class analysis
summary: This latent class analysis identified distinct subgroups of pelvic pain patients with differential associations to endometriosis and comorbid inflammatory conditions. The study revealed that chronic pelvic pain is multifactorial, with the most severe subgroup—characterized by both severe cyclic and acyclic pain—showing the highest burden of comorbidities and the strongest association with surgically-diagnosed endometriosis (94.1% prevalence). Notably, endometriosis prevalence varied dramatically across pain subgroups (4.0% to 94.1%), and migraine emerged as the only condition associated with all four pain subgroups, while fibromyalgia was uniquely linked to the most severe pain phenotype. The mechanistic underpinnings involve neuro-immune interactions, including hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation leading to corticotropin-releasing factor elevation, mast cell activation, and central pain sensitization with widespread pain hypersensitivity. These findings suggest that endometriosis-associated pelvic pain involves both local inflammatory pathology and central sensitization mechanisms, and that the overlap of endometriosis with conditions like migraine, fibromyalgia, and allergies reflects shared neuroinflammatory and neuro-immune dysregulation rather than isolated organ pathology.

title: Anxiety and Depression in Youth With Chronic Pain
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 79 studies encompassing 12,614 youth with chronic pain demonstrates a substantial comorbidity between chronic pain and psychiatric symptoms, with youth experiencing chronic pain exhibiting significantly greater anxiety symptoms (effect size g = 0.61) and depression symptoms (g = 0.74) compared to controls. The relationship between pain and mental health is bidirectional, with anxiety and depression functioning both as consequences of chronic pain and as factors that may perpetuate pain and associated disability. Multiple demographic and clinical variables moderate the strength of these associations, including patient sex, age, and pain location, suggesting heterogeneity in the psychological burden experienced across different pediatric pain populations. Notably, both anxiety and depression are linked to increased functional disability in youth with chronic pain, highlighting the compounding impact of comorbid psychiatric symptoms on overall health outcomes. These findings underscore the importance of integrated assessment and treatment approaches that address the psychological dimensions of pediatric chronic pain rather than treating pain in isolation.

title: Endometriosis Patients Have an Increased Risk of Experiencing Long-Covid Symptoms: Results from a Cross-Sectional Multicenter Study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that women with surgically confirmed endometriosis have approximately a twofold increased risk of developing long COVID symptoms compared to unaffected women, with the risk further elevated by comorbid psychiatric conditions such as anxiety disorder and depression (odds ratios of 2.08 and 2.02, respectively). The underlying mechanisms appear to involve shared pathophysiological features, particularly a hypercoagulable state associated with both endometriosis and long COVID, potentially driven by elevated estrogen and progesterone levels and the formation of microclots characteristic of post-COVID infection. Long COVID manifestations in endometriosis patients encompass multi-system symptoms including fatigue, pelvic pain, headaches, brain fog, and dyspnea, which collectively impair quality of life and restrict occupational, leisure, and family activities. Additional risk factors include fibromyalgia (odds ratio 3.19), migraine, and dysmenorrhea, with dysmenorrhea combined with endometriosis conferring a particularly elevated risk. The study further reveals that patients with endometriosis who develop long COVID experience significantly heightened menstrual pain disturbance and migraine frequency, suggesting that hormonal factors may amplify susceptibility to prolonged post-infectious sequelae in this population.

title: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) and Smoking: An Evaluation of IBS Symptom Severity and Anxiety Sensitivity among Adults in the United States
summary: This study examined how irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) severity and anxiety sensitivity independently predict smoking cessation difficulties among U.S. adults with IBS. Both IBS symptom severity and anxiety sensitivity were significantly associated with greater perceived barriers to quitting, more severe problems during quit attempts, and stronger negative reinforcement smoking expectancies—whereby individuals smoke to avoid or manage aversive bodily sensations. The research revealed moderate to strong correlations between IBS severity and cigarette dependence, as well as between anxiety sensitivity and smoking dependence, suggesting that gastrointestinal dysfunction and heightened fear of bodily sensations reinforce continued smoking behavior. Anxiety sensitivity appears particularly relevant given its established link to increased risk of aversive bodily experiences and subsequent smoking relapse. These findings identify IBS severity and anxiety sensitivity as important vulnerability factors for sustained smoking among adults with IBS, highlighting the need for smoking cessation interventions that address gut-brain dysfunction and anxiety-related coping mechanisms in this population.

title: When Thinking About Pain Contributes to Suffering: The Example of Pain Catastrophizing
summary:
# Summary

Pain catastrophizing—the tendency to magnify pain threats and adopt negative pain-related thoughts—is a potent psychological factor that transforms acute pain into chronic suffering through multiple biological and behavioral pathways. Originating in childhood through parental modeling, learning experiences, and caregiver emotional availability, pain catastrophizing becomes a stable cognitive style influenced by genetic susceptibility and environmental stressors that shape developing pain regulatory systems. This mindset activates key neurobiological processes including altered default mode network connectivity, central sensitization, impaired descending pain modulation, and dysregulated stress responses (cortisol), while simultaneously amplifying pain perception, spreading pain to new anatomical areas, and increasing risk for disability, depression, anxiety, and opioid misuse. Pain catastrophizing associates with structural brain changes including gray matter density reduction and correlates with inflammatory markers like IL-6, demonstrating integration across cognitive, emotional, and biological levels. Critically, this trajectory is modifiable: interventions that reduce catastrophizing produce corresponding reductions in brain connectivity and pain outcomes, and resilience factors like optimism can buffer against catastrophizing's harmful effects. The evidence indicates pain catastrophizing represents a teachable, treatable psychological mechanism central to understanding why thoughts about pain profoundly contribute to suffering and chronicity across the lifespan.

title: PTSD symptoms, pain catastrophizing, and pain outcomes after acute orthopedic injury
journal: Pain Medicine: The Official Journal of the American Academy of Pain Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) symptoms contribute to chronic pain outcomes following acute orthopedic injury, with particular focus on pain catastrophizing as a mediating mechanism. PTSD was significantly associated with greater chronic pain incidence, though the relationship operated through distinct pathways depending on pain context. Pain catastrophizing largely mediated the relationship between re-experiencing, avoidance, and negative cognitive/mood alterations with pain at rest, while hyperarousal emerged as a unique predictor of pain intensity during activity and physical dysfunction independent of catastrophizing. Notably, PTSD symptoms maintained significant associations with activity-related pain even when catastrophizing was included in statistical models, suggesting multiple mechanisms linking trauma symptoms to pain outcomes. The findings indicate that interventions targeting pain catastrophizing may be most effective for reducing resting pain associated with certain PTSD symptom clusters, while addressing hyperarousal may be particularly important for improving physical functioning and activity-related pain management in acute pain populations.

title: Functional Connectivity Associations with Menstrual Pain Characteristics in Adolescents: An Investigation of the Triple Network Model
summary: This study reveals that primary dysmenorrhea in adolescent girls involves altered functional connectivity within the triple network model of pain processing. Central sensitization contributes to heightened pain severity and sensitivity in affected individuals, with abnormal activation patterns in multiple brain networks compared to controls. The cingulo-opercular salience network shows increased connectivity with regions including the amygdala, hippocampus, insula, and auditory cortex in proportion to menstrual pain severity and interference, reflecting enhanced encoding of pain's behavioral relevance. Both the left and right central executive networks demonstrate heightened connectivity with posterior regions and increased activity correlating with pain severity and functional impairment. Notably, cumulative menstrual pain exposure associates with progressive alterations in default mode network connectivity, suggesting that repeated pain episodes drive neurobiological changes that may predispose adolescents to future chronic pain conditions. These findings indicate that menstrual pain in adolescence represents a dynamic process involving stable neural dysfunction in pain-processing networks, with potential implications for chronic pain development.

title: Chronic pelvic pain among transgender men and gender diverse adults assigned female at birth
summary: This study examines chronic pelvic pain (CPP) among transgender men and gender diverse adults assigned female at birth, identifying multiple biological and psychological correlates. The research documents high prevalence associations between CPP and several gynecological and gastrointestinal conditions, including irritable bowel syndrome (37% prevalence), polycystic ovary syndrome (20%), uterine fibroids (9%), inflammatory bowel disease, kidney stones, and pelvic inflammatory disease, as well as post-gynecological procedures such as hysterectomy and oophorectomy. Mental health comorbidities including post-traumatic stress disorder, depression, and anxiety showed strong correlations with CPP in this population. Notably, current testosterone use was associated with a 21% lower prevalence of CPP compared to non-users, though longitudinal analysis revealed that approximately 19% of participants developed new-onset pelvic pain after initiating testosterone treatment. These findings suggest that CPP in transgender and gender diverse individuals involves complex interactions between gynecological pathology, gastrointestinal dysfunction, psychological factors, and hormone therapy effects.

title: Symptomatic Autonomic Dysfunction in Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome
summary: Interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) is characterized by widespread autonomic nervous system dysfunction that extends beyond bladder-specific symptoms, particularly in non-bladder-centric patient subgroups. Patients with high autonomic symptom loads, measured using the COMPASS-31 questionnaire, demonstrate dysregulation across multiple autonomic domains including orthostatic intolerance, vasomotor, secretomotor, gastrointestinal, bladder, and pupillomotor functions. This elevated autonomic dysfunction correlates with higher bladder capacity, increased genitourinary symptom severity, and significantly greater prevalence of multiple comorbid conditions including fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, migraines, endometriosis, pelvic floor dysfunction, and vulvodynia. The non-bladder-centric IC/BPS phenotype, characterized by fewer Hunner lesions and larger bladder capacity, shows the strongest association with high autonomic symptom loads and appears driven by central nervous system sensitization rather than localized bladder pathology. These findings suggest that IC/BPS represents a disorder of systemic nervous system dysfunction with heterogeneous clinical presentations, where the COMPASS-31 questionnaire may serve as a valuable tool for phenotyping patients and tailoring therapeutic approaches.

title: Modifiable factors for irritable bowel syndrome: evidence from Mendelian randomisation approach
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identified numerous modifiable and genetic factors causally associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). Psychologically, IBS showed strong causal relationships with depression (OR=1.50), neuroticism (OR=1.20), and reduced well-being indicators including lower life satisfaction (OR=0.31) and positive affect (OR=0.30), establishing mental health as a key determinant. Physiologically, IBS demonstrated robust causal associations with multisite chronic pain (OR=2.20) and gastroesophageal reflux disease (OR=1.31), alongside high genetic correlations with multiple gastrointestinal disorders including inflammatory bowel disease, coeliac disease, and dyspepsia. Lifestyle and environmental factors showed significant genetic correlations with IBS, including smoking, alcohol consumption, sleep disturbances, dietary patterns, and childhood maltreatment, while notably, endometriosis exhibited high genetic correlation with IBS. These findings underscore IBS as a complex disorder with interconnected psychological, physical, and environmental contributions, suggesting that interventions addressing mental health, pain management, and modifiable lifestyle factors may improve outcomes.

title: The Phenotype of Recovery XII: A Reinforcer Pathology Perspective on Associations between Delay Discounting and Pain Catastrophizing in Substance Use Disorder Recovery
journal: Journal of substance use and addiction treatment
summary: This study examines how delay discounting and pain catastrophizing interact to affect quality of life and sustained remission in individuals with substance use disorder and comorbid chronic pain. The research demonstrates that individuals with greater delay discounting (a tendency to devalue future rewards) exhibit elevated pain catastrophizing and experience worse outcomes across all quality-of-life domains—physical, psychological, social, and environmental. Pain catastrophizing serves as a critical mediator in the relationship between delay discounting and reduced quality of life across all four domains, suggesting it is a key mechanistic pathway linking impulsive decision-making to poorer health outcomes. Both delay discounting and pain catastrophizing are independently associated with reduced sustained remission, increased pain severity, greater pain interference, and heightened anxiety, depression, and suicidality. The findings underscore that pain-related cognitive processes, particularly catastrophic thinking patterns, amplify the negative effects of impulsive time-preference behaviors on recovery outcomes. These results suggest that interventions targeting pain catastrophizing may be particularly effective in improving treatment response and quality of life for individuals recovering from substance use disorders with concurrent chronic pain.

title: Clinical indications associated with new opioid use for pain management in the United Kingdom: using national primary care data
summary:
# Summary

This study examined clinical indications for new opioid use in United Kingdom primary care, revealing that musculoskeletal conditions dominate opioid initiation, with osteoarthritis (60.7%) and low back pain (41.0%) being the most frequent diagnoses. Musculoskeletal conditions were present in 80.8% of patients, followed by respiratory conditions (57.6%), infections (30.4%), and trauma/injury (20.4%), while less common indications included gynecological conditions such as endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and fibroids (1.7% collectively). Notably, codeine accounted for 77.4% of new opioid prescriptions, with most patients receiving low-dose formulations (<50 MME/day), despite evidence indicating that nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs are more effective than opioids for chronic musculoskeletal pain. The cohort was predominantly female (61%), White or Caucasian (87%), and socioeconomically deprived, with significant overlaps between clinical systems indicating that many patients had multiple concurrent conditions contributing to opioid initiation. These findings highlight the widespread use of opioids for conditions where they offer limited clinical advantage and underscore the need for evidence-based prescribing practices in primary care.

title: Management of Fibromyalgia: Novel Nutraceutical Therapies Beyond Traditional Pharmaceuticals
summary: Fibromyalgia is characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, chronic fatigue, and sleep disturbances that stem from central sensitization and abnormal neuroendocrine function affecting pain signaling pathways in the brain and spinal cord. The underlying pathophysiology involves elevated glutamate and reduced gamma-aminobutyric acid levels in key brain regions, elevated biomarkers including miRNA-320a and S100 proteins, chronic oxidative stress, and dysregulation of monoaminergic systems—all contributing to amplified pain perception. Traditional pharmaceutical approaches include pregabalin and duloxetine for pain relief, amitriptyline for sleep disturbances, tramadol for opioid receptor activation, and naltrexone for reducing microglia-mediated central inflammation and improving pain thresholds. NSAIDs address peripheral sensory hypersensitivity through prostaglandin inhibition, while myofascial release offers manual therapeutic benefit. Beyond conventional medications, the article emphasizes emerging nutraceutical interventions, particularly polyphenols, which show promise in managing both neuropathic pain and fibromyalgia symptoms through mechanisms that likely reduce oxidative stress and neuroinflammation.

title: Towards a better definition of nociplastic pain conditions: a psychological grounded study on fibromyalgia, chronic headache and vulvodynia
summary: This study defines nociplastic pain (NP) as a distinct pain classification encompassing fibromyalgia, chronic headache, and vulvodynia, which share common central nervous system mechanisms and are frequently accompanied by fatigue, cognitive deficits, hypersensitivity to environmental stimuli, anxiety, and depression. The research identifies central sensitization as the underlying neurophysiological mechanism driving the transition from acute to chronic pain and the amplification of pain signals across these conditions. Psychological factors—particularly early traumatic experiences, anxiety, depression, and alexithymia—are strongly associated with all three nociplastic pain syndromes, with trauma contributing to altered pain perception and stress responses. Notably, individuals with nociplastic pain conditions demonstrate increased reliance on immature defense mechanisms such as dissociation and alexithymia, which interfere with emotional self-regulation and promote somatization of emotions, thereby exacerbating pain severity. Patients with mixed nociplastic conditions and fibromyalgia exhibit the highest levels of traumatic exposure, emotional neglect, bodily threat perception, and difficulty identifying feelings compared to healthy controls, suggesting a dose-response relationship between psychological trauma and pain manifestation. The findings emphasize that nociplastic pain conditions represent a psychophysiological integration of altered central sensitization and maladaptive psychological responses to trauma.

title: Comorbid bladder pain syndrome and vulvodynia - a cross-sectional analysis of the UNICORN-4 study
summary: This cross-sectional analysis of the UNICORN-4 study examined comorbidity patterns in bladder pain syndrome/interstitial cystitis (BPS/IC) and vulvodynia, identifying three distinct patient clusters with different symptom profiles and psychological burdens. BPS/IC was strongly associated with vulvodynia, depression, anxiety, alexithymia, and sexual dysfunction, alongside characteristic urinary symptoms of pelvic pain, urgency, and frequency. Cluster analysis revealed heterogeneous presentations: Cluster 1 showed moderate bladder symptoms with psychological distress, Cluster 2 exhibited severe bladder symptoms with the highest psychological distress, and Cluster 3 represented a vulvodynia-predominant subtype with severe vulvodynia but minimal bladder involvement. Sexual function was significantly impaired across all clusters, with Cluster 3 experiencing the most severe dysfunction, and psychological distress was prominent in both Cluster 2 and Cluster 3. The study also documented higher rates of irritable bowel syndrome and migraine in BPS/IC patients compared to controls, suggesting broader pain syndrome comorbidities. These findings underscore the complex, multifaceted nature of BPS/IC with important implications for stratified treatment approaches addressing both physical symptoms and psychological comorbidities.

title: Risk factors for developing irritable bowel syndrome: systematic umbrella review of reviews
summary: This systematic umbrella review identifies multiple overlapping risk factors for irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) across biological, psychological, and environmental domains. Female gender and anxiety disorders emerge as the most consistently reported high-evidence risk factors, while depression and gastroenteritis represent additional high-evidence associations. Beyond these primary factors, IBS development involves genetic, immunological, and psychological mechanisms, with growing recognition of gut microbiota alterations and gut-brain axis dysfunction as mediators of disease. Environmental and lifestyle factors—including Western diet consumption, high ultra-processed food intake, smoking, stress, and work-related strain—alongside trauma history and childhood adversity significantly elevate IBS risk. The condition demonstrates extensive comorbidity with other pain disorders (fibromyalgia, temporomandibular joint disorder), sleep disturbances, metabolic conditions (diabetes), and gastrointestinal disorders (GERD, small intestinal bacterial overgrowth), suggesting shared pathophysiological pathways. IBS substantially impairs quality of life and generates considerable workplace burden through absenteeism, underscoring the clinical significance of identifying modifiable risk factors for prevention and early intervention.

title: Unravelling pain in diabetic neuropathy patients: Exploring the relationship between perceived pain severity, lifestyle, and coping strategies mediated by self-focused attention and rumination: A cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary: Pain Severity in Diabetic Neuropathy—The Role of Psychological Mediation

Diabetes causes neuropathy, which produces intense pain and related symptoms affecting the majority of diabetic patients. This cross-sectional study reveals that pain severity in diabetic neuropathy is determined not only by direct factors like lifestyle and coping strategies, but critically by psychological mediators including self-focused attention and rumination. Both self-focused attention and rumination act as key mediating pathways that amplify pain perception: improved lifestyle and positive coping strategies reduce these maladaptive psychological processes, which in turn decreases reported pain severity. Notably, self-focused attention and rumination each positively correlate with pain severity, while they are simultaneously reduced by better lifestyle habits and effective coping strategies. The findings suggest that pain management in diabetic neuropathy should target these psychological mediators—reducing self-directed attention and rumination through behavioral interventions—as complementary approaches to lifestyle modification and direct pain management strategies.

title: Exploration of the relationship between primary dysmenorrhea, pain perception, and menstruation-related quality of life in young women: a cross-sectional observational study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined how primary dysmenorrhea (menstrual pain) relates to pain perception and quality of life in young women, revealing a complex interplay of biological and psychological mechanisms. Primary dysmenorrhea involves elevated prostaglandins causing increased uterine contractility and nociceptor activation, producing characteristic symptoms including abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fatigue, and lower back pain; NSAIDs effectively treat these symptoms by reducing prostaglandin levels. The research demonstrates that women with primary dysmenorrhea exhibit significantly higher pain catastrophizing—exaggerated negative thinking about pain—which strongly correlates with pain intensity and accounts for 42.8% of the variance in menstruation-related quality of life. Chronic menstrual pain associates with mood disorders, sleep disturbances, and central sensitization, creating a bidirectional relationship where pain amplifies catastrophizing while catastrophizing worsens pain perception through maladaptive coping strategies. The findings underscore that primary dysmenorrhea is multifactorial, involving psychological, social, and cultural factors alongside physiological mechanisms like prostaglandin elevation and inadequate uterine perfusion, suggesting that comprehensive treatment addressing both pain intensity and pain-related cognitions is essential for improving quality of life.

title: Exploratory Ultrasound Analysis of the Diaphragm and Respiratory Capacity in Women with Primary Dysmenorrhea: A Cross-Sectional Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

Primary dysmenorrhea, the most common gynecological condition causing menstrual pain, is mechanistically linked to increased prostaglandin secretion and decreased estrogen during the menstrual cycle, which together trigger dysfunctional uterine contractility. This chronic pain condition is associated with significant alterations in core musculature, including reduced thickness of the rectus abdominis and external oblique muscles, diminished muscle endurance, and altered muscle activation patterns, likely due to pain-induced physical inactivity and muscle atrophy. This study's ultrasound analysis revealed that women with primary dysmenorrhea exhibited reduced left intercostal distance both at rest and during contraction, suggesting compensatory changes in respiratory mechanics, though diaphragmatic thickness and global respiratory capacity remained unchanged. The altered intercostal spacing may contribute to shallow breathing patterns and intercostal muscle dysfunction, perpetuating a cycle where sympathetic nervous system activation during chronic pain induces hyperventilation and heightened stress responses. Psychological factors such as stress further exacerbate pain perception, while the diaphragm's critical role in regulating intra-abdominal pressure and coordinating with the broader musculoskeletal system indicates that respiratory dysfunction may compound the functional limitations observed in primary dysmenorrhea.

title: Coping in youth living with chronic pain: A systematic review of qualitative evidence
summary: This systematic review of qualitative evidence demonstrates that chronic pain in youth produces substantial negative effects across multiple domains, including functional impairment, mental health complications (anxiety, depression, loneliness), reduced quality of life, diminished self-esteem, and increased school absenteeism, with particular risk for substance use disorders. Chronic pain disproportionately affects girls, older youth, those from lower socioeconomic backgrounds, and racial minorities. Importantly, youth who implement effective coping strategies—encompassing both pharmacological approaches (medications) and behavioral interventions (exercise, relaxation, meditation, yoga, mindfulness, distraction, and deep breathing exercises)—alongside psychosocial support (family and peer support) demonstrate significantly improved quality of life and better pain management. The effectiveness of coping strategies correlates directly with pain severity levels, suggesting that tailored, multimodal interventions may be more efficacious in youth with lower pain burdens. Additionally, diagnostic uncertainty and undiagnosed pain conditions create substantial psychological stress for affected youth and their families. These findings underscore the critical need for integrated pain management approaches that combine physical and psychological interventions while addressing the disparities in chronic pain prevalence among vulnerable youth populations.

title: The number of central nervous system-driven symptoms predicts subsequent chronic primary pain: evidence from UK Biobank
summary: This UK Biobank study demonstrates that central nervous system-driven symptoms, particularly sleep impairment, mood disturbance, and cognitive dysfunction (the SPACE cluster), are significant predictors of subsequent chronic primary pain development. Individuals with two or more baseline CNS symptoms had a 35% greater risk of developing primary pain conditions during an 8-year follow-up period, with a clear dose-response relationship observed. The findings reveal that CNS-driven symptoms are associated not only with future primary pain diagnosis but also with increased severity and chronicity of nociplastic pain, characterized by widespread pain and altered nociception despite apparent normal tissue integrity. Sleep disturbance emerged as a particularly strong associated symptom, along with mood and cognitive dysfunction, suggesting these central features commonly accompany primary pain conditions like fibromyalgia and low back pain. The study employed validated assessment tools including the Widespread Pain Index and revised Fibromyalgia Survey Criteria to measure nociplastic pain severity and classify pain phenotypes. These findings support the hypothesis that CNS dysregulation preceding pain onset may represent a shared pathophysiological pathway in primary pain development and underscore the clinical importance of early identification and management of sleep, mood, and cognitive symptoms to potentially mitigate chronic pain risk.

title: Innovations in acute and chronic pain biomarkers: enhancing diagnosis and personalized therapy
summary:
# Summary

This article reviews innovations in chronic pain biomarkers to advance personalized diagnosis and treatment. The lack of reliable biomarkers represents a major challenge, yet emerging evidence demonstrates that stratification and selection biomarkers significantly improve clinical trial design and therapeutic outcomes—for example, target engagement biomarkers increased the probability of advancing pharmaceutical projects to Phase II by 25 percent. Validated neuroimaging approaches including fMRI, PET, and structural MRI, alongside electrophysiological techniques (EEG, MEG) and quantitative sensory testing, reveal both structural and functional abnormalities in pain pathways while elucidating how psychological factors like anxiety, fear, catastrophizing, and depression modulate pain processing. Machine learning and artificial intelligence combined with high-dimensional omic-based and blood-based biomarkers show promise for predicting individual treatment responses and understanding underlying pathophysiological mechanisms. Integration of these objective biomarkers with psychosocial factors within a biopsychosocial framework represents the most effective approach to revolutionizing chronic pain management and enabling truly personalized therapeutic strategies.

title: Chronic postsurgical pain and transitional pain services: a narrative review highlighting European perspectives
summary: Chronic postsurgical pain (CPSP) represents a significant clinical problem affecting 5–85% of surgical patients and extending beyond immediate discomfort to cause physical disability, psychological distress, social isolation, and sleep disturbances. Multiple pathophysiological mechanisms contribute to CPSP development, including neuropathic pain from surgical nerve injury, central sensitization leading to hyperalgesia and allodynia, and opioid-induced neuroinflammation that paradoxically worsens pain outcomes. Risk factors include perioperative anxiety, depression, inadequate acute pain control, severe postoperative pain intensity within 30 days, pre-existing chronic pain conditions, and procedures with inherently high CPSP rates such as thoracotomy, mastectomy, and spine surgery. Multimodal analgesia combining NSAIDs, COX2 inhibitors, ketamine, gabapentinoids, and regional anesthesia techniques like epidural anesthesia and paravertebral blocks effectively reduces both acute pain and opioid consumption while preventing central sensitization. Transitional pain services employing comprehensive multidisciplinary approaches have demonstrated effectiveness in reducing CPSP incidence and opioid dependence, while emerging neuromodulation techniques including cryoneurolysis and peripheral nerve stimulation offer additional therapeutic options. Early identification of non-optimal acute pain trajectories and implementation of preventive strategies during the perioperative period are critical for mitigating the transition from acute to chronic postsurgical pain.

title: A pilot longitudinal study of decrease in cognitive functions during the most painful day of the period among women with primary dysmenorrhea
summary: This pilot longitudinal study demonstrates that primary dysmenorrhea, a common menstrual pain condition associated with prostaglandin dysregulation, significantly impairs multiple cognitive domains during the most painful day of menstruation. Women with dysmenorrhea showed substantial reductions in inhibitory control, attention, processing speed, concentration, cognitive control, reaction time, and task-switching ability compared to pain-free days, with measurable deficits in Word Fluency, Go/No Go, and Backwards Counting tests. The pain-induced cognitive impairment appears to result from increased attentional processing load, whereby menstrual pain demands greater cognitive resources for pain management, leaving fewer resources available for executive function. Notably, menstrual pain did not impair working or episodic verbal memory, suggesting selective vulnerability of attention-dependent processes. Beyond cognitive effects, primary dysmenorrhea was strongly associated with comorbid anxiety, depression, and stress, collectively contributing to reduced quality of life in affected women. These findings provide evidence that dysmenorrhea-related pain exerts temporary but measurable cognitive consequences during peak symptom days, highlighting an underrecognized dimension of menstrual pain's impact on daily functioning.

title: A mixed method study of menstrual health in Spain: pain, disorders, and the journey for health
summary:
# Menstrual Health and Dysmenorrhea in Spain: Pain, Disorders, and Clinical Recognition

This Spanish mixed-methods study reveals that dysmenorrhea, affecting up to 95% of menstruating individuals, causes debilitating multisystem symptoms including pain (84.4% of participants), abdominal swelling (74.5%), diarrhea (51.4%), and heavy bleeding (43.2%), with prevalence increasing significantly in younger birth cohorts. Primary dysmenorrhea results from prostaglandin-induced uterine contractions, while secondary dysmenorrhea arises from underlying gynecological conditions, particularly endometriosis and adenomyosis, which are characterized by aberrant endometrial tissue presence and independently cause chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and heightened pain sensitivity. The study documents substantial diagnostic and healthcare access barriers, including delayed diagnosis of endometriosis and adenomyosis when primary dysmenorrhea symptoms are dismissed, along with reduced quality of life manifesting as school and work absenteeism. Additionally, dysmenorrhea correlates strongly with other chronic pain syndromes such as migraine, fibromyalgia, and irritable bowel syndrome, while systemic gender bias in medical settings results in women's menstrual pain being systematically under-recognized and undertreated compared to men's pain, exacerbating disease progression and functional impairment.

title: Chronic Pain and Mood Disorders Are Not Barriers to Symptom Improvement Under Collaborative Co-Managed Care (C3)
summary:
# Summary

This study examined patients with gastrointestinal disease managed under collaborative co-managed care (C3), focusing on whether comorbid chronic pain and mood disorders impede therapeutic response. Patients presenting with chronic overlapping pain conditions and mood disorders exhibited significantly more severe gastrointestinal disease and greater healthcare utilization at baseline compared to those without these comorbidities. However, the presence of chronic pain conditions or mood disorders did not substantially prevent symptom improvement or quality-of-life gains under the C3 treatment model. Notably, patients with chronic constipation achieved statistically significant symptom relief more frequently than other patient subgroups. These findings suggest that integrated, collaborative care approaches can effectively improve gastrointestinal outcomes regardless of comorbid pain and psychiatric conditions, challenging the notion that these comorbidities serve as barriers to treatment success.

title: Cognitive Dysfunction in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome—Aetiology and Potential Treatments
summary: Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) is characterized by cognitive dysfunction—including impaired memory, concentration, and attention affecting occupational and social functioning—driven by low-grade persistent inflammation and immune dysregulation. Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha) and C-reactive protein associate with cognitive deficits and sleep disturbance, while peripheral inflammation promotes microglial activation and blood-brain barrier dysfunction, leading to altered regional cerebral blood flow and neuronal dysfunction. Structural and functional neuroimaging reveals reduced cerebral blood flow, brainstem hypoperfusion, grey matter reduction, elevated brain temperature in cortical regions, and altered metabolite ratios (increased lactate, choline, and myo-inositol), with positron emission tomography demonstrating correlation between cognitive impairment and translocator protein signals indicating neuroinflammation. Brainstem dysfunction, including impaired ascending reticular activating system activity and thalamic ischemia, disrupts attention and memory networks, while comorbid pain, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances further tax cognitive domains through attentional mechanisms. Viral infections, particularly Epstein-Barr virus and human herpesvirus 6, are associated with ME/CFS onset and cognitive sequelae, suggesting infection-triggered immune dysregulation and neuroinflammation as key pathogenic mechanisms underlying cognitive dysfunction in this debilitating condition.

title: Sociodemographically Differential Patterns of Chronic Pain Progression Revealed by Analyzing the All of Us Research Program Data
summary: This analysis of All of Us Research Program data reveals significant sociodemographic disparities in chronic pain progression and comorbidity patterns. Females demonstrate substantially elevated odds of developing fibromyalgia (9.99-fold), migraine (3.31-fold), anxiety, and depression compared to males, while Black or African Americans show higher prevalence of chronic low back pain and schizophrenia. The study identifies complex sequential disease pathways where chronic pain conditions and mental health disorders trigger subsequent conditions: chronic low back pain leads to anxiety, depression, migraine, and irritable bowel syndrome, while depression and anxiety reciprocally increase the risk of developing back pain. These associations are amplified in individuals with low household income, who experience 1.5- to 2-fold increased odds of developing chronic overlapping pain conditions and migraine following initial pain diagnoses. The findings suggest that biological mechanisms including sex hormones, central sensitization, and inflammatory responses interact with psychosocial factors and healthcare access barriers to create differential pain progression patterns across demographic groups.

title: Menstrual‐related symptoms as red flags for school absenteeism among Norwegian adolescents (MINA): A cross‐sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study of Norwegian adolescents identifies severe dysmenorrhea as a significant driver of both academic and social absenteeism, with 85% of students with severe dysmenorrhea reporting school absences compared to substantially lower rates in those with milder symptoms. Beyond primary dysmenorrhea, several associated menstrual symptoms—including menorrhagia, dyschezia, vomiting, and fatigue—independently predict academic absenteeism, with vomiting showing the strongest effect. Severe dysmenorrhea frequently clusters with multiple systemic complaints including headaches, syncope, dysuria, polyuria, and urinary incontinence, suggesting a complex symptom constellation affecting adolescent functioning. The study reveals critical disparities in burden, as adolescents with non-Norwegian ethnicity report significantly higher rates of dysmenorrhea-related absenteeism, yet only one-third of students with severe dysmenorrhea use evidence-based hormonal contraception despite its proven efficacy. While 83% of participants use over-the-counter analgesics like NSAIDs and paracetamol for symptom management, the widespread absenteeism underscores that current self-medication strategies are insufficient and highlights the need for better screening and targeted interventions to support affected adolescents.

title: Relationship Between BMI, Self-Rated Depression, and Food Addiction—A Cross-Sectional Study of Adults in Postpandemic Poland
summary: This cross-sectional study of Polish adults reveals complex bidirectional relationships among obesity, depression, and food addiction (FA) in the postpandemic period. Higher body mass index and increased depression severity were both significantly correlated with greater FA severity, with FA showing particularly elevated prevalence in individuals with obesity (25-32%) and eating disorders (70%). The neurobiological mechanism underlying these associations involves impaired reward processing in depression, which can increase susceptibility to food addiction, compounded by reduced dopamine D2 receptor expression in obesity. Ultra-processed foods high in refined carbohydrates and added fats contribute to food addiction, while negative emotions and emotional distress during periods of stress and social isolation drive increased consumption of palatable foods. Notably, while BMI and depression each independently associated with food addiction, direct correlation between BMI and depression severity was not statistically significant, suggesting food addiction may mediate the relationship between these conditions. These findings underscore food addiction as a central comorbid condition linking obesity, depression, and eating disorders in the postpandemic population, with implications for integrated treatment approaches addressing behavioral, emotional, and neurochemical factors.

title: Psychiatric morbidity in people with autoimmune arthritides as a model of inflammatory mechanisms in mental disorders
summary: This study demonstrates that rheumatoid arthritis (RA) and axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) show robust associations with multiple psychiatric conditions, particularly mood and anxiety disorders and behavioral syndromes, across all age groups. The associations with depression appear particularly strong and consistent, with an age-dependent gradient suggesting earlier onset of psychiatric symptoms in individuals with inflammatory arthritis. Notably, both conditions show inverse associations with psychotic disorders including schizophrenia and Alzheimer's disease, potentially reflecting protective effects of antipsychotic medications or shared immune mechanisms. The inflammatory basis of these psychiatric comorbidities involves dysregulation of the interleukin-6 pathway and elevated inflammatory markers such as C-reactive protein that characterize both rheumatoid arthritis and depression. While inflammatory cytokines are implicated in the pathophysiology of RA, their role as direct mediators between RA and depression remains uncertain, suggesting additional biological mechanisms beyond classical inflammatory pathways. Overall, autoimmune arthritides serve as a clinical model demonstrating how chronic inflammatory processes contribute to psychiatric morbidity through shared immunological alterations.

title: Associations of dietary and plasma lutein + zeaxanthin with depression in US adults: findings from NHANES
journal: Asia Pacific Journal of Clinical Nutrition
summary:
# Summary

This NHANES-based study examined associations between lutein and zeaxanthin (L+Z), carotenoid antioxidants found in leafy greens and other foods, and depression risk in US adults. Both dietary intake and plasma levels of L+Z showed significant inverse associations with depression, with individuals in the highest quartile of intake or plasma concentration demonstrating substantially reduced depression risk compared to the lowest quartile. The findings suggest that adequate L+Z consumption may serve a protective role against depression, potentially through the antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties of these compounds. Higher plasma L+Z levels were particularly associated with lower depression symptomatology, indicating that systemic bioavailability of these carotenoids may be clinically relevant. These results support dietary strategies emphasizing L+Z-rich foods as a potential complementary approach to managing depression risk in the general population. The work highlights the growing evidence linking nutritional status of specific micronutrients to mental health outcomes.

title: Suicide by Firearm and Hormonal Imbalances: A Forensic Case Report With Literature Review
summary: This forensic case report examines the interconnected relationships between uterine fibroids, hormonal imbalances, chronic pain, and psychiatric disorders, with particular focus on their collective contribution to suicide risk. Uterine fibroids develop through sex steroid hormone dysregulation and are associated with multiple physical symptoms including abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and infertility, which collectively contribute to chronic pain and psychological distress. Women with symptomatic fibroids experience significantly elevated rates of depression and anxiety, a risk further amplified in those experiencing concurrent pain and heavy menstrual bleeding, with additional worsening observed following hysterectomy. The biological mechanisms linking these conditions involve hormonal dysregulation affecting the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal and hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axes, alterations in monoamine neurotransmitters, and changes in neuroplasticity through brain-derived neurotrophic factors and the glutamate system. Genetic predisposition to major depressive disorder increases fibroid risk, while oral contraceptive use may further influence mood disturbance, creating a bidirectional pathophysiological relationship between gynecological disease and mental health. The cumulative burden of hormonal imbalance, chronic pain, psychiatric comorbidity, and environmental stressors such as workplace stress substantially elevates suicide risk in affected women.

title: Genome-wide association study of dry eye disease reveals shared heritability with systemic comorbidities
summary: This genome-wide association study identifies substantial shared genetic architecture between dry eye disease (DED) and multiple systemic comorbidities, revealing a complex pleiotropy network. The analysis discovered ten independent loci across nine susceptibility regions, including genes involved in synaptic maintenance (EPHA5, GRIA1, SYNGAP1) and autoimmunity (BLK), with notably consistent effects across diverse ancestries. DED demonstrated particularly strong genetic correlations with fibromyalgia (rG = 0.69), multi-site chronic pain (rG = 0.64), and post-traumatic stress disorder (rG = 0.58), alongside moderate associations with other pain conditions, gastrointestinal disorders, and psychiatric traits. A shared susceptibility locus near BLK showed pleiotropic effects across DED and multiple autoimmune conditions including Sjögren's disease, systemic sclerosis, and rheumatoid arthritis, suggesting common immune dysregulation mechanisms. The findings indicate that DED should be conceptualized within a broader framework of chronic pain and comorbid conditions, with genetic factors contributing to both ocular manifestations and systemic health outcomes.

title: Postpartum Hormonal Contraceptive Use and Risk of Depression
summary:
# Summary

This large cohort study of over 610,000 first-time mothers found that hormonal contraceptive initiation in the postpartum period significantly increases the risk of depression, with an adjusted hazard ratio of 1.49 overall. Among specific contraceptive types, combined nonoral contraceptives showed the highest risk (1.97), followed by combined oral contraceptives (1.72) and progestogen-only nonoral contraceptives (1.40), while progestogen-only pills showed no increased risk. The depression risk was highest when combined oral contraceptives were initiated earliest postpartum and gradually decreased over the first year, suggesting that the abrupt hormonal changes in the early postpartum period may be particularly vulnerable to adverse mood effects when combined with exogenous hormones. Notably, women without a prior mental disorder history showed higher depression risk from hormonal contraceptive exposure (HR 1.63) compared to those with prior mental illness (HR 1.32), and younger mothers experienced greater risk than older mothers. These findings suggest that the timing and type of postpartum hormonal contraceptive initiation should be carefully considered in clinical practice, particularly in the early postpartum period and in women without psychiatric history.

title: The pain of PTSD: integrating persistent or chronic pain within emotional processing theory of posttraumatic stress disorder
summary:
# Summary

This paper integrates chronic pain within Emotional Processing Theory to explain the high comorbidity and severity of posttraumatic stress disorder co-occurring with chronic pain (PTSD-Pain). The authors propose that trauma creates a "trauma structure" containing maladaptive associations, and when patients avoid processing these memories, avoidance simultaneously prevents extinction learning and reinforces pain-related threat beliefs through central sensitization—a neurobiological mechanism underlying conditions like fibromyalgia, endometriosis, and chronic migraine. PTSD hyperarousal symptoms and pain catastrophizing amplify this cycle, leading to greater opioid use, disability, and suicidal behavior compared to either condition alone. Prolonged Exposure therapy, which targets the underlying trauma structure through corrective emotional processing, is proposed as an effective treatment that can be integrated with cognitive-behavioral pain interventions to address both conditions simultaneously. The neurobiological mechanisms linking PTSD and pain—involving neurohormones, neurotransmitters, and inflammatory factors—suggest that treating the emotional/trauma component may enhance pain modulation and treatment efficacy.

title: A Survey on the Use of Cannabidiol (CBD) Isolate, Its Perceived Benefits, and Associated Side Effects Among Subjects With Chronic Pain
summary: Cannabidiol (CBD) demonstrates efficacy as a pain management treatment by reducing chronic pain symptoms through multiple mechanisms of action, including inhibition of cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase enzymes, modulation of opioid receptors, activation of serotonin 5-HT1A receptors, and interaction with the endocannabinoid system. Survey data shows that CBD treatment significantly decreased average pain scores from 5.4 to 2.6, with over half of respondents achieving relief at doses of 100 mg or less, across various chronic pain conditions including arthritis, neuropathy, fibromyalgia, and disk herniation. CBD represents a promising alternative to conventional opioid-based pain management, particularly given the substantial overdose mortality associated with opioid use, while producing minimal side effects in most users. The most commonly reported adverse effects were mild, including drowsiness and fatigue in approximately 30% of those experiencing symptoms, with severe side effects absent from the survey data. This evidence supports CBD isolate as a potentially safer and effective therapeutic option for chronic pain management while maintaining quality of life and work productivity.

title: Analysis of dysmenorrhea-related factors in adenomyosis and development of a risk prediction model
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, an estrogen-dependent disease characterized by endometrial invasion into the myometrium, is strongly associated with dysmenorrhea, affecting over 61% of adenomyosis patients in this cohort, along with related symptoms including pelvic pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, and infertility. The underlying mechanism involves endometrial invasion triggering improper uterine contractions, excessive prostaglandin and estrogen secretion, increased tissue pressure, local ischemia, and inflammatory responses that collectively produce dysmenorrhea. Multiple risk factors for dysmenorrhea in adenomyosis patients were identified, including menorrhagia, pelvic inflammatory disease, family history of adenomyosis, increased parity, older age at disease onset, infrequent menstruation, and overwork during menstruation, while protective factors included regular exercise and longer menstrual cycles. Beyond the physical symptoms, dysmenorrhea leads to significant psychological comorbidities including depression and anxiety, which conversely exacerbate endometriosis-related disease progression, and increases women's overall pain sensitivity and susceptibility to other chronic pain conditions. The study developed a predictive model demonstrating that adenomyosis dysmenorrhea is a multifactorial condition requiring consideration of clinical, demographic, behavioral, and inflammatory parameters for comprehensive patient management and risk stratification.

title: Melatonin and sleep parameters in infertile women with endometriosis: first results from the triple-blind randomized controlled trial of administration of melatonin in chronic pelvic pain and sleep disturbance
summary:
# Summary

This triple-blind randomized controlled trial demonstrates that endometriosis creates a detrimental cycle linking chronic pelvic pain, sleep disturbances, and reduced quality of life in infertile women. Endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain impairs sleep quality, which in turn exacerbates pain sensitivity, fatigue, anxiety, and depression through a mutual reinforcing cycle. Melatonin supplementation significantly improved multiple sleep parameters in 80 infertile women with endometriosis, including increased sleep duration, decreased sleep latency, and reduced overall sleep disturbances with large effect sizes. Beyond sleep improvements, melatonin substantially reduced chronic pelvic pain and decreased reliance on sleep medications, effects attributed to its antinociceptive, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties, as well as its ability to reduce brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), a biomarker elevated in endometriosis. These findings suggest melatonin addresses multiple pathological aspects of endometriosis simultaneously by regulating circadian rhythms and endometrial epithelial cell function while interrupting the pain-sleep deprivation cycle. The results indicate melatonin may offer a promising therapeutic approach for managing both sleep dysfunction and pain in endometriosis, though further investigation is warranted to establish optimal clinical protocols.

title: Dyspareunia and pelvic pain in women with chronic migraine: A retrospective, observational analysis
summary: This retrospective observational study demonstrates that chronic migraine is significantly associated with multiple pelvic pain conditions, including dyspareunia, pelvic pain, interstitial cystitis, vaginismus, and vulvodynia, with women with chronic migraine showing substantially elevated odds ratios for these conditions compared to controls. The association appears to extend beyond simple comorbidity, with evidence suggesting a direct effect of migraine on sexual dysfunction and dyspareunia. A unifying mechanism linking these conditions is central sensitization, a neurobiological process implicated in both chronic migraine and dyspareunia, with endometriosis and related pelvic pain conditions demonstrating increased pain sensitivity across multiple body regions. The study identifies that dyspareunia and migraine share common comorbidities including depression, anxiety, irritable bowel syndrome, fibromyalgia, and post-traumatic stress disorder, which independently increase the risk of dyspareunia in women with migraine. Notably, while selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors adversely affect sexual function, dyspareunia and chronic migraine arising from central sensitization respond to tricyclic antidepressants, acupuncture, cognitive behavioral therapy, and mindfulness-based interventions. These findings suggest that dyspareunia and pelvic pain in women with chronic migraine represent interconnected conditions warranting integrated clinical management strategies.

title: The association between endometriosis and migraine: a systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: This meta-analysis of 13 observational studies demonstrates a significant association between endometriosis and migraine, with individuals with endometriosis having increased migraine risk compared to controls. The association is particularly strong for migraine without aura (OR 2.64) and is dose-dependent, with moderate-to-severe endometriosis and specific subtypes such as ovarian endometriomas (OR 2.78) and deep infiltrating endometriosis (OR 2.51) showing elevated migraine risk, whereas minimal-mild and superficial peritoneal forms show weaker associations. The proposed mechanisms involve shared hormonal pathways, as estrogen and progesterone regulate neurotransmitter systems critical for migraine pathogenesis, and women with migraine experience more rapid estrogen decline during the menstrual cycle, a known migraine trigger. Both conditions share genetic overlap and are linked to similar comorbidities including autoimmune traits, fibromyalgia, and chronic fatigue syndrome, suggesting underlying biological commonalities beyond hormonal influences. The central role of pain in both conditions, combined with the established role of estrogen in promoting ectopic endometrial growth, indicates that estrogen dysregulation may be a key mechanism connecting these two prevalent conditions affecting women of reproductive age.

title: The association of pain phenotype and providing caregiving with depressive symptom trajectory for older adults: a longitudinal analysis using the health and retirement study
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal study using the Health and Retirement Study identified distinct chronic pain phenotypes in older adults and their differential associations with depression trajectories. Chronic pain leads to depression through multiple pathways, with pain intensity, interference, persistence, and multisite involvement all strongly predicting depressive symptoms. The severe-persistent pain group, characterized by high activity-interfering pain and predominantly affecting females, Hispanic individuals, and those with poor self-rated health and functional limitations, showed elevated baseline depression but paradoxically exhibited a slower rate of depression symptom increase over time. Notably, caregiving roles significantly reduced the rate of depression symptom progression in both the severe-persistent and back pain groups, suggesting that engagement in caregiving may provide psychological benefits that buffer against worsening depression despite ongoing pain. The findings indicate that pain phenotype-informed depression interventions and promotion of caregiving benefit-finding may be more effective than pain relief alone in addressing depression in older adults with chronic pain.

title: Association between common mental disorders and the severity of dysmenorrhea among female medical students at the University of Ibadan, Nigeria
summary:
# Summary

This study examined associations between common mental disorders and dysmenorrhea severity in female medical students at the University of Ibadan, Nigeria. The researchers found strong positive associations between anxiety, depression, and stress with increased dysmenorrhea severity, while self-esteem showed a weak negative relationship. Anxiety emerged as the strongest predictor, accounting for approximately 36% of the influence on dysmenorrhea severity, followed by depression. The mechanisms underlying these associations likely involve shared physiological pathways including hormonal fluctuations affecting menstrual cycles and mood, heightened emotional distress leading to increased pain sensitivity, and cognitive distortions that intensify pain perception. Notably, the combined influence of mental disorders on dysmenorrhea was significantly greater among participants reporting unsatisfactory relationships with their parents, suggesting that psychosocial stressors amplify the mental health-pain connection. These findings highlight dysmenorrhea as a complex condition requiring integrated management of both physical symptoms and psychological comorbidities.

title: Youths with Extreme Obesity: A High-Risk Group for Pain and Mental Health Impairments
summary: Youths with extreme obesity represent a high-risk group for concurrent pain and mental health impairments, particularly depression and reduced health-related quality of life. Female youth with extreme obesity experience disproportionately elevated levels of pain, depression, and psychological distress compared to males, with hormonal fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone potentially amplifying pain sensitivity in women. The pain experienced by these youth appears mechanistically linked to joint overloading and inflammation from excess weight, while depression may further amplify pain perception through psychological pathways. A vicious cycle emerges wherein chronic pain reduces physical activity and exercise capacity, limiting weight loss outcomes and potentially perpetuating further weight gain and unhealthy nutritional patterns. Pain-related school and activity absences have the potential to disrupt important developmental milestones in this adolescent population. Regular physical activity shows promise as an intervention to reduce pain severity and frequency, suggesting that addressing pain barriers may be crucial for improving lifestyle interventions and overall outcomes in youth with extreme obesity.

title: Psychological, Relational, and Fertility-Related Characteristics of Italian Women with Vulvodynia: A Comparative Study with Controls
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
summary:
# Summary

This comparative study of Italian women reveals that vulvodynia, characterized by persistent vulvar pain, is associated with a complex constellation of psychological, relational, and reproductive complications. Women with vulvodynia demonstrate significantly higher levels of attachment anxiety and avoidance, reduced resilience, depression, and sexual dysfunction compared to controls, alongside diminished relationship quality and elevated infertility-related distress. The biopsychosocial mechanisms underlying these outcomes involve multifactorial pathways: local inflammation and peripheral/central sensitization contribute to pain chronicity, while chronic stress, fear, and anxiety activate a pelvic stress reflex that increases pelvic floor muscle tension and perpetuates vulvar pain. Attachment insecurity intensifies pain perception and psychological distress, and dyspareunia leads to avoidance of sexual intimacy, creating relational strain, reduced emotional closeness, and increased conflict. These integrated dysfunctions impair reproductive planning and contribute to higher rates of separation and divorce in this population, underscoring the need for comprehensive biopsychosocial interventions addressing pain, emotional regulation, attachment patterns, and relational dynamics.

title: A Correlation Between Migraine and Endometriosis and Its Clinical Implications—A Systematic Literature Review
summary: This systematic review demonstrates a robust bidirectional association between migraine and endometriosis, with individuals with either condition having significantly elevated odds of developing the other—endometriosis patients show approximately 5.4 times greater odds of migraine, while migraine patients experience endometriosis at roughly 2.3 times the rate of healthy controls. Chronic migraine appears most strongly linked to endometriosis, and menstrually-related migraine is particularly prevalent in endometriosis patients, with both conditions disproportionately affecting women. The two disorders share common genetic pathways, particularly involving estrogen receptor genes, suggesting shared biological mechanisms underlying their association. Patients with concurrent endometriosis and migraine experience exacerbated pain symptoms alongside multiple comorbidities including fibromyalgia, widespread nonpelvic pain, and psychological distress (anxiety and depression), indicating a complex central sensitization phenomenon. Additionally, family history of migraine in endometriosis patients correlates with reduced responsiveness to hormonal contraceptive treatment, highlighting important clinical implications for personalized therapeutic strategies in this comorbid population.

title: Non-menstrual pelvic symptoms and women’s quality of life: a cross-sectional observational study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a highly prevalent inflammatory condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women globally, causing pelvic pain and infertility that substantially impair quality of life. Beyond menstrual symptoms, non-menstrual pelvic pain emerges as the most prevalent and clinically significant symptom, demonstrating the strongest associations with worse health-related quality of life, increased anxiety and depression, and impaired mental health across nearly all domains. The presence of non-menstrual acyclic symptoms including deep dyspareunia, dyschezia, and dysuria suggests central sensitization—a neurobiological condition characterized by pain hypersensitivity, allodynia, and hyperalgesia—which may amplify pain perception and further compromise psychological well-being and sexual function. Additional factors including anxiety, depression, poor sleep quality, and associated conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome and fibromyalgia act as potential confounders worsening outcomes. Minimally invasive surgery remains the treatment of choice when medical management proves ineffective, though the multifaceted nature of endometriosis-related pain suggests comprehensive approaches addressing both peripheral and central nervous system sensitization are needed to optimize women's quality of life.

title: ADHD (Attention-Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder) Symptoms Are Associated With Chronic Pain Interference: Results From a Prospective Cohort Study
summary: This prospective cohort study demonstrates that ADHD symptoms are significantly associated with chronic pain interference in young adults, with each additional ADHD symptom increasing the odds of moderate-to-severe pain interference by 52%. The association appears to operate through multiple pathways: ADHD symptoms are linked to pain catastrophizing and heightened selective attention to pain-related stimuli, both of which independently increase pain interference risk. Depression and pain catastrophizing also emerged as independent contributors to moderate-to-severe pain interference. Notably, the study found that baseline pain interference did not predict six-month outcomes, suggesting that psychological factors like ADHD symptoms may be more predictive of long-term pain trajectories than baseline pain severity. The findings have significant clinical implications given that ADHD is already established as a risk factor for prescription opioid misuse and that ADHD is associated with poorer outcomes in cognitive-behavioral therapy for chronic pain. The authors recommend early screening for pain interference in patients with ADHD and suggest that integrated pain management strategies addressing attentional and emotional regulation may improve treatment outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: Somatosensory and prefrontal cortex activity relates to emotional outcomes and hair cortisol concentration in chronic postsurgical pain
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals critical neural mechanisms underlying the emotional and physiological consequences of chronic postsurgical pain. Left somatosensory cortex (SMC) activation shows strong positive associations with both depressive symptoms and anxiety, suggesting that heightened sensory processing in this region—normally involved in pain perception and sensory-discriminative aspects of pain—becomes pathologically linked to emotional dysfunction. Conversely, right lateral prefrontal cortex (PFC) activity demonstrates a negative correlation with hair cortisol concentration, a marker of long-term stress exposure, indicating that reduced prefrontal regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis leads to elevated cortisol levels. The findings support a model wherein maladaptive neural plasticity in somatosensory regions drives emotional comorbidities (depression, anxiety, and perceived stress) while simultaneously impairing prefrontal control over stress hormone secretion. This integrated brain-endocrine dysregulation explains why chronic postsurgical pain patients experience pronounced psychological and physiological stress responses, with implications for understanding similar mechanisms in other chronic pain conditions such as endometriosis.

title: Serious Adverse Drug Reactions to Long-Term Oral Liquid Morphine in Patients with Chronic Pain: A Cross-Sectional Survey in Palliative Care
summary: This cross-sectional survey examined serious adverse drug reactions in chronic pain patients receiving long-term oral liquid morphine in palliative care. While oral liquid morphine effectively treats moderate to severe chronic pain, including cancer pain and sickle cell disease pain, the study identified significant safety concerns associated with prolonged opioid therapy, particularly addiction, opioid-induced hyperalgesia, cognitive disorders, and increased risks of overdose and opioid use disorder. Notably, patients with sickle cell disease appeared especially vulnerable to addiction and comorbid psychiatric conditions including depression and anxiety. The study documented cases where opioid-induced hyperalgesia manifested as widespread pain with allodynia and was managed through multimodal approaches combining pharmacological interventions such as amitriptyline with psychoeducation, relaxation training, and supportive psychotherapy. These findings underscore the complex challenge of balancing opioid therapy's analgesic benefits against serious adverse effects, particularly in vulnerable populations, and highlight the importance of comprehensive psychosocial support alongside pharmacological management in palliative care settings.

title: Neuro-Nutritional Approach to Neuropathic Pain Management: A Critical Review
summary:
# Summary

Neuropathic pain is a chronic condition arising from somatosensory nervous system damage that involves inflammation, oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, and glial cell activation, creating a vicious cycle of pathology.
