The underlying mechanisms include ectopic activity in sensory fibers, dysregulation of inhibitory and excitatory neurotransmitter balance, and the involvement of voltage-gated ion channels and cytokine-mediated neuronal excitability. Multiple etiologies contribute to neuropathic pain, including diabetes mellitus (via hyperglycemic oxidative stress and microvascular hypoxia), chemotherapy-induced neurotoxicity, and viral infections, with additional risk factors encompassing lifestyle elements such as physical inactivity, sleep disturbances, obesity, and poor diet quality. The gut-brain axis plays a critical role, as dysbiosis triggered by imbalanced nutrition and excess refined carbohydrates promotes systemic inflammation that escalates neuroinflammation and nervous system sensitization. Melatonin and other bioactive compounds including polyphenols and vitamins demonstrate therapeutic potential through anti-inflammatory and antioxidant mechanisms, offering a neuro-nutritional approach to pain management. Sex and age differences significantly modulate pain perception and chronicity, indicating the need for personalized treatment strategies that address the complex interplay of metabolic, immune, and neurobiological factors underlying neuropathic pain.

title: Cluster and survival analysis of UK biobank data reveals associations between physical multimorbidity clusters and subsequent depression
summary:
# Key Findings Summary

This UK Biobank study reveals that specific clusters of physical multimorbidity are significantly associated with subsequent depression risk. The strongest associations emerged in "Very extensive morbidity," migraine, and respiratory disease clusters, alongside cardiometabolic conditions like cardiovascular disease combined with diabetes. Risk increased progressively with the number of concurrent physical conditions, and distinct sex-specific patterns were identified, with musculoskeletal disease clusters showing particularly high depression risk in women (hazard ratio 1.81) and musculoskeletal plus other conditions showing elevated risk in men (hazard ratio 2.50). Inflammation was identified as a potential biological mediator linking physical disease to depression. The findings suggest that depression risk in multimorbid patients is not uniform but depends on specific disease combinations, offering insights for targeted clinical intervention and prevention strategies in patients with overlapping physical conditions.

title: Exploring the role of negative expectations and emotions in primary dysmenorrhea: insights from a case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study demonstrates that primary dysmenorrhea involves a substantial cognitive-emotional component alongside well-established physiological mechanisms. Women with high menstrual pain exhibited significantly elevated pain expectations, anticipatory anxiety, stress, and worry in the 10 days preceding menstruation, with expected pain levels closely correlating with actual peak menstrual pain intensity and disability. The research reveals a bidirectional relationship whereby anxiety can amplify menstrual pain through nocebo hyperalgesia mechanisms, while menstrual pain itself amplifies emotional distress, and negative expectations trigger physiological stress responses via hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis activation. Classical prostaglandin-mediated pathways—involving progesterone reduction, increased prostaglandin release, vasoconstriction, myometrial contraction, and resulting ischemia—remain central to dysmenorrhea pathophysiology. The authors propose that cognitive-behavioral and mindfulness-based interventions may complement standard non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug treatment by targeting the expectation-emotion-pain cycle that perpetuates and potentially amplifies menstrual pain experience.

title: Effect of seasonal exposure in aeroallergen-sensitised patients with irritable bowel syndrome-diarrhoea
summary: This study demonstrates that seasonal pollen exposure significantly exacerbates symptoms in irritable bowel syndrome with diarrhea (IBS-D) patients sensitized to grass allergens, with symptom severity increasing during pollination season as measured by gastrointestinal symptom rating scales and visual analog pain scores. The research reveals that pollen allergen sensitization is particularly common in IBS-D patients with atopic comorbidities including allergic rhinitis and eczema, and that grass-sensitized patients show markedly worse lower gastrointestinal tract symptoms during high pollen periods. The proposed mechanism involves mast cell activation in the intestinal mucosa, where seasonal respiratory allergen exposure triggers mast cell degranulation and release of mediators such as histamine and tryptase, which alter gut motility and secretion while contributing to a pro-inflammatory state characterized by eosinophil and T-cell infiltration. The study suggests that respiratory airway inflammation may systemically amplify gut mast cell activity through circulating cytokines including IL-25, IL-33, and TSLP, with potential sex-specific differences in neurogenic responses. These findings suggest that mast cell-targeted therapies such as antihistamines and chromones warrant investigation as potential treatments for IBS-D patients with documented pollen sensitization, particularly during high-allergen seasons.

title: Multidimensional Evaluation of Myofascial Pelvic Pain and Other Comorbidities in Endometriosis Patients
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that nearly 95% of endometriosis patients experience multiple comorbid conditions, with myofascial pelvic pain (MPP) emerging as a particularly significant clinical feature associated with worse health outcomes. Endometriosis patients with MPP exhibit substantially higher burden of pain symptoms, including dysmenorrhea, non-cyclic chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and widespread musculoskeletal pain, alongside increased gastrointestinal disturbances such as abdominal bloating and intestinal dysfunction. Central sensitization, fatigue, insomnia, anxiety, and depression are significantly more prevalent in the MPP subgroup, suggesting shared neurobiological mechanisms involving chronic neuroinflammation and central nervous system sensitization. Through multivariate analysis, severe non-cyclic pelvic pain, abdominal bloating, and elevated central sensitization scores (≥40) emerged as independent predictors of MPP development. The presence of myofascial trigger points may perpetuate pain signaling to the central nervous system, potentially contributing to endometriosis-associated pain independent of central sensitization alone. These findings highlight that addressing myofascial pain as a distinct clinical entity alongside psychological comorbidities is essential for optimizing pain management and quality of life in endometriosis patients.

title: Can EEG‐Neurofeedback Training Enhance Effective Connectivity in People With Chronic Secondary Musculoskeletal Pain? A Secondary Analysis of a Feasibility Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial
summary: Chronic musculoskeletal pain involves dysregulation of cortical neuronal activity and disrupted effective connectivity between pain-processing brain regions, particularly within the infraslow fluctuation (ISF) frequency band. This study evaluated whether ISF electroencephalography-neurofeedback (EEG-NF) training targeting the pregenual anterior cingulate cortex (pgACC) could enhance effective connectivity between key pain-modulatory regions including the pgACC, dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC), and primary somatosensory cortex (SSC). The ISF-NF intervention successfully increased effective connectivity from the pgACC to the dACC and bilateral SSC while simultaneously reducing connectivity from the right SSC to the dACC, thereby strengthening the cortical pain inhibitory pathway and weakening ascending pain signals. These connectivity changes occurred predominantly in the ISF frequency band and were associated with improvements in pain severity, suggesting that modulating infraslow brain oscillations can effectively rebalance communication between somatosensory, affective, and antinociceptive cortical networks. The findings demonstrate that EEG-NF is a viable non-invasive neuromodulatory technique capable of inducing specific, therapeutically relevant changes in brain effective connectivity in individuals with chronic secondary musculoskeletal pain.

title: Professional-Facing Digital Health Technology for the Care of Patients With Chronic Pain: Scoping Review
summary: Chronic pain represents a significant clinical and economic burden associated with serious complications including opioid dependency and substantial healthcare costs. The condition stems from biopsychosocial factors, with negative attitudes and lack of healthcare provider knowledge identified as substantial barriers to effective management. Digital health technologies (DHTs) offer a cost-effective approach to mitigate these challenges through remote patient monitoring, clinical decision support, assessment and diagnosis tools, and healthcare provider education. Optimal chronic pain management requires interdisciplinary rather than merely multidisciplinary care coordination, with primary care settings and multidisciplinary teams serving as common implementation sites. DHTs have demonstrated positive outcomes including reduced pain intensity, improved quality of life, and enhanced coping strategies, tracking patient-reported outcomes such as pain intensity, physical activity, and disease activity. The review identified arthritis as the most commonly targeted condition (34% of technologies), though DHTs have been developed for diverse chronic pain presentations including low back pain, headache, endometriosis, and temporomandibular disorder.

title: Multimorbidity patterns and early signals of diabetes in online communities
summary:
# Summary

This study analyzed online community engagement patterns to identify early warning signals and multimorbidity associations with diabetes. The research revealed that diabetes exhibits extensive comorbidity with both metabolic and mental health conditions, including strong associations with obesity, cardiovascular disease, depression, anxiety, ADHD, chronic pain, and gastrointestinal disorders. Notably, mental health conditions—particularly depression, anxiety, ADHD, and bipolar disorder—emerge as early signals preceding type 2 diabetes diagnosis, alongside obesity and pregnancy-related factors. The bidirectional relationship between mental health and metabolic risk appears mechanistically linked through alterations in eating behavior, reduced physical activity, and stress-induced physiological changes. These findings suggest that monitoring mental health status and weight management engagement in online health communities could serve as predictive indicators for identifying individuals at risk of developing type 2 diabetes, offering opportunities for early intervention and preventive care strategies.

title: Education about pain and experience with cognitive-based interventions do not reduce healthcare professionals’ chronic pain
summary: This study reveals a paradoxical finding: while cognitive-based interventions including pain neuroscience education, cognitive behavioral therapy, and acceptance commitment therapy are theoretically established to reduce chronic pain symptoms, healthcare professionals with chronic pain who possessed both specialized knowledge of and direct experience with these interventions did not experience reduced pain intensity compared to those without such exposure. The research demonstrated that chronic pain affects a substantial portion of healthcare professionals, with particularly high prevalence among women, younger professionals, and those working rotating shifts, with low back pain being the most commonly reported condition. Notably, among healthcare professionals who recovered from chronic pain, only 11% attributed their recovery to cognitive-based interventions, suggesting that spontaneous recovery and physical therapy played more significant roles than psychological approaches. While pain intensity did not differ between primary chronic pain (without identifiable tissue damage) and secondary chronic pain presentations, quality of life was measurably lower in affected healthcare professionals compared to healthy colleagues. The findings suggest that despite the theoretical basis of the biopsychosocial model and brain prediction mechanisms underlying pain perception, implementing these evidence-based psychological interventions alone may be insufficient for managing chronic pain in this population, and additional treatment modalities or approaches may be necessary.

title: Psychological Distress in Women with Fibromyalgia: The Roles of Body Appreciation, Self-Compassion, and Self-Criticism
summary:
# Summary

Fibromyalgia predominantly affects women and is characterized by continuous pain, poor sleep quality, fatigue, and cognitive difficulties, with approximately 77% of patients developing comorbid depression and anxiety. The study reveals that psychological distress in fibromyalgia emerges through a complex pathway: the disease reduces body appreciation and self-compassion while simultaneously increasing self-criticism, each of which independently elevates depression and anxiety symptoms. Self-criticism serves as a key mediator between reduced body appreciation and depression/anxiety, while self-compassion moderates this relationship by protective buffering effects. Notably, interventions centered on enhancing self-compassion—ranging from online writing exercises to group sessions—effectively reduce both depression and anxiety while improving functional status and well-being in fibromyalgia patients. The findings suggest that psychological interventions targeting self-compassion and body image acceptance may represent important adjunctive treatments for managing the psychiatric burden associated with this chronic pain condition.

title: Associations between death anxiety and fear of illness progression or recurrence: A protocol for a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review protocol examines the associations between death anxiety and fear of illness progression or recurrence across chronic physical illnesses. The research highlights that death anxiety is strongly linked to fear of progression or recurrence and represents a transdiagnostic factor across multiple psychopathologies associated with chronic disease. Chronic physical illnesses, including cancer, endometriosis, coronary heart disease, and stroke, elevate risk for comorbid anxiety and depression, which in turn are associated with poor disease management outcomes such as decreased metabolic control and medication non-adherence. Fear of cancer recurrence specifically correlates with anxiety, depression, rumination, and distress, while cancer diagnosis triggers existential awareness of mortality that perpetuates death anxiety. The review identifies cognitive behavioral therapy and meaning-focused psychological interventions as effective treatments for reducing death anxiety symptoms across patient populations. This protocol aims to synthesize evidence on these psychological associations in chronic illness to establish death anxiety as a clinically relevant target for intervention in disease management and mental health outcomes.

title: Mental Disorders and Having a First Child Among Young Adults: A Nationwide Register‐Based Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide register-based cohort study examined how mental disorders affect first childbirth rates among young adults born 1980–1995, finding complex age-dependent and disorder-specific patterns. Mental disorders overall were associated with substantially reduced likelihood of parenthood, with psychotic disorders and intellectual disabilities showing the strongest effects—for example, men with schizophrenia were 83% less likely to have a first child, while those with mood disorders were 38% less likely. However, before age 25, certain conditions including substance use disorders, anxiety disorders, and childhood-onset disorders paradoxically showed higher rates of early parenthood, suggesting distinct developmental pathways. Partnership status strongly mediated these associations, as individuals with mental disorders were significantly less likely to cohabit, which substantially reduced their opportunity for childbearing. The findings highlight that mental illness reduces fertility primarily through barriers to relationship formation rather than biological infertility, with implications for reproductive counseling and psychosocial support in this population.

title: The influence of primary dysmenorrhea on chronotypes, social jetlag, and night eating habits: a cross-sectional study of anxiety and depression
summary: Primary dysmenorrhea (PD) initiates a complex pathophysiological cascade beginning with cyclooxygenase pathway activation, which increases prostaglandin production and triggers myometrial contractions that restrict uterine blood flow and create hypoxia. This hypoxic environment perpetuates myometrial hypercontractility and pelvic pain, while simultaneously promoting widespread comorbidities including depression, anxiety, night eating syndrome, and circadian rhythm disruptions. The study demonstrates a severity-dependent relationship wherein patients with severe PD exhibit significantly elevated Beck Anxiety and Depression Inventory scores, higher rates of night eating syndrome and social jetlag, and increased inflammatory markers (C-reactive protein) compared to mild PD groups. Beyond mood disorders, severe PD associates with heightened gastrointestinal symptoms (nausea, diarrhea, abdominal pain), dizziness, and fatigue, suggesting multisystem involvement. Notably, circadian rhythm gene variants identified in PD patients may mechanistically link dysmenorrhea to disrupted sleep-wake cycles and maladaptive eating behaviors, creating reinforcing cycles of metabolic dysfunction. These findings underscore PD as a multidimensional condition requiring integrated management addressing both nociceptive and affective domains to prevent progression to chronic pain and psychological comorbidities.

title: What can speech tell us about pain?
summary:
# Speech Biomarkers and Pain Modulation

Natural speech characteristics serve as quantifiable biomarkers of pain sensitivity and pain experience, with specific acoustic features correlating with individual pain thresholds across multiple pain modalities. Both acute and chronic pain alter speech production measurably, including increased syllabic rate and speech-to-pause ratio during acute pain, while chronic pain induces slowed speech patterns. Remarkably, engaging in speech tasks—particularly those requiring complex cognitive and motor planning such as monologues—reliably reduces pain intensity, with the analgesic effect increasing proportionally to task complexity compared to simpler vocalizations like sustained vowels. This pain-reducing mechanism appears to operate through top-down cognitive processes that compete with nociceptive processing, as evidenced by reduced speech volume and altered fundamental frequency during pain states. Acoustic analysis of speech holds promise as a novel, non-invasive clinical tool for objective pain assessment and management, complementing traditional pain evaluation methods.

title: Altered Brain Functional Connectivity and Neurometabolite Correlations in Orofacial Chronic Pain with Central Sensitization
summary: Central sensitization drives chronic orofacial pain through amplified neural signaling, which manifests as altered functional connectivity within the salience network and between the salience network and mesolimbic reward system. The nucleus accumbens, a key reward center component, shows strengthened connectivity with the salience network in pain patients and modulates both sensory aspects of nociception and emotional symptoms through dopaminergic transmission. Neurometabolites in the anterior cingulate cortex—including excitatory agents like glutamate, glutamine, and aspartate that bind NMDA receptors—critically modulate this altered connectivity; excessive NMDA receptor stimulation triggers oxidative stress and free radical production, which drives central sensitization despite glutathione's protective detoxification efforts. Specific connectivity patterns correlate with central sensitization severity: stronger connections between ACC and reward-processing regions (putamen, globus pallidus) and between left rostral prefrontal cortex and nucleus accumbens associate with higher symptom burden. These findings reveal that chronic orofacial pain in central sensitization involves a dysregulated network where excitatory neurometabolite imbalance, oxidative stress, and heightened salience-mesolimbic coupling combine to sustain pain hypersensitivity and associated anxiety.

title: Sociodemographically differential patterns of chronic pain progression revealed by analyzing the all of us research program data
summary:
# Summary

This analysis of the All of Us Research Program data reveals significant sociodemographic disparities in chronic pain progression and comorbidity patterns. Females experience substantially higher prevalence of fibromyalgia (9.99-fold odds), migraine (3.31-fold odds), anxiety, and depression compared to males, while Black or African American individuals show elevated rates of chronic low back pain and schizophrenia. Chronic pain conditions demonstrate interconnected progression pathways, with chronic low back pain and migraine serving as risk factors for developing subsequent conditions including anxiety, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, and other pain syndromes. Sex-based differences in chronic overlapping pain conditions appear mediated by biological mechanisms including hormone factors, central sensitization, and inflammatory responses, while racial disparities in pain progression may involve pain sensitivity differences, psychological responses to chronic pain, elevated stress levels, and cumulative physiological burden (allostatic load). These findings highlight how demographic variables fundamentally shape both the epidemiology of chronic pain and the trajectory of comorbid disease development, suggesting that personalized pain management strategies must account for these sociodemographic patterns.

title: Open-Label-Placebos can reduce pain, but not indigestion during gluten challenge in chronic pain patients
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how open-label placebos and expectation effects influence symptom responses to gluten challenge in chronic pain patients with conditions including fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, and endometriosis. Positive instructions significantly reduced pain reports compared to neutral instructions, with a strong correlation between treatment expectations and actual pain relief, suggesting expectation effects rather than gluten itself drive pain modulation in these populations. However, neither gluten exposure nor instructional framing affected indigestion symptoms, indicating dissociable mechanisms between pain and gastrointestinal outcomes. The findings highlight the substantial role of psychological factors—including positive instructions, treatment expectations, and conditioning processes—in mediating dietary intervention effects on chronic pain, while underscoring that gluten per se has minimal direct biological impact on pain or digestive outcomes in non-celiac individuals. These results suggest that media narratives and nocebo responses may reinforce symptom reporting related to gluten in the absence of objective biomarkers, and that optimizing expectancy through positive framing could enhance therapeutic benefit in chronic pain management.

title: Health effects associated with exposure of children to physical violence, psychological violence and neglect: a Burden of Proof study
summary: This Burden of Proof study systematically evaluates the health consequences of childhood violence and neglect across multiple disease outcomes. Physical violence exposure demonstrates the strongest associations with major depressive disorder and ischaemic heart disease, each carrying at least a 16% increased risk, alongside medium-strength links to anxiety disorders, substance use disorders, and eating disorders. Psychological violence shows high-strength associations with drug use disorders, migraine, and gynaecological diseases, while neglect particularly increases anxiety disorder risk by at least 15%. Beyond direct health impacts, childhood violence exposure broadly increases the risk of physical injuries, developmental problems, chronic diseases, and substance use, while also predicting subsequent violent behaviour, violence victimization, and reduced educational attainment. These findings underscore violence against children as a pervasive public health burden with substantial long-term consequences spanning mental health, cardiometabolic, reproductive, and substance-related outcomes.

title: Internalized stigma mediates the relationship between pelvic pain severity and both depression and anxiety in women with chronic pelvic pain
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how internalized stigma influences the relationship between chronic pelvic pain (CPP) severity and psychological distress in women. The researchers found that CPP severity is directly associated with both depression and anxiety sensitivity, and that internalized stigma serves as a critical moderating factor that strengthens these associations. Specifically, depression and anxiety sensitivity have more pronounced effects on CPP severity in women who report higher internalized stigma, suggesting that stigma amplifies the psychological burden of pain. The study also documented the broader impact of CPP, including its associations with endometriosis, dysmenorrhea, and other gynecological conditions, as well as significant reductions in quality of life, social interaction, physical activity, and work functioning. These findings highlight that addressing internalized stigma may be an important therapeutic target for improving psychological outcomes and pain management in women with chronic pelvic pain, particularly those with endometriosis-related conditions.

title: Paraventricular hypothalamic input to anterior cingulate cortex controls food preferences in chronic visceral pain mice
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a neural circuit linking chronic visceral pain to altered feeding behavior in mice. Cholecystokinin-expressing neurons in the paraventricular hypothalamus project to glutamatergic neurons in the anterior cingulate cortex, and this PVHCCK-to-ACCGlu circuit encodes changes in food preference during chronic visceral pain induced by neonatal colonic inflammation. In pain-afflicted mice, this circuit exhibits heightened neural activity and drives preference away from fatty foods (intralipid) toward simple sugars (sucrose), while chemogenetic inhibition of the circuit simultaneously reduces both visceral pain symptoms and reverses the maladaptive food preference shift. Conversely, activating this circuit in healthy mice reproduces pain hypersensitivity and alters food choices, establishing the circuit's causal role in pain-induced feeding dysfunction. These findings mechanistically link pain processing circuits to nutritional behavior and suggest therapeutic targeting of this pathway may address both chronic pain and associated eating disturbances in conditions like endometriosis and other visceral pain disorders.

title: Association between stress and dysmenorrhea among Chinese female adolescent students: a cross-sectional epidemiology study
summary: This cross-sectional study of Chinese female adolescents demonstrates a strong association between stress coping ability and primary dysmenorrhea, with adolescents reporting moderate difficulty coping with stress showing 1.6-fold increased risk and those with severe difficulty showing 1.97-fold increased risk compared to those with no difficulty. Beyond stress, the research identified multiple contributing factors including menstrual cycle characteristics, dietary structure, sleep duration, family history, and psychological disorders such as depression and anxiety. The underlying mechanisms likely involve the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and cortisol's effects on reproductive health, with stress potentially operating through both direct biological pathways and alterations in pain perception and central stress sensitivity. Notably, dysmenorrhea presents with diverse symptoms in over 20% of students (insomnia, diarrhea, headaches, nausea) and over 50% reporting back pain, while longer sleep duration (>8 hours) appeared protective against dysmenorrhea. The study suggests a bidirectional relationship between psychological stress and menstrual pain, where monthly repeated menstrual pain may further increase stress burden in adolescents.

title: Causal Relationship Between Depression and Traumatic Brain Injury: A Two‐Sample Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates bidirectional causal relationships between depression and traumatic brain injury (TBI), with depression increasing TBI risk and TBI subsequently elevating depression risk. TBI-induced depression involves multiple pathological mechanisms, including structural brain damage (prefrontal gray matter reduction, basal ganglia lesions, frontal cortex damage) and neurochemical dysfunction across cholinergic, glutamatergic, dopaminergic, and serotonergic systems, alongside elevated proinflammatory cytokines. Post-TBI complications including severe disability, social isolation, chronic pain, and fatigue further contribute to depressive symptoms, while depression itself increases TBI vulnerability through emotional dysregulation, self-injurious behavior, and elevated fall risk. Depression demonstrates significant comorbidity with multiple conditions including hypertension, insomnia, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and obesity, while other factors such as stroke, sleep disorders, and chronic pain independently cause depression. Current treatment approaches span pharmacological interventions (SSRIs, SNRIs, tricyclic antidepressants, MAOIs) and psychotherapeutic modalities (cognitive behavioral therapy, interpersonal therapy, mindfulness-based approaches, family interventions) alongside neuromodulatory techniques such as transcranial magnetic stimulation and electroconvulsive therapy.

title: Dysmenorrhea and Adolescent Mental Health: A School‐Based Cross‐Sectional Study
summary: This school-based cross-sectional study demonstrates a strong bidirectional association between dysmenorrhea and adolescent mental health disorders. Dysmenorrhea was significantly associated with depression, anxiety, self-injury, and suicidal ideation, with severe dysmenorrhea occurring 1.75 to 2.89 times more frequently in girls with these mental health conditions. The relationship appears multifactorial: underlying mental health conditions may amplify pain perception through neurobiological mechanisms, while chronic menstrual pain itself can exacerbate psychological distress and contribute to mood and behavioral problems through pain-related social withdrawal and interference with coping adaptation. Hormonal fluctuations, fear-avoidance behaviors, and catastrophizing about pain were identified as potential mechanistic links. The substantial functional impact of dysmenorrhea—including missed school attendance and social activities—further compounds psychological wellbeing in this vulnerable adolescent population.

title: Social influences in the experience of transition to or from long-term (chronic) pain: A systematic review of qualitative research studies
summary: This systematic review demonstrates that chronic pain profoundly disrupts social relationships and psychological well-being through multiple interconnected pathways. Chronic pain significantly impacts family dynamics, social interactions, occupational functioning, and overall well-being, with effects intensified when individuals experience role changes within family life that lead to social embarrassment, fragmented relationships, and decreased psychological well-being. Key negative mechanisms include social isolation and withdrawal, which foster rumination and low mood in a self-perpetuating cycle, while the burden of awareness that one's pain strains family members paradoxically increases pain symptoms. Importantly, the review identifies protective factors: family emotional support and validation, friends providing distraction, community engagement, and formal support networks through groups and online communities all reduce pain symptoms and counteract isolation. Notably, pre-emptive social withdrawal and self-silencing to maintain normality paradoxically increase loneliness, whereas engaging in activities like caring for others provides pain distraction and boosts well-being despite potential physical exacerbation. These findings underscore that chronic pain is fundamentally a social experience requiring integrated interventions that strengthen support networks and address psychological barriers to social engagement.

title: Chronic Pain and Substance Use Disorders: A Brief Narrative Review of Genetic, Neurobiological, and Environmental Contributions to Comorbidity
journal: Journal of psychiatry and brain science
summary:
# Chronic Pain and Substance Use Disorders: Key Findings

Chronic pain and substance use disorders frequently co-occur, with approximately 40% of individuals with chronic pain meeting criteria for a substance use disorder, reflecting shared genetic vulnerabilities and overlapping neurobiological mechanisms. Both conditions activate stress system pathways and dysregulate dopaminergic signaling in reward-processing brain regions including the ventral tegmental area, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex, creating a biological vulnerability to chronic pain-related anhedonia that increases substance abuse risk. Chronic pain drives specific maladaptive neurochemical changes—such as decreased phasic dopamine release and increased tonic dopamine, alterations that also characterize chronic alcohol exposure—while simultaneously reducing hippocampal volume and activating corticotropin-releasing factor signaling associated with both pain chronification and withdrawal symptoms. Although opioids, alcohol, and cannabis may provide temporary pain relief, their prolonged use paradoxically worsens pain trajectories and triggers abuse potential, with genetic liability studies revealing bidirectional causal relationships between multi-site chronic pain and cannabis, alcohol, and tobacco use disorders. Neurobiologically, the insular and anterior cingulate cortices mediate emotional processing of pain and cravings in substance use disorders, while orexin receptor signaling in the hypothalamus and ventral tegmental area emerges as a potential therapeutic target for reducing both pain and addiction-related reward-seeking. Social support represents a critical protective factor against both chronic pain and substance use disorders, highlighting the importance of psychosocial interventions alongside neurobiologically-informed pharmacological approaches.

title: Home-based self-administered transcranial direct current stimulation for women affected by primary dysmenorrhoea in Northeastern Brazil: a protocol study
summary: Primary dysmenorrhoea is a highly prevalent condition affecting approximately 25-90% of women that extends beyond uterine pain to encompass systemic symptoms including nausea, vomiting, dizziness, headache, fatigue, and diarrhoea, alongside comorbid depression, anxiety, and affective disorders. The condition is characterized by central nervous system dysfunctional processing and central sensitization, where peripheral nociceptive signals become amplified during menstruation, causing increased neuronal excitability and sustained activation of pain-processing brain regions including the dorsal spinal horn, limbic system, and cortical structures. Women with primary dysmenorrhoea demonstrate significant alterations in functional connectivity of the anterior cingulate cortex, disruptions in brain metabolism, and impaired pain modulatory systems, creating a hypervigilance cycle mediated by limbic and sensory systems that perpetuates chronic pain perception. This protocol study proposes transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) targeting the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) and primary motor cortex (M1) as a home-based neuromodulatory intervention to enhance cortical excitability changes that promote top-down pain control and emotional regulation. The therapeutic mechanism involves anodal stimulation of M1 to activate descending inhibitory pain pathways while simultaneously modulating the cingulate cortex, periaqueductal grey, and amygdala, combined with DLPFC stimulation to upregulate positive emotional processing and downregulate negative reactions. Previous evidence supports tDCS efficacy in reducing dysmenorrhea-related pain, anxiety, and incapacitation while improving functional capacity, positioning this dual-target approach as a promising self-administered treatment for this disabling condition.

title: Shared neural signatures in Functional Neurological Disorder and Chronic Pain: a multimodal narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Functional neurological disorder (FND) and chronic pain (CP) share overlapping neural signatures across multiple brain networks, establishing a biological basis for their frequent co-occurrence. Both conditions exhibit hyperactivation of sensorimotor networks, dysfunction in the default mode network involved in self-referential thought, and impaired emotional processing in the anterior cingulate cortex and insula. A unifying neurophysiological mechanism identified is thalamocortical dysrhythmia, characterized by abnormal theta and beta oscillations that enhance pain perception and trigger functional symptoms, alongside reduced alpha oscillations that amplify sensory sensitivity and emotional responsiveness. Psychologically, both disorders are linked to trauma and stress, with emotional triggers capable of exacerbating symptoms; notably, chronic pain often precedes FND and predicts poorer outcomes. The shared pathophysiology involves defective inhibitory mechanisms and maladaptive sensorimotor-limbic integration, with clinical evidence showing that fibromyalgia predicts functional seizures with a 75% positive predictive value, suggesting a continuum of overlapping neurobiological dysfunction across these conditions.

title: The Burden of Chronic Pain on Women: A Secondary Analysis of Data From the National Study on Disability (ENDISC) in Chile
summary:
# Summary

This secondary analysis of Chilean national disability data reveals that chronic pain affects 17.2% of adult women, with highest prevalence in the 51-65 age group, and carries substantial consequences for mental and physical health. Women with chronic pain demonstrate significantly worse functioning across all domains and exhibit elevated rates of depression and anxiety, with biological factors including estrogen fluctuations, as well as conditions like endometriosis and menopause, contributing to pain susceptibility. Socioeconomic factors including lower education, reduced income, unemployment, lack of partner support, and caregiving responsibilities substantially amplify the burden of chronic pain and impair recovery outcomes. Employment emerges as a critical protective factor that improves functional outcomes and reduces disability-related retirement, while age-related biological, psychological, and social changes during the 51-65 year transitional phase further exacerbate pain perception. The study underscores that chronic pain in women is not merely a medical condition but a complex biopsychosocial phenomenon with profound implications for disability, workforce participation, and overall well-being.

title: Genome-wide insights into generalised anxiety using a dimensional symptom severity approach
summary: This genome-wide association study identified 82 independent variants across 76 loci associated with anxiety symptom severity using a dimensional approach, with polygenic scores significantly predicting both anxiety severity and disorder diagnosis across multiple ancestry populations. The genetic architecture of anxiety symptom severity shows substantial overlap with numerous mental and physical health conditions, including depression, neuroticism, irritable bowel syndrome, coronary artery disease, migraine, and notably endometriosis, suggesting shared biological pathways across these comorbid traits. Gene-based and pathway analyses revealed that synaptic and axonal processes are centrally implicated in anxiety pathogenesis, with enriched expression of relevant genes in the brain. These findings underscore the polygenic nature of anxiety and its interconnection with both psychiatric and somatic conditions, implicating neurobiological mechanisms related to synaptic function as key contributors to anxiety susceptibility.

title: Psychological Factors Contributing to Health-Related Quality of Life Following Endometriosis Surgery: Results of a Cross-Sectional Study
summary: Endometriosis causes severe chronic pain that substantially impairs health-related quality of life, and while surgical intervention effectively reduces pain intensity, psychological factors significantly modulate post-surgical outcomes. Beyond pain intensity itself, pain catastrophizing and comorbid anxiety and depression independently contribute to reduced quality of life in endometriosis patients, with pain catastrophizing showing particularly strong negative associations independent of actual pain levels. Additional factors including fatigue, sleep quality, analgesic use, and hormonal medication also correlate with quality of life outcomes following surgery. The study of 195 post-surgical endometriosis patients reveals that central sensitization and heightened pain perception mechanisms amplify the psychological burden of the condition. These findings underscore that surgical treatment alone is insufficient for optimizing patient outcomes, and extended post-surgical psychological care targeting pain catastrophizing, anxiety, and depression may be warranted to improve long-term quality of life across all endometriosis severity stages.

title: The Intersection of Psychology and Gynecology: A Comprehensive Review of Pain Management Strategies in Women's Health
summary:
# Summary: Psychology and Gynecology in Pain Management

Chronic gynecological pain conditions including dysmenorrhea, endometriosis, vulvodynia, and chronic pelvic pain syndrome are strongly associated with psychological comorbidities such as anxiety and depression, reflecting a biopsychosocial pathophysiology. Multiple biological mechanisms contribute to gynecological pain: reproductive hormones estrogen and progesterone regulate pain perception through activation of opioidergic and serotonergic inhibitory pathways and GABAergic inhibition, while inflammatory mediators like prostaglandins and elevated estrogen levels during the follicular phase increase pain sensitivity and dysmenorrhea. Chronic conditions such as endometriosis and vulvodynia trigger central sensitization through prolonged nociceptive signaling, glial cell activation, and pro-inflammatory cytokine production, leading to pain amplification and neuroplastic changes in the spinal cord and brain regions involved in pain processing. Psychological factors including depression, anxiety, PTSD, and trauma-related autonomic dysregulation intensify pain perception and worsen treatment outcomes, while maladaptive cognitions like catastrophizing promote disability and poor therapeutic responses. Integrative treatment approaches combining pharmacological interventions (NSAIDs, hormonal therapies, GnRH analogs) with psychological therapies including cognitive-behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based stress reduction, biofeedback, and virtual reality show efficacy in reducing symptoms across multiple gynecological pain disorders.

title: Pain Acceptance in Chronic Postoperative Pain: Concept Analysis
journal: Pain management nursing : official journal of the American Society of Pain Management Nurses
summary: Chronic postoperative pain (CPOP) represents a significant clinical burden that extends beyond physical symptoms, causing psychological trauma, functional restrictions, and opioid dependence through a complex interplay of genetic and psychosocial factors including preoperative pain, anxiety, and depression. Pain acceptance emerges as a critical psychological construct that enhances well-being, reduces anxiety and depression, improves pain control, and prevents opioid misuse and dependence in patients with CPOP and other chronic pain conditions including endometriosis and fibromyalgia. Psychological interventions such as Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) and mindfulness-based approaches promote pain acceptance by fostering psychological flexibility, open-mindedness, present orientation, and self-acceptance, which are further supported by preoperative education, social support systems, and motivational counseling. Key psychological mechanisms underlying pain acceptance include developing resilience and optimism through open-mindedness, building endurance through goal-setting and graded physical activities, and improving self-confidence through self-acceptance. The integration of a biopsychosocial model in clinical practice enables healthcare providers to develop comprehensive interventions that address both the physical and psychological dimensions of CPOP, ultimately improving quality of life and reducing opioid dependency. However, clinicians must balance promoting pain acceptance with ensuring patients remain motivated to seek appropriate medical care for treatable conditions.

title: Cognitive Functioning in Females with Endometriosis-Associated Chronic Pelvic Pain: A Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic pelvic pain through estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory responses in endometriotic lesions, which subsequently triggers cognitive impairment in affected women. The pain develops through multiple mechanisms including central sensitization, cross-organ sensitization via shared pelvic nerve pathways, and direct bidirectional interactions between endometriotic lesions and the central nervous system, with the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis amplifying pain experiences. Neuroimaging studies reveal that women with endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain exhibit altered brain connectivity patterns, including decreased connectivity between the right anterior insula and both the cerebellum and middle frontal gyrus, alongside enhanced connectivity between pain-related networks and the default mode network that correlates with pain intensity. Self-reported cognitive complaints are highly prevalent, with women experiencing difficulties in attention, memory, and inhibitory control, alongside reduced gray matter volume in pain-related brain regions. The pain intensity and cognitive processing of pain independently impair health-related quality of life, with the majority of women reporting significant work impairment, loss of work efficiency, and diminished ability to perform daily activities. These findings demonstrate that endometriosis-associated chronic pain involves complex interactions between peripheral inflammation, central pain sensitization, and maladaptive brain network reorganization that collectively compromise cognitive functioning and functional capacity.

title: The Role of Psychological Flexibility and Psychological Factors in Chronic Pelvic Pain Among Women: A Correlational Study
summary:
# Summary

This correlational study reveals that chronic pelvic pain, commonly associated with endometriosis, vulvodynia, and adenomyosis, is substantially influenced by psychological flexibility—a mental health protective factor encompassing acceptance, cognitive defusion, values clarity, and self-as-context awareness. Women with chronic pelvic pain showed elevated psychological distress including anxiety, depression, and stress, with stress demonstrating the strongest correlation with both pain intensity and pain interference. Psychological inflexibility and its components—particularly cognitive fusion, experiential avoidance, and lack of values clarity—significantly predicted greater pain intensity, pain interference, and diminished quality of life in both physical and mental domains. The findings suggest that pain and psychological distress operate in a cyclical relationship, where enhanced psychological flexibility through acceptance and cognitive defusion strategies may reduce pain's functional impact and improve overall well-being in women with chronic pelvic pain conditions.

title: Sex-Related Differences in Chronic Pain: A Narrative Review by a Multidisciplinary Task Force
summary:
# Summary: Sex-Related Differences in Chronic Pain

Chronic pain, which persists beyond three months and represents a major cause of global disability, demonstrates striking sex differences that are rooted in both biological and psychosocial mechanisms. Women experience substantially higher prevalence and greater pain sensitivity than men, a disparity largely driven by hormonal fluctuations—particularly elevated progesterone during ovulation, which increases sensitivity to heat and pressure, whereas testosterone exerts protective anti-nociceptive effects. Multiple pain conditions show sex-biased prevalence, including endometriosis (affecting ~10% of reproductive-age women), fibromyalgia, rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and migraines, all more common in women, while cluster headaches occur more frequently in men. Beyond hormonal influences, sex differences in immune mechanisms (T cells more prominent in women, microglia in men), psychosocial factors (women more likely to use maladaptive coping like catastrophizing), and healthcare biases contribute to worse outcomes in women, who experience greater disability, depression, anxiety, and reduced quality of life. Notably, women with chronic pain exhibit distinct responses to analgesics influenced by genetic and pharmacokinetic factors, yet social support networks provide a protective buffer. These multifactorial sex differences underscore the necessity for personalized, sex-informed approaches to chronic pain diagnosis and management.

title: Pain-related support seeking? Situating the response to pain within the social context using a sample of women experiencing menstrual pain
summary: This longitudinal study examined how social contextual factors influence pain catastrophizing in women experiencing menstrual pain, including those with endometriosis. The research found a robust negative association between perceived social support and pain catastrophizing, with greater social support at baseline predicting lower catastrophizing at follow-up, independent of confounding variables such as age, menstrual pain severity, and endometriosis diagnosis. Pain catastrophizing appears to function as a pain-related coping and support-seeking strategy, suggesting it is a socially-influenced psychological response rather than purely a pathological cognition. The study demonstrated that both pain catastrophizing and pain levels decreased over the study period, though social support remained relatively stable, indicating these psychological responses can shift with time. These findings highlight the importance of social support in managing menstrual pain-related cognitions and suggest that interventions targeting menstrual pain literacy and social support could help reduce catastrophizing and improve pain management outcomes in women with dysmenorrhea.

title: Current Pathways of Care Continue to Fail Those With a Diagnosis of Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis presents a complex pain management challenge that current clinical pathways fail to adequately address, as evidenced by poor treatment satisfaction rates: only 42.8% of patients improved with hormonal medication and only 17.3% were satisfied with surgical outcomes. The pain associated with endometriosis involves multiple mechanisms—nociceptive pain from inflammation and fibrosis, neuropathic-like pain from peripheral and central nervous system alterations, and nociplastic pain from systemic inflammation and altered stress responses—with up to 40% of patients exhibiting neuropathic components and similar proportions showing nociplastic features. Current therapeutic approaches rely heavily on hormonal treatments and surgery despite limited efficacy, while evidence-based alternatives such as tricyclic antidepressants, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, and multimodal approaches combining medication with pain psychology and physiotherapy remain underutilized. Surgery itself poses significant risks, carrying a high probability of generating post-surgical chronic pain through both laparoscopic and open procedures, particularly in patients with nociplastic pain who show reduced surgical benefit. Beyond clinical concerns, endometriosis diagnosis carries substantial socioeconomic consequences, causing progressive reductions in employment and monthly income that extend beyond 12 months post-diagnosis, creating a dual burden of inadequate pain management and financial strain on affected individuals.

title: Primary and Secondary Fibromyalgia Are The Same: The Universality of Polysymptomatic Distress
summary: This study demonstrates that polysymptomatic distress (PSD), a composite measure of symptom burden, is the fundamental underlying metric of fibromyalgia, with a PSD score of ≥15 effectively distinguishing fibromyalgia patients from those without the condition. Primary and secondary fibromyalgia are functionally equivalent when PSD is controlled for, showing nearly identical pain, quality of life, and functional outcomes despite secondary fibromyalgia patients having slightly elevated joint counts and disability scores related to concurrent inflammatory disease. Higher PSD scores consistently correlate with more severe symptoms and worse health-related outcomes across all measured variables except prednisone use, suggesting that PSD is more informative for assessing disease severity than the traditional dichotomization into fibromyalgia versus non-fibromyalgia categories. Fibromyalgia patients demonstrate increased use of opioids and pregabalin, along with elevated body mass index and worse mental component scores compared to those with inflammatory conditions like rheumatoid arthritis, which is primarily characterized by structural joint damage rather than polysymptomatic distress. The universal applicability of PSD across noninflammatory fibromyalgia and inflammatory conditions comorbid with fibromyalgia indicates that polysymptomatic distress represents a fundamental disease process independent of underlying inflammatory status.

title: Validation of the menstrual sensitivity index in adolescents
summary:
# Summary

This study validates the Menstrual Sensitivity Index (MSI) in adolescents as a reliable measure of attunement to and fear of menstrual symptoms. Dysmenorrhea is highly prevalent among school-aged girls and has significant functional consequences, causing reductions in classroom attendance and potentially leading to chronic pain conditions, making early assessment critical. The MSI comprises three components—somatic anxiety, fear/danger, and medication use—and demonstrates convergent validity with pain catastrophizing and pain behaviors during menstruation, while diverging from general anxiety sensitivity, depression, and body pain measures. Notably, menstrual sensitivity appears lower in adolescents compared to adults, and the relationship between MSI scores and actual menstrual pain severity is weaker in younger populations. These findings suggest that menstrual sensitivity operates as a distinct psychological mechanism underlying dysmenorrhea experiences, with potential implications for early intervention and understanding chronic pain trajectories in adolescents.

title: Estimating the longitudinal association between pain characteristics and clinical outcomes in young people with mental ill-health
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal study in young people with mental ill-health demonstrates robust associations between multiple pain characteristics and adverse mental health outcomes. Pain intensity, pain frequency, and pain-related limitations each independently predicted increased depressive and anxiety symptoms, with pain intensity showing particularly strong associations with suicidal ideation and tobacco use risk. The bidirectional relationship between pain and mental ill-health is evident, as both chronic pain predicts worsening mental health trajectories and mental illness perpetuates pain severity. Beyond mood effects, increases in pain limitations were associated with significant decrements in social and occupational functioning, highlighting the pervasive impact of pain on young people's daily lives and quality of life. These findings underscore that pain assessment and management are essential components of comprehensive mental health treatment in this population, as the co-occurrence of these conditions appears to compound clinical severity and functional impairment.

title: Reframing Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS): Biological Basis of Disease and Recommendations for Supporting Patients
summary: Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS) is characterized by a dysregulated inflammatory response involving upregulation of IL8, NFKBIA, and TNFAIP3 genes, which drive elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-1, and PMN-elastase. The disease features a skewed T-helper cell balance with reduced interferon-gamma and elevated interleukin-10, contributing to the cardinal symptom of fatigue alongside post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and widespread pain. Neuroimaging reveals structural brain abnormalities including reduced white and gray matter volume, with functional imaging demonstrating impaired cerebral blood perfusion, reduced internal carotid and vertebral artery blood flow, and alterations in brain activity patterns. ME/CFS frequently follows viral infections, particularly COVID-19 and Epstein-Barr virus, suggesting an infection-triggered pathophysiology, and demonstrates significant overlap with related post-infectious conditions including Long COVID. Comorbid psychiatric conditions such as depression and anxiety are common, though the disease has a documented biological basis distinct from psychological etiology. Management approaches focus on exertional pacing strategies to minimize post-exertional symptoms, while emerging biologics such as monoclonal antibodies represent potential future therapeutic targets.

title: From Roots to Canopy: Understanding Chronic Pelvic Pain Through a Tree-Inspired Model
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pelvic pain represents a complex, multifactorial condition involving interconnected peripheral, spinal, and central mechanisms that extend beyond the pelvic region itself. Over 80% of women with chronic pelvic pain have a pelvic floor myofascial component, and up to 90% exhibit a musculoskeletal component, often triggered by protective muscular bracing in response to pain that progressively leads to myalgia. The condition frequently coexists with painful bladder syndrome (48% of cases), irritable bowel syndrome (30-70% of cases), and fibromyalgia, demonstrating viscero-visceral sensitization through neuronal convergence that hypersensitizes multiple organ systems. Neurobiologically, peripheral inflammation activates nociceptors through proinflammatory cytokines, chemokines, and Toll-like receptors, triggering a cascade involving substance P and glutamate release in the dorsal horn that shifts the balance toward excitatory over inhibitory neurotransmission and establishes central sensitization. Microglial activation in the dorsal horn reinforces peripheral connections and develops "molecular pain memory," while astrocytes perpetuate central sensitization through multiple neuronal and inflammatory pathways, compounded by blood-brain barrier leakiness that permits peripheral cytokine entry into the central nervous system. Psychological factors including adverse childhood events, depression, anxiety, and anger contribute to this pain amplification, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of fear, avoidance, and functional impairment that underscores the need for comprehensive, multimodal treatment approaches.

title: Interpretation bias and its relationship with pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that interpretation bias—the tendency to perceive ambiguous information as pain-related or threatening—is significantly associated with chronic pain and related outcomes. People with chronic pain display substantially greater interpretation bias than those without pain (effect size g = 0.60), with correlations to pain severity, pain interference, and pain catastrophizing, though these individual correlations are modest in magnitude. The review identifies a mechanistic pathway whereby interpretation bias drives catastrophic thinking about pain, leading to avoidance behaviors and hypervigilance that perpetuate chronic pain, consistent with fear-avoidance and threat-interpretation models. Importantly, intervention studies using Cognitive Bias Modification for Interpretation successfully modified interpretation bias and improved pain outcomes in chronic pain populations. Measurement methodology significantly influences findings, with word association tasks detecting greater interpretation bias than ambiguous scenario tasks. These findings suggest that targeting interpretation bias through cognitive interventions represents a viable therapeutic approach for reducing chronic pain and associated disability.

title: The epidemiology, management, and the associated burden of migraine in Australian primary care: a retrospective analysis of electronic health record data
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: This retrospective analysis of Australian primary care electronic health records examined the epidemiology and management burden of migraine, revealing substantial comorbidity patterns and treatment gaps. Migraine patients presented with characteristic acute symptoms including photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, and vomiting, while also demonstrating significantly elevated associations with mental health conditions (depression and anxiety occurring approximately four times more frequently than in non-migraine populations), cardiovascular disease, and chronic pain conditions such as fibromyalgia and endometriosis. The study identified upper respiratory tract infections, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and urinary tract infections as frequent comorbidities in migraine patients. Current treatment patterns showed suboptimal use of evidence-based therapies, with triptans prescribed in only 51% of cases despite their efficacy, while opioids were used in over half of migraine patients, indicating potential overreliance on non-specific pain management. Newer CGRP-targeted therapies, including CGRP receptor antagonists and anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies for preventive management, along with rimegepant offering dual acute and preventive indications, represent more targeted treatment options that remain underutilized in primary care practice.

title: Employees’ experiences of chronic pain in the workplace
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pain significantly impairs workplace functioning, leading to higher absenteeism, reduced productivity, unemployment, and loss of earnings, while negatively affecting both physical and mental health. Effective chronic pain self-management at work is facilitated by flexible and hybrid working arrangements, reasonable workplace adjustments, manageable workloads, in-house occupational health services, and supportive employee networks—factors that increase autonomy and control. Conversely, major barriers include workplace stigma and discrimination, organizational lack of awareness about chronic pain impacts, unmanageable workloads, vague disability policies, and limited access to occupational health and well-being services. Line managers play a critical gatekeeping role as either enablers or barriers to disability disclosure and support access, with training and personal experience of chronic conditions significantly improving their understanding and provision of accommodations. Job type matters substantially, as desk-based roles facilitate self-management through easier adjustments, whereas physically demanding jobs present intractable challenges. Psychologically safe workplace environments that normalize discussion of chronic conditions and foster supportive networks are essential for enabling employees to access the support and accommodations necessary to sustain employment.

title: Factors Influencing Chronic Pain After Hysterectomy for Uterine Fibroids: Development and Validation of a Nomogram Prediction Model
summary: This study developed and validated a nomogram prediction model to identify patients at high risk for chronic pain following hysterectomy for uterine fibroids. The researchers identified five major risk factors: preoperative pain, history of abdominal or pelvic surgery, endometriosis, anxiety, and high pain catastrophizing scores (Pain Catastrophic Scale). Endometriosis emerges as a particularly important risk factor through its pathophysiological mechanisms—as an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease, it triggers chronic inflammation that progressively weakens pain inhibition and amplifies sensory input, ultimately leading to central sensitization and persistent postoperative pain. Similarly, preoperative localized pain causes central nervous system changes in pain processing that cannot be reversed by hysterectomy alone. Pain catastrophizing independently increased the risk of chronic postsurgical pain by 17% per point increase on the Pain Catastrophic Scale. The nomogram model integrates these biological and psychological predictors to enable clinicians to identify high-risk patients preoperatively and potentially implement preventive interventions, such as psychological treatments that have demonstrated efficacy in reducing chronic pelvic pain and associated complications.

title: Clinical Reasoning and Practices in the Osteopathic Management of Visceral Disorders: A Grounded Theory Study in the Italian Context
summary: Functional visceral disorders, including conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome and inflammatory bowel disease, are frequently associated with dysregulation of the central nervous system and commonly co-occur with multiple psychological and somatic comorbidities including anxiety, depression, insomnia, and fibromyalgia. Visceral pain in these disorders can manifest as referred pain in somatic structures, compounding diagnostic complexity and contributing to chronic musculoskeletal pain presentations. Organic conditions such as endometriosis, inflammatory bowel disease, and organic dyspepsia share overlapping features with functional visceral disorders despite their distinct etiologies. Osteopathic practitioners commonly employ visceral manipulation techniques targeting structures like the insula, which regulates both peristalsis and pain signaling, to address these disorders; however, systematic reviews have reported no statistically significant therapeutic benefit from visceral osteopathic interventions. This discrepancy between clinical practice and evidence-based outcomes highlights the need for further rigorous investigation into the mechanisms underlying osteopathic approaches to visceral dysfunction and their efficacy in managing the complex comorbidity patterns associated with these conditions.

title: Defining the Chronic Complexities of hEDS and HSD: A Global Survey of Diagnostic Challenges, Life-Long Comorbidities, and Unmet Needs
summary: This global survey reveals the severe burden and diagnostic challenges of hypermobility Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) and hypermobility spectrum disorder (HSD), which stem from collagen mutations affecting connective tissue integrity and cause joint hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and ligament fragility. Patients with hEDS experience a particularly severe disease course, averaging 24 comorbid conditions with nearly universal chronic pain (98.9%) alongside gastrointestinal disorders (84.3%), dysautonomia (71.4%), and significant neurological complications including cerebrospinal fluid leaks, cranio-cervical instability, and small-fiber neuropathy. A major challenge is the diagnostic odyssey patients face, with symptom onset typically occurring around age 9.3 but diagnosis not achieved until age 31.4, representing a 22-year delay driven partly by the absence of a diagnostic test and lack of clinical awareness. Common triggering events for symptom manifestation include puberty and infections, while specific anatomical and neurological features distinguish hEDS from HSD, including higher rates of scoliosis, Raynaud's phenomenon, occipital neuralgia, and notably, over 40-fold increased risk of tendon rupture. Current pain management relies primarily on medical marijuana and ketamine, indicating insufficient evidence-based treatments for this complex, multisystem condition affecting every organ system and profoundly impacting quality of life.

title: A Literature Review on Pain Management in Women During Medical Procedures: Gaps, Challenges, and Recommendations
summary: Women experience significant disparities in pain management across medical procedures and chronic pain conditions, driven by both physiological factors and systemic gender bias. Hormonal fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone modulate pain thresholds and sensitivity, while conditions including endometriosis, fibromyalgia, interstitial cystitis, and vulvodynia disproportionately affect women. Common gynecological procedures such as IUD insertion, hysteroscopy, and cervical biopsy cause moderate to severe pain in the majority of patients, yet local and cervical anesthesia remain underutilized despite proven effectiveness in reducing pain perception. Healthcare provider bias—including gender stereotypes, unconscious assumptions about emotionality, and attributing women's pain to psychosocial causes—contributes to systematic undertreatment, with women receiving 25% fewer opioids than men and lower rates of analgesics in emergency settings. Interventions including implicit bias training, gender-adapted clinical guidelines, patient-centered communication, and guided imagery show promise in improving outcomes and patient satisfaction. The review highlights that addressing these disparities requires standardized gender-sensitive protocols, adequate female representation in clinical research with sex-based analyses, and awareness among providers that unconscious bias recognition can promote more equitable and respectful patient interactions.

title: Loneliness and analgesic overuse among low-dose estrogen-progestin users in Japan
summary: Among Japanese women using low-dose estrogen-progestin contraceptives, loneliness emerges as a significant psychosocial factor associated with both menstrual disorders and increased analgesic overuse. High loneliness scores, measured via the Three-Item Loneliness Scale, were strongly associated with frequent concomitant analgesic use (≥1 day per month), with particularly heavy analgesic consumption (≥10 days monthly) linked to endometriosis and low back pain. The relationship between loneliness and physical pain appears partially mediated by inflammatory mechanisms and bidirectionally connected to poor sleep quality, which independently increases pain sensitivity and analgesic consumption. Women experiencing low health literacy and poor sleep quality (PSQI ≥6) showed significantly elevated loneliness levels, suggesting a complex interplay between psychological distress, somatic symptoms, and medication overuse patterns. Chronic analgesic overuse poses additional clinical risks including gastrointestinal and renal dysfunction, while frequent use can paradoxically trigger medication overuse headaches. These findings underscore the importance of integrated psychosocial assessment and counseling alongside hormonal and pharmacological management in women with menstrual disorders, particularly those at risk for analgesic dependence.

title: Measurement of fatigue in sickle cell disease: a systematic review of fatigue measures
summary: Sickle cell disease is a chronic inherited disorder caused by abnormal hemoglobin production that leads to erythrocyte sickling, hemoglobin polymerization in deoxygenated red blood cells, and ultimately tissue damage through hyperviscosity, ischemic-reperfusion injury, hemolysis, and inflammation. The disease manifests with acute bone pain as its hallmark symptom alongside chronic anemia, and fatigue emerges as a highly prevalent complication affecting over 65% of patients, significantly reducing quality of life and disrupting daily activities. Fatigue in sickle cell disease is multidimensional, encompassing physical, mental, cognitive, and emotional components, and is driven by multiple disease complications including chronic hemolytic anemia, systemic inflammation, and chronic pain. This systematic review identified nine distinct dimensions of fatigue and evaluated multiple assessment tools including the PROMIS system, PedsQL™ MFS, Fatigue Severity Scale, Multidimensional Fatigue Inventory, Brief Fatigue Inventory, and Multidimensional Fatigue Symptom Inventory–Short Form as approaches to measuring this debilitating symptom. The comprehensive evaluation of fatigue measurement instruments underscores the importance of standardized assessment in capturing the full burden of fatigue in sickle cell disease patients.

title: Genital pain and the spectrum of bladder related symptoms: Findings from the Prevention of Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms (PLUS) Research Consortium RISE FOR HEALTH Study, USA
summary:
# Summary

This study from the PLUS Research Consortium demonstrates that genital pain, including vulvodynia, is strongly associated with a broad spectrum of lower urinary tract symptoms and bladder dysfunction across a diverse female population. Women reporting genital pain exhibited significantly worse bladder health scores and bladder function indices compared to pain-free controls, alongside elevated rates of interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, urinary urgency, frequency, and voiding dysfunction. A key biological mechanism underlying these associations appears to involve pelvic floor dysfunction, wherein vulvar hypersensitivity triggers compensatory pelvic floor muscle hypertonicity that subsequently causes myofascial pain and perpetuates urinary symptoms. The study identifies chronic inflammation and central sensitization as potential contributing factors and notably demonstrates that pelvic floor physical therapy effectively improves both genital pain and associated lower urinary tract symptoms. These findings support screening for lower urinary tract symptoms in women presenting with genital pain and underscore the multifactorial nature of overlapping urogenital pain syndromes.

title: Paradoxical Insomnia Versus Atypical Obstructive Sleep Apnea or Mixed Disorder? Case Report and Literature Review
summary: Paradoxical insomnia is a sleep disorder characterized by subjective sleep deprivation despite objective evidence of adequate sleep, often involving misperception of sleep time and presenting with difficulty falling asleep and sleep fragmentation. The condition is strongly associated with psychiatric comorbidities, particularly depression and anxiety, as well as structural and functional brain alterations in regions including the caudate nucleus and hippocampus, along with distinctive electroencephalographic patterns featuring increased alpha activity and cyclic alternating patterns. Paradoxical insomnia frequently coexists with other sleep disorders such as obstructive sleep apnea and restless legs syndrome, necessitating comprehensive diagnostic evaluation to distinguish between these potentially overlapping conditions. Pharmacological management has shown promise with atypical antipsychotics, particularly olanzapine and risperidone, though chronic benzodiazepine use is contraindicated due to increased nocturnal awakenings, while cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia offers a non-pharmacological therapeutic option. The case presented illustrates the diagnostic complexity of paradoxical insomnia in a patient with prior endometriosis and multiple psychiatric conditions requiring multimodal treatment approaches. Recognition of paradoxical insomnia's distinct phenotype, characterized notably by the absence of daytime sleepiness despite subjective sleep complaints, is essential for appropriate clinical management and differentiation from other sleep and psychiatric disorders.

title: Multi-ancestry genome-wide association study of endometriosis and its clinical manifestations in ~1.4 million women: translating gene discovery into pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic targets
summary: This large multi-ancestry genome-wide association study of 1.4 million women identified genetic variants that influence endometriosis susceptibility through multiple interconnected biological pathways, including immune regulation, tissue remodeling, and cell differentiation mechanisms. The research revealed strong genetic overlap between endometriosis and adenomyosis, suggesting shared molecular underpinnings between these related gynecological conditions. Beyond the primary disease phenotype, the study demonstrated that endometriosis polygenic risk scores interact with multiple comorbid conditions including chronic abdominal pain, anxiety, migraine, and nausea, indicating that genetic architecture contributes to the broader clinical manifestation profile of the disease. These findings bridge gene discovery to pathogenic mechanisms by demonstrating how common genetic variation across diverse populations converges on specific biological processes dysregulated in endometriosis. The integration of genomic and multi-omics data provides a framework for understanding both the molecular basis of endometriosis and its association with overlapping pain and neuropsychiatric comorbidities, potentially enabling identification of novel therapeutic targets.

title: Causes of symptoms and symptom persistence in long COVID and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Long COVID and myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) share strikingly similar underlying pathophysiological mechanisms, including autoantibodies against neural targets, endothelial dysfunction, mitochondrial dysfunction, and pro-inflammatory gut dysbiosis.
