These conditions feature a complex cascade where neuroinflammation—triggered by persistent infectious agents, residual viral antigens, and peripheral inflammation—activates neural circuits that generate sickness behavior and cognitive impairment. Endothelial dysfunction drives vascular spasm and blood-brain barrier disruption, reducing cerebral blood flow and exacerbating cognitive dysfunction, while an overactive immune system depletes ATP reserves, contributing to fatigue and depressed mood. Systemic inflammation induces a hypometabolic state in the brain and creates a self-perpetuating cycle in which peripheral inflammatory mediators activate the brain's innate immune system through both humoral and neural pathways, sustaining the chronic symptom burden characteristic of both diseases.

title: The Underlying Mechanisms of Comorbid Anxiety and Depression Among Young Women: Evidence From Brain Structure and Hormone
summary: This study examines how anxiety and depression co-occur in young women by investigating underlying brain structural and hormonal mechanisms. Young women with comorbid anxiety and depression (CAD) exhibit significantly reduced gray matter volume and cortical thickness in the right superior frontal gyrus and middle occipital gyrus, brain regions critical for emotional regulation and executive function. Progesterone levels are negatively correlated with gray matter volume in these regions specifically among the CAD group, suggesting that hormonal fluctuations may contribute to structural brain changes that impair emotional compensation mechanisms. The research indicates that decreased ovarian steroid hormones (estrogen and progesterone) dysregulate neurotransmitter systems including serotonin and GABA, which normally suppress negative emotions; conversely, healthy hormone function enhances neuroplasticity and protects against mood disorders. Brain structural alterations in frontal and parietal regions appear to mediate the progression from anxiety to depression, with the limbic system playing a supporting role in this comorbid presentation. These findings highlight how sex hormone fluctuations during reproductive stages, combined with vulnerable brain architecture, place young women at elevated risk for developing comorbid anxiety and depression.

title: Investigation of Sensory and Neuropsychological Parameters in Migraine Sufferers: A Cross-Sectional Study with Negative Findings
summary: This cross-sectional study investigated sensory and neuropsychological parameters in migraine sufferers, finding that central sensitization represents a key mechanistic feature distinguishing migraine phenotypes. Specifically, statistically significant differences in central sensitization measures were observed between high-frequency episodic migraine and chronic migraine patients, whereas most other sensory and psychological parameters showed minimal group differences. The wind-up ratio, a measure of both peripheral and central sensitization derived from repeated pain stimuli, demonstrated weak to moderate correlations with emotion-associated and somatosensory-associated parameters, suggesting partial mechanistic links between pain processing and emotional regulation. Chronic migraine frequently co-occurs with other chronic pain conditions including endometriosis and low back pain, and is associated with multiple comorbidities including depression, mood disorders, and alexithymia, which reflects difficulty in emotion recognition. Although migraine is well-established to involve nociplastic mechanisms—including altered sensory thresholds, hyper-responsiveness, and impaired pain modulation—alongside structural features like mechanical allodynia and early hypothalamic activation preceding headache onset, this study's predominantly negative sensory and neuropsychological findings suggest that central sensitization quantification may be more sensitive than individual sensory testing parameters for characterizing migraine phenotypes.

title: Women's experiences of living with chronic pain: A qualitative meta‐synthesis
summary: This qualitative meta-synthesis reveals that chronic pain disproportionately affects females, who experience more pain-related conditions, higher pain severity, and longer pain duration compared to males. The biopsychosocial nature of chronic pain emerges as central to how women experience the condition, with psychological factors such as stress and anxiety amplifying pain perception, while optimism, relaxation, and positive self-efficacy beliefs can mitigate it. Beyond physical symptoms, chronic pain causes profound multidimensional losses including reduced functionality, autonomy, sleep disruption, employment, identity, and psychological distress, with many women describing a pervasive sense of losing themselves. Women's domestic responsibilities—including housework, caregiving, and parenting—significantly exacerbate their pain burden, creating a dual burden particularly for mothers. The synthesis encompasses diverse chronic pain conditions in females, including endometriosis-related pelvic pain, fibromyalgia, rheumatoid arthritis, and multiple sclerosis, underscoring the wide-ranging impact of chronic pain across various etiologies in women's lived experiences.

title: Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis Responses in Women with Endometriosis-Related Chronic Pelvic Pain
summary: Women with endometriosis-related chronic pelvic pain exhibit significant dysfunction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, the body's central stress response system. The study demonstrates that endometriosis, as a chronic inflammatory condition, is strongly associated with multiple comorbidities including dysmenorrhea, non-menstrual pain, dyspareunia, anxiety, depression, and sleep disturbances. Women with endometriosis and pain show characteristic HPA axis dysregulation marked by low salivary cortisol with dampened diurnal variation but elevated hair cortisol, indicating prolonged chronic stress exposure. Importantly, greater pain severity—both menstrual and non-menstrual—correlates with blunted cortisol and ACTH responses to stress testing, with racial differences in HPA axis responsiveness observed between Black and non-Black women. The findings suggest that chronic stress from pain itself drives progressive HPA axis dysfunction through mechanisms including immune system disinhibition and prolonged inflammation, a process potentially reversible through physical therapy and psychological intervention. These results support targeting HPA axis dysfunction as a therapeutic strategy in endometriosis management, with animal models demonstrating that stress mitigation and corticotropin-releasing hormone receptor inhibitors reduce endometriosis lesion growth.

title: Causal factors for migraine in Mendelian randomization studies: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization meta-analysis identified bidirectional causal relationships between migraine and multiple disease and lifestyle factors. Migraine was causally associated with serious vascular and neurological complications, including Alzheimer's disease, cervical artery dissection, venous thromboembolism, coronary artery disease, angina, and large artery stroke, with migraine without aura showing particularly strong associations with several of these conditions. Conversely, multiple modifiable risk factors causally influenced migraine susceptibility, including behavioral factors (television watching, smoking initiation, reduced physical activity, delayed age at first sexual intercourse, increased schooling), dietary factors (coffee and alcohol intake, cheese and salad consumption), and the gut microbiota taxon LachnospiraceaeUCG001. Additionally, psychiatric and medical conditions such as major depressive disorder, insomnia, breast cancer, and venous thromboembolism were identified as causal contributors to migraine risk. The analysis demonstrated that migraine itself consequentially influences body mass index, interleukin-2 levels, and vitamin D status, while standard pharmacological treatments including triptans, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, acetaminophen, and antiemetics remain the primary therapeutic interventions for migraine management.

title: Digital dependence and its association with weight and body image among female medical students in Pakistan: a large cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined digital dependence and its associations with body image and weight perception among female medical students in Pakistan. The research demonstrates that excessive smartphone use and nomophobia (fear of being without one's phone) are significantly associated with depression, anxiety, stress, poor sleep hygiene, and emotional dysregulation. Prolonged exposure to curated and filtered social media images distorts body image perceptions and promotes unrealistic beauty standards, with approximately 87% of participants believing photo-editing filters contribute to unrealistic body ideals and 78% perceiving societal pressure from digital media. Digital media exposure drives harmful weight-control practices and eating disturbances, while viewing others' social media content negatively impacts body satisfaction. Notably, moderate smartphone engagement characterized by regular breaks showed positive associations with general well-being, whereas prolonged usage and extreme digital detoxes were negatively associated with psychological outcomes. These findings highlight how digital dependency functions as a mechanism linking excessive smartphone use to body image concerns and mental health comorbidities in young female medical professionals.

title: From Biomarkers to Behavior: Mapping the Neuroimmune Web of Pain, Mood, and Memory
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review maps the neuroimmune mechanisms linking chronic pain, mood disturbance, and cognitive decline through interconnected pathways. Cytokines, glial activation, and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal signaling integrate nociceptive and limbic circuits to drive pain, mood dysregulation, and cognitive impairment. At the molecular level, a spinal NTRK1–IGF2–endoplasmic reticulum stress cascade drives chronic postsurgical pain, which can be reversed by TrkA inhibition or IGF2 silencing, while anti-CGRP/R monoclonal antibodies simultaneously alleviate migraine pain, depression, anxiety, fatigue, and allodynia. Physical inactivity, metabolic comorbidities (diabetes, obesity), and periodontal inflammation emerge as modifiable risk factors for the anxiety-depression-pain triad and cognitive decline. Natural compounds including curcumin and quercetin, along with lifestyle interventions (exercise, personalized nutrition, light therapy), counteract neuroinflammation and mitochondrial dysfunction through microglial inhibition and restoration of mitochondrial function. Future precision medicine approaches integrating single-cell transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and real-time ecological momentary assessment will enable dynamic phenotyping and adaptive, personalized therapeutic targeting of these interconnected conditions.

title: Assessment of the Impact of Chronic Pain on the Prevalence of Depressive Disorders in Patients with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the relationship between chronic pain and depressive disorders in endometriosis patients, revealing significant cyclical patterns tied to menstruation. Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition characterized by endometrial-like tissue outside the uterus, is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain, depressive symptoms, and anxiety, all of which substantially impair quality of life. Pain intensity and depressive symptoms both peaked dramatically during menstruation compared to other cycle phases, with 87% of patients experiencing continuous pain during menses and 40% reporting very severe depression during this period. Critically, a strong dose-response relationship emerged between pain severity and depression severity—patients with severe pain were significantly more likely to experience severe depressive symptoms, while mild pain correlated with mild depression. Beyond pain and mood, endometriosis triggered multiple comorbid symptoms including sleep disturbances, gastrointestinal complaints, reduced activity, and cognitive-emotional dimensions of pain perception that extend beyond simple tissue damage mechanisms. The coexistence of depression with chronic endometriosis-related pain substantially exacerbated social and occupational dysfunction, highlighting the need for integrated management approaches addressing both the physical and psychological burden of this condition.

title: Health Care Content and Engagement in Chronic Illness Instagram Posts: Content Analysis
summary: This content analysis examined chronic illness narratives on Instagram, finding that posts featuring medicalized content—particularly those depicting invasive medical equipment like intravenous lines and feeding tubes—generated significantly higher user engagement than educational health content. The analysis revealed that chronic pain, fibromyalgia, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome dominated the dataset, with 80% of posts created by women, while conditions susceptible to self-diagnosis (anxiety, depression, Lyme disease, mast cell activation syndrome) were substantially overrepresented compared to objectively diagnosable conditions like diabetes and cancer. Four major thematic categories emerged in post captions: health care experiences, illness journeys, community connection, and non-illness experiences, with longer captions and health care-focused content predicting higher engagement. The findings suggest that social media algorithms amplify emotionally compelling, medicalized portrayals of illness through observational learning mechanisms, potentially reinforcing illness-centered identities and overmedicalization while simultaneously fostering community support. This dynamic creates tension between the platform's value for peer support and its role in symptom reinforcement and the psychogenic spread of illness through repeated exposure to illness narratives. The study highlights how social media visibility of chronic conditions can shape illness identity formation in ways that may either empower or pathologize users depending on whether identity integration occurs through acceptance or engulfment.

title: Understanding the Comorbidities Among Psychiatric Disorders, Chronic Low Back Pain, and Spinal Degenerative Disease Using Observational and Genetically Informed Analyses
summary: This study examined the comorbidities between psychiatric disorders, chronic low back pain (CLBP), and spinal degenerative disease (SDD) using observational and genetic approaches in large population cohorts. Multiple psychiatric conditions—including anxiety, depression, posttraumatic stress disorder, and alcohol use disorder—showed strong associations with both CLBP and SDD, with particularly robust associations observed for the comorbid CLBP+SDD phenotype. Notably, anxiety and depression demonstrated the strongest associations with the combined CLBP+SDD condition, while PTSD showed a relatively stronger association with CLBP alone. Schizophrenia exhibited an inverse relationship across all pain phenotypes, suggesting protective effects. Genetic analyses using polygenic risk scores confirmed that depression and attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder variants were significantly associated with pain outcomes, and alcohol use disorder genetics specifically predicted the comorbid CLBP+SDD presentation. Mendelian randomization analyses provided evidence that internalizing disorders may have a direct causal effect on both pain conditions, and drug repurposing analyses identified histone deacetylase inhibitors as potentially targeting shared molecular pathways across these comorbid conditions.

title: The Role of Psychologists in Treating Pelvic Pain Conditions
journal: Clinics in Colon and Rectal Surgery
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and irritable bowel syndrome are both highly associated with pelvic floor dysfunction, which manifests in multiple symptom domains including pelvic pain, urinary dysfunction, sexual dysfunction, and gastrointestinal problems. Pelvic pain triggers a maladaptive cycle wherein pain causes chronic muscle tension and hypertonicity of the pelvic floor, which subsequently irritates surrounding nerves and perpetuates the pain cycle. Cross-sensitization among the bowel, bladder, and reproductive organs contributes to the complexity of these conditions, while the gut-brain axis—mediated by the microbiome, neural signaling, and neurotransmitters like serotonin—links gastrointestinal and psychological symptoms. Chronic pelvic pain is strongly associated with depression, anxiety, reduced quality of life, sexual and relationship dysfunction, and fertility problems, with psychological factors such as trauma history and posttraumatic stress disorder further exacerbating sympathetic nervous system overactivation and gastrointestinal dysfunction. Psychological interventions including acceptance and commitment therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy, gut-directed hypnotherapy, and hypnosis represent evidence-based approaches that reduce symptom severity, improve mood, and enhance quality of life, with pain acceptance itself associated with lower pain intensity during intercourse and improved sexual functioning.

title: Living with pain—a systematic review on patients’ subjective experiences
summary: This systematic review examines the subjective experiences of patients living with chronic pain and identifies critical interconnections between pain, psychological factors, and physiological complications. The analysis reveals a bidirectional relationship among anxiety, sleep deprivation, and acute pain, where anxiety and poor sleep quality reduce pain thresholds and increase pain sensitivity, perpetuating cycles of pain catastrophizing and avoidance behaviors. Untreated acute pain creates serious complications including deep vein thrombosis, muscle spasms, and anxiety, with even short-term pain capable of establishing long-lasting pain memories that transition to chronic pain. Chronic pain conditions manifest with distinct characteristics—fibromyalgia involves central sensitization and widespread musculoskeletal pain with comorbid fatigue and depression, while neuropathic pain and arthritis-related pain present with specific sensory and inflammatory features—yet all associate with depression, stress, poor concentration, and social isolation. Cancer-related and post-cancer pain further demonstrate how treatment and disease progression create persistent chronic pain with significant psychological sequelae in survivors. These findings underscore the importance of early pain intervention and integrated approaches addressing both pain mechanisms and psychological comorbidities to prevent the development of chronic pain conditions and associated complications.

title: Book of Posters
summary:
# Summary

This collection of pain research studies reveals multiple biological and psychosocial mechanisms underlying chronic pain conditions and their comorbidities. Amyloid-beta, traditionally associated with Alzheimer's disease, was found to modulate synaptic plasticity and sensory processing in the spinal dorsal horn, with intrathecal or hydrogel-delivered amyloid-beta transiently reducing neuropathic pain while altering microglia morphology. Gut microbiota composition emerges as a causal factor in fibromyalgia, where fecal microbiota transplantation from fibromyalgia patients induces pain hypersensitivity and reduced nerve fiber density in recipient mice, while transplantation from healthy controls reverses these effects. The research demonstrates that pain expression is highly modulated by social context and emotional states, with female mice showing opioid-mediated social approach during pain while males isolate, and amygdala kindling producing mechanical hypersensitivity alongside heightened emotional pain behaviors through anterior cingulate cortex mechanisms. Body perception disturbances appear common across diverse chronic pain conditions including complex regional pain syndrome, phantom limb pain, low back pain, and fibromyalgia, correlating with thermal pain sensitivity, depression, and pain catastrophizing. Collectively, these findings underscore pain as a multisystem disorder integrating peripheral mechanisms, central nervous system plasticity, microbiota-immune interactions, and psychological-emotional dimensions.

title: Association Between Preoperative Obstructive Sleep Apnea Diagnosis and Prolonged Postoperative Opioid Use Among Patients Undergoing Common Surgical Procedures
summary: This study of 270,320 surgical patients examined the relationship between preoperative obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) diagnosis and prolonged postoperative opioid use. While OSA features including intermittent hypoxemia and sleep fragmentation theoretically promote hyperalgesia and increased opioid consumption, the analysis found no statistically or clinically significant association between preoperative OSA diagnosis and prolonged postoperative opioid use after adjusting for comorbidities and surgical factors. However, the research identified important mechanistic pathways relevant to this population: insomnia, a condition highly comorbid with OSA, was associated with chronic opioid utilization, and patients with concurrent chronic pain and OSA were twice as likely to receive opioids compared to those without OSA. The study also highlights that postoperative opioid use may worsen sleep disorders, creating a potentially harmful feedback loop, and that OSA patients face elevated risk for opioid-induced respiratory depression. Preoperative positive airway pressure therapy may mitigate hyperalgesia by reducing sleep fragmentation and hypoxemia. These findings suggest that while OSA itself may not directly predict prolonged postoperative opioid use, comorbid conditions like insomnia and chronic pain warrant careful perioperative pain management in OSA patients undergoing surgery.

title: The presence of abnormal palpatory findings in the sacrococcygeal area is correlated with chronic pelvic pain: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that abnormal palpatory findings (APFs) in the sacrococcygeal region and pelvic floor are strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain and chronic pelvic pain syndrome (CPP/CPPS), with sacrococcygeal APFs showing an odds ratio of 3.02 and pelvic floor APFs showing an odds ratio of 2.99 in multivariate analysis. The research identifies that patients with CPP/CPPS frequently present with pelvic floor muscle dysfunction, myofascial dysfunctions involving trigger points in muscles such as the piriformis and iliopsoas, and tenderness in the suprapubic, abdominal, and intrapelvic regions. CPP/CPPS is characterized by multiple comorbidities including urinary, gastrointestinal, and sexual symptoms, with weak correlations observed between abnormal palpatory findings and psychosocial factors such as anxiety, depression, and fear-avoidance. The authors propose that multimodal physical treatments including patient education, biofeedback, manual therapy, and myofascial release may effectively address CPP/CPPS by targeting these identified pelvic floor dysfunctions and myofascial abnormalities. These findings suggest that sacrococcygeal and pelvic floor APFs serve as important clinical markers for identifying patients who would benefit from targeted manual therapy interventions.

title: Pediatric erythromelalgia from multidisciplinary perspectives: a scoping review
summary:
# Pediatric Erythromelalgia: A Multidisciplinary Scoping Review

Pediatric erythromelalgia is a rare chronic pain disorder characterized by recurrent episodes of burning sensation, erythema, and increased temperature primarily affecting the hands, feet, and ears. The condition is predominantly caused by gain-of-function mutations in the SCN9A gene encoding voltage-gated sodium channels, with several specific variants (F1449V, S241T, V400M, I848T, and L858F) accounting for the majority of inherited cases, while additional genetic variants in TRPV3, MEFV, and NMNAT2 genes account for secondary cases. Environmental and immunological triggers including infection, illness, physical activity, and vaccination can precipitate symptom onset, while symptomatic relief is achieved through cooling, rest, and elevation. The condition is frequently associated with significant neurological abnormalities including small fiber neuropathy, sudomotor dysfunction, abnormal quantitative sensory testing, and reduced intraepidermal nerve fiber density, alongside vascular changes involving increased blood flow and temperature dysregulation. Patients experience severe psychosocial comorbidities including anxiety, depression, suicidal ideation, and sleep disturbance, with substantially impaired quality of life and functional limitations. Management with medications such as carbamazepine can help modulate brain activity involved in pain processing and decision-making, though comprehensive multidisciplinary care addressing neurological, vascular, and psychological aspects is essential.

title: Reduced vagal tone in women with adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary: Reduced Vagal Tone in Women with Adenomyosis

Women with adenomyosis exhibit significantly reduced vagal tone characterized by sympatho-vagal imbalance and elevated sympathetic activity, measured by decreased heart rate variability parameters (RMSSD, pNN50, and HF) and increased LF/HF ratio compared to healthy controls. This autonomic dysfunction is mechanistically linked to increased lesional stiffness driven by fibrosis and inflammation, with reduced expression of the anti-inflammatory α7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor in adenomyotic lesions. Vagal dysfunction directly correlates with adenomyosis severity markers including dysmenorrhea intensity, menstrual blood loss, and uterine size, while chronic inflammation mediated by NF-κB activation and Th17-Treg imbalance further perpetuates tissue pathology through immune infiltration and fibrosis. Stress-responsive pathways including catecholamine activation of adenomyotic lesion-expressed β2 adrenergic receptors may accelerate disease progression, a mechanism potentially contributing to the observed association between depression and adenomyosis. These findings suggest that vagal nerve stimulation, including non-invasive transcutaneous auricular stimulation, represents a promising therapeutic strategy to restore autonomic balance, reduce inflammation through α7nAChR activation, and ameliorate adenomyosis-associated symptoms.

title: The Silent Cognitive Burden of Chronic Pain: Protocol for an AI-Enhanced Living Dose–Response Bayesian Meta-Analysis
summary: Chronic pain, particularly chronic back pain and pelvic pain, is associated with significant cognitive decline through multiple interconnected neurobiological mechanisms. Persistent nociceptive input triggers chronic neuroinflammation, activating microglia and astrocytes, which impairs the glymphatic system's ability to clear neurotoxic metabolic waste including amyloid-β and tau proteins—accumulation of these proteins accelerates neurodegenerative processes and cognitive deterioration. Chronic pain disrupts attentional networks and reduces cognitive resources available for executive functioning, while autonomic dysregulation from sustained pain increases sympathetic tone, impairing cerebral autoregulation and promoting microvascular damage that further contributes to cognitive decline. The cognitive burden extends from subjective cognitive impairment to measurable executive dysfunction and increased risk of mild cognitive impairment and dementia, particularly in middle-aged and older adults. Modifiable lifestyle factors including smoking cessation, physical activity, and proper sleep habits demonstrate protective effects against pain-related cognitive decline, while alcohol intake exacerbates it. This living Bayesian meta-analysis protocol aims to synthesize emerging evidence on the cognitive consequences of chronic pain and identify intervention strategies to mitigate this "silent" cognitive burden.

title: Fibromyalgia diagnosis from a multi-omics approach: a gut feeling
summary: This multi-omics study elucidates fibromyalgia as a complex disorder characterized by chronic widespread pain and fatigue, with pathogenesis rooted in interconnected gut-immune-mitochondrial dysfunction. Gut microbiota dysbiosis, marked by reduced bacterial diversity and altered abundance of specific taxa including Streptococcus salivarius, drives mitochondrial-derived oxidative stress and inflammation through increased intestinal permeability and dysregulated immune signaling. Deficiencies in antioxidant enzymes such as superoxide dismutase 1 and catalase lead to elevated reactive oxygen species and reduced total antioxidant capacity, which correlate directly with pain severity and fatigue. Dysregulated chemokines and cytokines activate both innate and adaptive immune responses, triggering neuroinflammatory cascades and central nervous system sensitization that amplify pain perception. Proteomic analysis identified 30 differentially expressed proteins and 19 dysbiotic microbial taxa associated with fibromyalgia, implicating blood coagulation, immune dysfunction, and extracellular matrix remodeling as key pathogenic mechanisms. These integrated molecular findings support a multifactorial model where intestinal dysbiosis initiates a cascade of oxidative stress, immune deregulation, and mitochondrial imbalance that converges on peripheral and central neuroinflammatory pathways underlying fibromyalgia's diverse clinical manifestations.

title: Depression and anxiety in patients with endometriosis-associated chronic pain: Neuroimmune mechanisms mediated by inflammatory factors
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by chronic local and systemic inflammation with elevated proinflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-1β, TNF-α, and CRP) that contribute to both chronic pelvic pain and associated psychiatric comorbidities. Approximately 42.5% of endometriosis patients experience clinically significant depression and 51% experience anxiety, with pain severity showing strong positive correlations with depression and anxiety scores as well as inflammatory marker levels. The neuroimmune mechanisms linking these conditions involve elevated inflammatory cytokines that cross the blood-brain barrier to promote depression and anxiety, combined with reduced brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), which is critical for neuronal plasticity, pain processing, and mood regulation. Additional pathways include dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis by chronic pain and inflammatory mediators, creating a complex interplay between pain sensitization and psychological symptoms. Multivariate analysis identified pain severity, IL-6, TNF-α, and BDNF as independent predictors of depression, while pain severity, IL-6, BDNF, and sleep quality predicted anxiety severity. These findings establish inflammatory cytokines, particularly IL-6, as key biomarkers linking endometriosis-associated pain to comorbid depression and anxiety through multiple neuroimmune pathways.

title: The Genetic Association Between Prescription Pain Medication Use and the Risk for Fibromyalgia: A Bidirectional and Multivariable Mendelian Randomization Analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates a robust causal relationship between prescription opioid use and fibromyalgia risk, with opioid use increasing fibromyalgia odds by approximately 45%. Chronic opioid use exacerbates fibromyalgia pathology by worsening hyperalgesia and central sensitization while simultaneously causing addiction, tolerance, allodynia, and dependence. Paradoxically, despite these adverse effects, opioids remain commonly used for fibromyalgia pain management, yet the FDA-approved treatments duloxetine, milnacipran, and pregabalin offer superior therapeutic profiles. The bidirectional analysis revealed no significant reverse causation, indicating that fibromyalgia does not causally lead to increased opioid prescription, and comorbid depression, anxiety, and insomnia do not mediate the opioid-fibromyalgia relationship. Patients with fibromyalgia face worse health outcomes than those with other chronic pain conditions, with common comorbidities including osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and lupus, alongside cognitive and emotional dysfunction. These findings underscore the clinical importance of avoiding prescription opioids in fibromyalgia management and instead prioritizing evidence-based alternatives to prevent iatrogenic worsening of pain phenotypes and opioid use disorder.

title: Comparison of sociodemographic factors, lifestyle, and gastrointestinal symptoms between patients with endometriosis and IBS
summary:
# Article Summary

Endometriosis and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) are highly associated chronic conditions that share overlapping pathophysiological mechanisms, including visceral hypersensitivity and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, and are both accompanied by significant psychiatric comorbidities such as depression and anxiety. Endometriosis presents primarily with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, while IBS causes more severe gastrointestinal symptoms including abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, and bloating—differences that guide distinct treatment approaches. Both conditions are associated with reduced physical activity and lower body mass index, and patients with these conditions require substantial sick leave, though endometriosis shows stronger association with former smoking while IBS associates with present smoking. Endometriosis patients are treated predominantly with hormonal therapies (combined estrogen-gestagen, gestagens, or GnRH analogs) and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, whereas IBS patients receive more gastrointestinal-targeted medications including proton pump inhibitors and laxatives. The conditions exhibit different triggers—menarche and menstruation initiate endometriosis symptoms, whereas stress, infection, and antibiotic treatment trigger IBS—yet both patient populations report symptom improvement following dietary modifications, highlighting the importance of personalized lifestyle and pharmaceutical management strategies.

title: Modeling Pain Without Injury: Inherited Rodent Models as Mechanistic Windows into Chronic Pain
summary: This article examines chronic pain through inherited rodent models as mechanistic tools, identifying convergent genetic and environmental pathways underlying pain chronification. Key genetic risk factors include IL6R and TNFRSF11A polymorphisms that amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine signaling (IL-6, TNF-α), plus sensory neuron excitability genes like SCN9A and TRPA1. The neuroimmune axis emerges as central, with microglial activation via CX3CR1-mediated neuron-glia communication driving central sensitization and pain amplification through pro-inflammatory mediator release. Critical modulatory pathways involve GCH1-synthesized tetrahydrobiopterin and COMT-mediated catecholamine metabolism, which regulate dopamine and nitric oxide-dependent pain signaling, while FKBP5 variants confer threefold increased chronic pain risk following trauma through glucocorticoid receptor dysfunction and sustained neuroinflammation. A striking finding is transgenerational neuroimmune priming, wherein early-life or ancestral trauma induces epigenetic modifications that persistently alter gene expression in stress and immune-related pathways, with microglia serving as central mediators of this inherited vulnerability. The work demonstrates that chronic pain comorbidities with anxiety, depression, and cardiovascular dysfunction share common genetic architecture, particularly in IL6R and TNFRSF11A, suggesting coordinated neuroimmune and oxidative stress mechanisms underlying multifactorial pain pathogenesis.

title: Lipedema and Hypermobility Spectrum Disorders Sharing Pathophysiology: A Cross-Sectional Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study reveals significant pathophysiological overlap between lipedema and hypermobility spectrum disorders (HSDs), with connective tissue dysfunction serving as a unifying mechanism. Lipedema, characterized by abnormal subcutaneous fat accumulation, inflammation, and fibrosis, shows high comorbidity with joint hypermobility, affecting 44% of lipedema patients in adulthood and 60% during childhood. The underlying pathology involves connective tissue laxity that compromises blood and lymphatic vessel structure, leading to increased vascular permeability, fluid leakage, capillary dysfunction, and subsequent edema and interstitial fibrosis. Both conditions share multisystemic manifestations including chronic pain, fatigue, gastrointestinal dysfunction, and autonomic disorders, with lipedema patients additionally reporting high rates of thyroid disorders, allergies, polycystic ovary syndrome, and mood disorders. Estrogen dysregulation, defective lymphatic vascularization, and altered microRNA expression contribute to the adipose tissue proliferation and metabolic dysfunction observed in lipedema. The authors conclude that recognizing the shared connective tissue pathophysiology between lipedema and HSDs is essential for comprehensive diagnosis and management of these frequently overlooked, comorbid chronic conditions.

title: Joint Hypermobility: An Under-Recognised Cause of Palpitations, Dizziness, and Syncope in Young Females
summary:
# Summary

Joint hypermobility (JH) syndromes present with a diverse constellation of cardiovascular and systemic symptoms that are frequently underrecognized in young female patients. The primary cardiovascular manifestations include dizziness upon standing (77.1%), palpitations, and syncope, often accompanied by orthostatic intolerance and gastrointestinal dysmotility. Tilt-table testing reveals abnormalities in 82% of JH patients, with initial orthostatic hypotension being the most common finding, followed by various presentations of reflex syncope and postural tachycardia syndrome (POTS). Beyond cardiovascular symptoms, JH syndromes commonly present with chronic pain, migraine, chronic fatigue, and significant psychiatric comorbidities affecting nearly 68% of patients, including depression, anxiety, and neurodevelopmental conditions. The underlying mechanisms likely involve excessive blood pooling in hyperelastic vessels, dysautonomia, small fiber peripheral neuropathy, and mast cell activation. Conservative management through hydration, salt intake, and exercise is effective in over half of cases, while pharmacological interventions such as ivabradine and fludrocortisone are reserved for refractory presentations.

title: Cross-cultural validation of the Chinese version of Disorder-Specific Intolerance of Uncertainty (DSIU): psychometric properties in female college students with premenstrual syndrome (PMS)
summary: Premenstrual syndrome involves cyclical fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone that regulate GABAergic neurotransmission and impair prefrontal cortex function, reducing cortical inhibitory tone and compromising emotional regulation. Individuals with high intolerance of uncertainty during the luteal phase display heightened hypervigilance and attentional bias toward threatening information, which intensifies anxiety and depression through maladaptive coping strategies. Intolerance of uncertainty operates as a transdiagnostic factor across multiple psychiatric conditions including generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, and obsessive-compulsive disorder, with disease-specific manifestations being particularly relevant for premenstrual symptom severity. The Chinese Disorder-Specific Intolerance of Uncertainty (C-DSIU) scale demonstrates robust psychometric validity in assessing PMS-related uncertainty intolerance in female college students, providing a culturally adapted tool that captures anxiety vulnerabilities specific to menstrual cycle pathophysiology. These findings suggest that targeting intolerance of uncertainty may offer psychological intervention pathways for managing the cognitive and emotional comorbidities associated with premenstrual dysphoric disorder and severe premenstrual syndrome.

title: The threat of an embodied virtual belly affects gastric feelings depending on somatic, interoceptive and alexithymic characteristics of healthy women
summary:
# Summary

This study used virtual reality to demonstrate that gastric interoception—the perception of stomach sensations—is modulated by both psychological and individual factors in healthy women. Through synchronous visuo-tactile stimulation, researchers induced a sense of embodiment with a virtual avatar while manipulating threat levels, finding that both embodiment and threat significantly increased the intensity and unpleasantness of gastric feelings. State anxiety and the sense of embodiment emerged as key mediators of these effects, with threatening conditions (a virtual syringe threat) enhancing the visceral quality of gastric sensations. Individual differences in somatic sensitivity, interoceptive concern, and alexithymia—difficulty recognizing and describing emotions—strongly predicted the magnitude of these perceptual changes, indicating that gastric interoception is profoundly shaped by psychological context and affective traits rather than being purely physiological. The findings highlight the gut-brain axis as a bidirectional system where conscious interoceptive processing involves the insula, prefrontal cortex, and striatum, demonstrating that virtual embodiment can effectively manipulate gastric perception through multisensory integration and anxiety pathways.

title: Gray matter alterations and pain-related emotional processing in patients with adenomyosis-associated dysmenorrhea: a neuroimaging perspective
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary: Adenomyosis causes dysmenorrhea through inflammatory and neurogenic pain mediators, which drives both chronic pain and psychological comorbidities including anxiety and depression. Patients with adenomyosis-associated dysmenorrhea exhibit significant gray matter volume alterations across multiple brain regions involved in pain processing and emotional regulation, including reduced gray matter in the right fusiform gyrus, right parahippocampal gyrus, and left superior frontal gyrus, alongside increased volume in the right lingual gyrus and bilateral thalamus. These structural brain changes correlate strongly with pain severity and psychological symptoms, suggesting that repeated pain episodes induce neuroplastic changes in emotion-processing and pain-modulation circuits. The reduced gray matter in the right fusiform and parahippocampal gyri—regions critical for emotional processing, memory consolidation, and limbic regulation—likely reflects impaired emotional regulation in response to chronic cyclic pain. The thalamus and superior frontal gyrus, which modulate pain through GABAergic and dopaminergic signaling, appear particularly susceptible to structural alterations in this condition. These findings provide neuroimaging evidence that adenomyosis-associated dysmenorrhea involves central sensitization mechanisms that fundamentally reorganize pain and emotion-processing brain networks.

title: Causal associations between sleep disorders and endometriosis: A Mendelian randomization analysis
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study establishes a robust causal relationship between insomnia and endometriosis risk, with insomnia increasing endometriosis odds by approximately twofold, whereas other sleep traits including chronotype, sleep duration, and daytime napping show no significant causal effects. Endometriosis manifests as a complex condition characterized by chronic pelvic pain (dysmenorrhea and deep dyspareunia), poor sleep quality, fatigue, and psychological distress including depression and anxiety, with endometriosis patients experiencing chronic pain being 2.8 times more likely to develop insomnia. The pathophysiological mechanisms involve a self-perpetuating cycle wherein ectopic endometrial tissue produces inflammatory mediators that activate nociceptive pathways, triggering central sensitization and hyperarousal that activate the hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axis, elevate cortisol, and disrupt sleep, while insomnia paradoxically amplifies inflammation and further perpetuates pain sensitivity and endometrial lesion growth. Additionally, hormonal dysregulation characteristic of endometriosis—particularly estrogen dominance and progesterone resistance—disrupts melatonin-estrogen signaling and clock-related gene expression, contributing to sleep disruption and endometrial cell proliferation. These findings suggest insomnia as a modifiable risk factor and therapeutic target for endometriosis management.

title: The Implementation of Persistent Spinal Pain Syndrome (PSPS): Mechanism‐Based Recommendations
summary:
# Summary

Persistent spinal pain syndrome (PSPS) is a complex chronic condition most commonly manifesting as chronic low back pain with or without lower limb symptoms, encompassing multiple pain mechanisms including nociceptive, neuropathic, and nociplastic pain. The condition is divided into two types: PSPS-T1, which involves chronic axial or radicular pain in patients without prior spinal surgery, and PSPS-T2, which develops despite or as a consequence of spine surgery and affects one in five post-operative patients. Because over fifty percent of orthopedic patients exhibit mixed pain phenotypes with contributions from degenerative changes, neuropathic lesions, and central sensitization, treatment approaches must address multiple underlying mechanisms. Spinal cord stimulation has emerged as superior to both comprehensive medical management and revision surgery, while mechanism-specific interventions such as targeted nerve root injections, surgical decompression with microdiscectomy for recent-onset radiculopathy, and novel approaches like multifidus nerve stimulation show promise for appropriate patient subgroups. Conservative treatments including physical therapy and medication remain foundational first-line options, though advanced interventions become necessary when these approaches fail to provide adequate pain relief.

title: Stress, pain intensity, and work productivity in individuals with chronic pain: a cross-sectional observational study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that chronic pain substantially impacts work productivity through interconnected biological and psychological pathways. Pain intensity and stress together explain approximately 19% of the variance in work productivity loss, with both factors independently reducing work capacity and increasing absenteeism. The study reveals a complex web of comorbidities wherein chronic pain associates with depression, anxiety, and insomnia, each of which further diminishes physical activity, sleep quality, and occupational functioning. Notably, insomnia emerges as a central mediator between stress and anxiety while also showing moderate-to-large correlations with pain intensity and psychological distress. Protective factors include high-intensity physical activity and Mediterranean diet adherence, which inversely associate with depression and improve sleep quality, whereas modifiable risk factors such as excessive smartphone use and sedentary work correlate with worsened mental health and pain chronification. These findings highlight the need for multifaceted interventions addressing pain, sleep, stress, physical activity, and nutrition to effectively restore work productivity in individuals with chronic pain.

title: Primary dysmenorrhea among female medical students at Haramaya university: a cross sectional study in Harar, Eastern Ethiopia
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study among female medical students in Ethiopia identifies multiple risk factors and clinical manifestations of primary dysmenorrhea, the most prevalent menstrual disorder in the population studied. The underlying pathophysiology involves excessive prostaglandin production at ovulation, which triggers uterine vasoconstriction, decreased blood flow, muscle ischemia, and increased pain receptor sensitivity, ultimately causing pelvic pain. Significant independent risk factors include family history of dysmenorrhea (2.57-fold increased odds), menstrual irregularity (1.70-fold), academic stress (2.39-fold), and consumption of more than three cups of coffee daily (3.25-fold). Beyond the primary pain symptoms, dysmenorrhea causes systemic manifestations including back pain, mood changes, headache, gastrointestinal distress, and autonomic symptoms, while substantially impairing academic performance through concentration loss and school absenteeism. Notably, dysmenorrhea frequently co-occurs with other chronic pain syndromes later in life, including irritable bowel syndrome, fibromyalgia, and chronic headache, suggesting a common underlying pain pathway or predisposition, and should be distinguished from secondary dysmenorrhea caused by structural pathology such as endometriosis, adenomyosis, and uterine fibroids.

title: A cross-sectional analysis of brain structure, pain behaviors, and mental health in persons with surgically confirmed endometriosis
summary: This cross-sectional study reveals that surgically confirmed endometriosis is associated with distinctive alterations in brain structure and pain-related psychological dysfunction beyond what would be expected from pain alone. Individuals with endometriosis demonstrate reduced gray matter volume in multiple pain-processing regions, including the thalamus, cingulate cortex, putamen, and insula, alongside decreased cortical thickness in the superior frontal gyrus that worsens with advancing age. These neuromorphological changes correlate with elevated pain catastrophizing, anxiety, and interference, which themselves amplify pain perception through hypervigilance and enhanced sensitivity. Notably, endometriosis stage does not correlate with pain severity, suggesting that brain structural modifications and maladaptive pain cognitions may be more important determinants of clinical symptoms than lesion burden alone. The study identifies functional connectivity disruptions in the anterior insula as a potential mechanism linking endometriosis to pain amplification. Clustering analyses revealed heterogeneous psychological presentations within the endometriosis population, with one subgroup exhibiting high depression and anxiety despite equivalent pain reporting, highlighting individual variability in central sensitization and psychological responses to chronic pelvic pain.

title: Brain Sensory Network Activity Underlies Reduced Nociceptive-Initiated and Nociplastic Pain via Acupuncture in Fibromyalgia
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that electroacupuncture treats fibromyalgia pain through distinct neurobiological mechanisms compared to sham treatment. Electroacupuncture reduces widespread nociplastic pain and increases pressure-pain tolerance by activating a "bottom-up" pathway: peripheral needle stimulation activates mechanoreceptors and afferent somatosensory pathways, leading to increased primary somatosensory cortex (S1) activation, which strengthens connectivity between S1 and the anterior insula—a region integrating sensory, emotional, and cognitive aspects of pain. Mediation analyses revealed that this increased S1-anterior insula connectivity directly correlates with reduced widespread pain, with enhanced GABAergic signaling supporting this analgesia. In contrast, sham acupuncture engages a "top-down" mechanism involving precuneus activation, reflecting introspective and self-referential processing. Electroacupuncture also modulates multiple pain-related neurotransmitter systems including endogenous opioids, endocannabinoids, serotonin, dopamine, and GABA, further contributing to pain relief. These findings elucidate how electroacupuncture targets central sensitization in fibromyalgia through enhanced somatosensory brain network activity.

title: The causal role of multiple negative emotions in chronic respiratory diseases: A two-sample Mendelian randomized study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates a causal relationship between negative emotions and chronic respiratory diseases, particularly asthma and sleep apnea syndrome. Specific emotional traits including neuroticism, mood swings, nervousness, and worry were each independently associated with increased risk of asthma, with mood swings showing the strongest effect (OR = 2.08). Similarly, mood swings emerged as the most robust causal factor for both COPD and sleep apnea syndrome across multiple analytical approaches, while other emotional states like feeling miserable and guilty showed weaker associations that did not persist in multivariable analysis. Mechanistically, prolonged negative emotions disrupt autonomic nervous system homeostasis, triggering dysregulated hormonal and immune responses that drive inflammatory processes and exacerbate Th2/Th17 cytokine production, with IL-5 and IL-17 levels correlating with depression and anxiety severity. Reverse causality analyses confirmed that respiratory diseases do not reciprocally cause negative emotions, establishing the directional causal pathway from emotions to respiratory disease. These findings suggest that emotional regulation may represent an important therapeutic target for managing chronic respiratory disease risk and progression.

title: Use of oral neuromodulators in chronic pelvic painNumber 8 – 2025
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: Chronic pelvic pain involves both neuropathic and nociplastic components, with the latter arising from central sensitization in the central nervous system and associated with mood changes, memory impairment, sleep disturbances, and fatigue. Oral neuromodulators provide targeted pharmacological treatment based on pain phenotype: gabapentinoids (particularly pregabalin) and tricyclic antidepressants (especially nortriptyline and amitriptyline) effectively treat neuropathic pain, while serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors like duloxetine are most effective for nociplastic pain and demonstrate FDA approval with superior efficacy. NSAIDs are ineffective for neuropathic pain and may paradoxically increase pain perception, whereas selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors show inferior efficacy compared to other antidepressant classes. Pregabalin offers advantages including linear pharmacokinetics and FDA approval for multiple chronic pain conditions with demonstrated quality-of-life improvements, though gabapentinoids and antidepressants carry distinct adverse effect profiles ranging from sedation and weight gain to sexual dysfunction. Emerging evidence suggests cannabinoids may have analgesic potential through endocannabinoid system modulation, though clinical trial results for chronic pelvic pain remain mixed.

title: Persistent sleep symptoms in post-COVID syndrome: a Brazilian prospective clinical study
summary:
# Sleep Disorders and Cognitive Dysfunction in Post-COVID Syndrome

This Brazilian prospective study demonstrates that COVID-19 infection causes persistent sleep disorders in post-COVID syndrome, with approximately 23% of patients experiencing insomnia one year after infection and 5% developing central hypersomnia. The underlying mechanism involves SARS-CoV-2 binding to angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 receptors and neuropilin-1 in the brainstem, leading to damage of sleep-regulating nuclei (periaqueductal gray matter, dorsal raphe nucleus, pedunculopontine nucleus, and laterodorsal tegmental nucleus) and causing CNS encephalitis and hypothalamic impairment. Sleep disruption in post-COVID syndrome triggers a cascade of neurobiological damage, including microglial activation that reduces oligodendrocytes and inhibits hippocampal neurogenesis, impairs glymphatic clearance resulting in amyloid-beta and tau accumulation, and reduces hippocampal cell proliferation. Patients with persistent insomnia show significantly higher cognitive complaints and subjective cognitive decline, as sleep deprivation reduces attention and short-term memory performance while promoting white matter microstructure changes. The study identifies critical links between sleep architecture disruption and long-term neuroinflammation-mediated neuronal dysfunction, suggesting that persistent sleep symptoms represent a harbinger of sustained central nervous system damage in post-COVID syndrome. Wakefulness-promoting agents provided symptomatic relief for some patients with central hypersomnia, though the study emphasizes the need for targeted interventions addressing the underlying neurobiological mechanisms.

title: Is canonical SD a clinically relevant model of migraine? Argument con
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary:
# Summary

Cortical spreading depolarization (CSD), a wave of neuronal depolarization characterized by loss of ionic gradients, membrane potential collapse, and cessation of synaptic activity, is widely hypothesized to underlie migraine aura. CSD induces multiple pathological cascades including intracellular edema, blood-brain barrier disruption, and activation of meningeal nociceptors, which subsequently activate trigeminal ganglion neurons and brainstem pain pathways implicated in migraine headache generation. However, this article argues against canonical SD as a clinically relevant migraine model, highlighting a critical mechanistic inconsistency: CSD-induced neuronal silencing cannot account for the predominantly positive sensory symptoms (scintillations, zig-zag lines, tingling) observed in migraine aura, since silencing would predict negative symptoms. Additionally, clinical evidence reveals important discordances with the unilateral CSD model, including bilateral DC shifts observed in magnetoencephalography studies and the occurrence of aura and headache symptoms in different brain hemispheres in 40–50% of patients. These clinical observations suggest that canonical SD, while demonstrating the expected cellular and vascular effects in experimental models, may not fully explain the phenomenology and lateralization patterns of human migraine aura.

title: Perceived cognitive functioning difficulties in individuals living with endometriosis
summary: Individuals with endometriosis experience substantial cognitive functioning difficulties, with 80% of study participants demonstrating cognitive impairments assessed through standardized measures. Pain, fatigue, and depressive symptoms—all common sequelae of endometriosis—independently and synergistically impair attention, memory, and decision-making by diminishing attentional control and disrupting information processing. Sleep disturbances associated with endometriosis further contribute to cognitive decline by promoting physical fatigue and mild cognitive impairment, while preliminary neuroimaging studies suggest structural brain changes in "endometriosis brain." Treatment modalities present a therapeutic dilemma: although hormonal treatments and pain medications reduce depression-related cognitive effects, they paradoxically exacerbate pain-related cognitive impairment and carry their own cognitive side effects, including memory loss and concentration difficulties. Risk factors for worse cognitive outcomes include older age, greater disease severity, lower education level, unemployment, and use of hormonal or pain medications, highlighting the multifactorial nature of cognitive dysfunction in endometriosis.

title: Persistent pelvic pain in women: unraveling diagnostic challenges and the overlooked role of pelvic congestion syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Persistent pelvic pain in women arises from multiple overlapping etiologies that are often diagnostically challenging, with pelvic congestion syndrome—characterized by dilated venous complexes and impaired drainage—emerging as a significant but frequently overlooked vascular contributor alongside the better-recognized endometriosis and post-surgical adhesions. Mechanical, hormonal, neuropathic, and psychological factors all contribute to pain generation, including pudendal nerve entrapment, pelvic floor hypertonicity, elevated estrogen-driven venous dilation, and stress amplification of pain perception, while comorbid conditions such as interstitial cystitis, irritable bowel syndrome, and central sensitization further complicate diagnosis. Pregnancy and multiparity increase pelvic congestion risk through venous stasis and reflux mechanisms, whereas trauma history and psychological disorders including depression and anxiety exacerbate symptoms. Effective management requires interdisciplinary approaches combining pharmacological interventions such as tricyclic antidepressants, physical therapy, psychological support, lifestyle modifications, and when warranted, interventional procedures like ovarian vein embolization, which collectively improve pain outcomes and quality of life more effectively than isolated specialty approaches.

title: Invisible Pain, Visible Inequalities: Gender, Social Agency, and the Health of Women with Fibromyalgia
summary:
# Summary

Fibromyalgia is a chronic condition predominantly affecting women, characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, cognitive impairment, and central nervous system hypersensitivity, frequently accompanied by anxiety and depression. Psychological factors including fear of movement, catastrophizing, low self-efficacy, and feelings of helplessness intensify the condition's impact, while symptom unpredictability, persistent pain, and daily functional limitations generate chronic stress that is further amplified by economic precarity and workplace discrimination. Beyond individual suffering, fibromyalgia disrupts family and social life, with family dynamics, partnership quality, and perceived loneliness significantly influencing symptom severity and mental health outcomes. Critical social determinants emerged as major predictors: satisfaction with social life and perceived family support were associated with reduced symptom burden, while perceived discrimination and skepticism from healthcare providers—rooted in gender biases and the condition's invisibility within biomedical paradigms—linked to poorer mental health and increased isolation. Notably, involvement in fibromyalgia-related organizations and peer support functioned as protective factors, promoting emotional validation and improved symptom perception and mental health. These findings underscore that fibromyalgia outcomes depend not only on biological mechanisms but fundamentally on gender-sensitive healthcare, social recognition, and equitable access to supportive community networks.

title: Cannabinoids for Anxiety and Sleep Disturbances: A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

Anxiety disorders and sleep disturbances are highly interconnected conditions that commonly co-occur, with approximately 60-80% of individuals with anxiety experiencing concurrent sleep problems. These conditions share underlying physiological mechanisms involving cortisol dysregulation, executive function impairment, and dysfunction in the serotonin and dopamine pathways that regulate both sleep-wake cycles and affective states. Cannabinoid compounds, particularly cannabidiol (CBD) and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), demonstrate significant efficacy in alleviating both conditions through multiple mechanisms: CBD inhibits the adenosine transporter to increase adenosine signaling, which reduces anxiety and promotes sleep, while both compounds modulate serotonin and dopamine pathways. Clinical evidence from multiple studies shows substantial improvements, with anxiety symptoms reduced by up to 79.9% and sleep quality improved by approximately 36% following cannabinoid treatment, including benefits for generalized anxiety disorder and insomnia. These findings suggest that cannabinoid-based therapies represent a promising therapeutic approach for managing the comorbid presentation of anxiety and sleep disturbances.

title: Irritable bowel syndrome and chronic pelvic pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates a substantial co-occurrence between irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and chronic pelvic pain (CPP), with IBS diagnosed in up to 35% of women experiencing CPP. Multiple clinical, psychological, and inflammatory factors associate with both conditions, including endometriosis, dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, anxiety, and depression, suggesting shared pathophysiological mechanisms. Gastrointestinal symptoms such as constipation and diarrhea, along with widespread pain conditions including fibromyalgia, migraine, and back pain, are strongly linked to CPP, while inflammatory markers including high-sensitivity C-reactive protein and tumor necrosis factor-alpha are elevated in affected patients. The analysis identified novel associations with IBS in the CPP population, notably pelvic floor tension myalgia and piriformis syndrome, and revealed that patients with overlapping IBS and CPP exhibit significantly higher somatization scores, depression, dizziness, and fatigue. History of physical or sexual abuse, along with trauma-related psychosocial factors, emerged as important contributors to this comorbidity complex, highlighting the multifactorial nature of these interrelated conditions.

title: Sources and consequences of self-doubt in patients with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: a qualitative study in Europe and North America
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study reveals that patients with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, particularly those with hypermobile and molecularly defined types, experience profound self-doubt driven primarily by clinician skepticism and dismissal of their pain reports. Clinician doubt—often amplified by biases including misogyny and ageism, as well as the chronic and atypical presentation of EDS pain—becomes internalized through repeated questioning and invalidation, leading patients to distrust their own sensations, pain severity assessments, and bodily perceptions. The consequences are multifaceted and severe: self-doubt produces psychological harms including dissociation and fear of malingering, undermines decision-making and self-efficacy, and creates dangerous medical harms by causing patients to hesitate seeking care, underestimate symptom severity, and in the case of vascular EDS patients, fail to recognize life-threatening events. This pattern represents a form of testimonial injustice that disproportionately affects women and disabled individuals, with particularly lasting impacts when doubt is encountered during childhood and teenage years. The study emphasizes that clinician skepticism inadvertently perpetuates a cycle of symptom invalidation, diagnostic delay, and disability that restricts patients' educational and career opportunities while compounding existing social disparities.

title: Diagnosis, Prognosis, and Drug Target Discovery for Chronic Widespread Pain: A Large Proteogenomic Study
summary: This large proteogenomic study identified distinct protein signatures distinguishing chronic widespread pain from acute pain and pain-free controls. Chronic pain patients exhibited substantial upregulation of immune and inflammatory proteins including CDCP1, IL18R1, GDF15, CCL7, and CHGA, alongside downregulation of protective proteins such as CA14, PON3, SELENOP, and EGFR. Pathway analyses revealed that widespread chronic pain associates with immune activation, leukocyte migration, cytokine-cytokine receptor interactions, and IL-10 and TNF signaling dysregulation. Two proteomic scoring models—the comprehensive C-ProtS and simpler S-ProtS—effectively predicted chronic pain phenotypes and pain mechanisms, demonstrating strong associations with fibromyalgia, nociplastic pain, and pain spread progression. Mendelian randomization identified 18 causally implicated proteins in widespread pain, with nine proteins including TNF, CA14, COL9A1, and IFI30 specifically linked to nociplastic pain mechanisms. These findings establish a robust proteomic signature for chronic pain diagnosis and prognosis while revealing immune dysregulation as a central biological mechanism driving widespread chronic pain conditions.

title: Pain Research and Treatment: Policy Implications
journal: Policy insights from the behavioral and brain sciences
summary:
# Summary

This policy-focused article examines chronic pain as a complex biopsychosocial disease with significant implications for clinical practice, research methodology, and healthcare policy. Chronic pain is strongly associated with psychiatric comorbidities including depression and anxiety, as well as elevated biochemical markers such as quinolinic acid, pyroglutamate, xanthurenic acid, and acrolein. Current pain management relies on multiple therapeutic approaches—opioids remain the standard for moderate to severe pain despite addiction risks, while cannabis, psychedelic medicines, complementary therapies, and combinations of pharmacological agents offer alternative or adjunctive options. The article emphasizes emerging technological advances including machine learning, deep learning, computer vision, and artificial intelligence for improved pain diagnosis and assessment across diverse animal models and clinical populations. Key barriers to optimal pain treatment include insufficient integration of mechanistic biomarkers in clinical trials, placebo effect inflation in analgesic trials, insurance barriers such as high-deductible health plans, and inadequate attention to sex as a biological variable in pain research. The article advocates for pragmatic clinical trials, real-world data integration, opioid stewardship programs, and personalized treatment approaches using prognostic profiling to enhance pain management outcomes and reduce opioid-related harms.

title: The pathophysiologic mechanisms of spinal manipulative therapy in the management of chronic musculoskeletal pain
journal: The Journal of the Canadian Chiropractic Association
summary: Chronic musculoskeletal pain represents a major cause of disability worldwide, with rising prevalence driven by aging populations. Central sensitization, a pathophysiological mechanism characterized by increased sensory, sympathetic, and motor excitability, underlies many chronic pain conditions. Spinal manipulative therapy appears to exert analgesic effects by downregulating central sensitization, thereby reducing pain signaling in susceptible patients. This mechanism offers a neurobiological explanation for the therapeutic benefits observed with spinal manipulation in chronic musculoskeletal pain management. The evidence suggests that modulation of central sensitization pathways may be a key process through which manual therapies achieve clinical pain relief.

title: Chronic pain: prevalence, demographic inequalities, and healthcare utilisation. A primary care database analysis
summary: This primary care database analysis reveals that chronic pain affects approximately 13.6% of males and 24% of females, with significant demographic disparities. Age is the strongest predictor of chronic pain, with individuals aged 60-69 years approximately nine times more likely to experience it than younger populations. Chronic pain encompasses diverse conditions, including secondary pain from underlying diseases such as osteoarthritis, endometriosis, and cancer, as well as primary pain conditions with no clear etiology including fibromyalgia, chronic daily headache, and irritable bowel syndrome. Notable demographic inequalities exist, with substantially higher prevalence in Black ethnicity patients (27.2%) compared to White patients (17.5%), and highest rates in Black Caribbean populations (32.4%). The study identifies strong associations between chronic pain and mental health disorders, with anxiety being particularly prevalent in the study population at 21.4%, suggesting bidirectional relationships between pain and psychological wellbeing. Social deprivation and non-UK country of birth were also associated with increased chronic pain prevalence, underscoring the multifactorial nature of this condition and highlighting healthcare disparities in access and outcomes.

title: Clinical Signs at Diagnosis and Comorbidities in a Large Cohort of Patients with Lipedema in Spain
summary:
# Summary

Lipedema is a chronic disorder predominantly affecting women, characterized by bilateral symmetrical subcutaneous fat accumulation with nodular and fibrotic appearance, most frequently affecting the buttocks, hips, and lower limbs, with upper limb involvement in approximately 30% of cases. The condition is strongly associated with genetic factors, including variants in AKR1C1 that impact progesterone metabolism and loci related to adipose and vascular biology (VEGFA, GRB14–COBLL1). Clinical manifestations include widespread pain (affecting 87.9% of patients), extensive connective tissue alterations, and high intestinal permeability (99%), alongside endocrine dysfunction including inflammatory ovarian dysfunction and thyroid disease (present in 76% and 59.5% of patients respectively). Beyond physical symptoms, lipedema patients experience substantial psychological burden, including anxiety, low self-esteem, poor mental well-being, and social stigmatization, with nearly 67% reporting psychological impairment. Notable comorbidities include ligamentous hyperlaxity (95.8%), bilateral trochanteric pain (97.4%), and iliotibial band involvement (96.6%), reflecting a systemic connective tissue disorder. Body weight significantly correlates with lipedema extent and severity, suggesting metabolic and mechanical contributions to disease progression.

title: Microglial Activation in Nociplastic Pain: From Preclinical Models to PET Neuroimaging and Implications for Targeted Therapeutic Strategies
summary:
# Summary

Nociplastic pain, characterized by altered nociception without clear tissue damage, represents a third category of chronic pain involving neuroinflammation and microglial activation as core pathophysiological mechanisms alongside central sensitization. This condition encompasses diverse disorders including fibromyalgia (the prototypic disease), migraines, complex regional pain syndrome, and visceral pain syndromes such as endometriosis, irritable bowel syndrome, and interstitial cystitis, each frequently presenting with comorbidities including sleep disorders, mood disturbances, and cognitive impairment. Neuroinflammatory cascades involve microglial-derived cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, IL-1β) and neurotransmitters (elevated glutamate and substance P, reduced GABA), with microglial activation triggered by extracellular ATP, chemokines, and neuron-derived signals through purinergic and chemokine receptors. Preclinical evidence demonstrates that pharmacological or genetic inhibition of microglial activation alleviates pain symptoms including hyperalgesia and allodynia, supporting microglia as a viable therapeutic target. Positron emission tomography neuroimaging with selective translocator protein radiopharmaceuticals can identify microglial activation in patient populations, enabling personalized therapeutic strategies directed at microglial signaling pathways and neuroinflammatory mediators to combat pain chronification.

title: Identifying and characterizing clinical subgroups in individuals with endometriosis
summary: This study identifies distinct clinical subgroups in endometriosis patients using a biopsychosocial framework, revealing that endometriosis is strongly associated with chronic pain, anxiety, depression, and pain catastrophizing, with over half of patients reporting clinically significant psychological strain. The research demonstrates a critical distinction between two classes of patients: a low biopsychosocial burden group (Class 1) with moderate pain and minimal psychological comorbidities, and a high biopsychosocial burden group (Class 2) characterized by severe pain intensity (7.1 vs. 3.8 on pain scales), widespread pain distribution (11.0 vs. 8.2 pain locations), and significantly elevated psychological symptoms including anxiety, depression, and pain catastrophizing. In the high-burden group, pain catastrophizing emerges as a key moderating factor linking pain severity to depression and reduced quality of life, while anxiety amplifies pain perception, creating a bidirectional relationship between psychological and physical symptoms. Central sensitization and nociplastic pain mechanisms appear involved in pain spreading, particularly in patients with more extensive and prolonged symptoms. The findings suggest that integrating biopsychosocial assessment principles into clinical care can identify patients requiring multidisciplinary interventions, such as cognitive behavioral therapy, to address both pain and psychological comorbidities and improve quality of life outcomes.

title: Minimally important difference and responsiveness to change for numerical rating scale of menstrual pain severity: a psychometric study
summary: This psychometric study establishes the measurement properties of the numerical rating scale (NRS) for assessing menstrual pain severity in reproductive-age women. Menstrual pain affects 45–95% of this population and is associated with significant functional impairment, including impaired sleep and decreased physical activity, while dysmenorrhea is linked to increased risk of developing other chronic pain conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome, fibromyalgia, and non-cyclic chronic pelvic pain. The researchers determined that the minimally important difference (MID) for NRS ranges from approximately 0.85 to 1.02 points depending on whether worst, least, average, or current pain is measured, with endometriosis patients potentially requiring a 4-point threshold for clinical significance. The 0–10 NRS demonstrated superior responsiveness compared to visual analogue and verbal rating scales, with area-under-the-curve estimates of 0.66–0.70 and moderate to strong correlations between NRS change scores and both the Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder and Hand scale and retrospective global ratings of change. These findings provide clinically relevant benchmarks for evaluating treatment efficacy in menstrual pain research and clinical practice.

title: Role of Gynecologic Findings in Interstitial Cystitis/Bladder Pain Syndrome: A Consensus
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome (IC/BPS) frequently coexist as comorbid conditions associated with complex pelvic pain mechanisms. Endometriotic lesions found throughout the pelvis, including in the bladder, contribute to pain through inflammatory mediators and neuropathic pathways, with patients exhibiting increased bladder capacity and elevated rates of concurrent chronic conditions such as fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, and migraine.
