Mast cell activation represents a unifying pathogenic mechanism across multiple pelvic pain disorders, triggering histamine release that generates hyperalgesia and neuropathic pain in endometriosis, vulvodynia, and irritable bowel syndrome. IC/BPS patients experience substantial sexual dysfunction affecting 38-83% depending on the domain, with comorbid endometriosis intensifying dyspareunia and postcoital pain, while pelvic floor muscle dysfunction and overactivity—present in up to 85% of IC/BPS patients—perpetuate pain cycles. Psychosocial factors including past sexual abuse, childhood trauma, and elevated lifetime stress correlate with IC/BPS development and severity, suggesting that gynecologic endometriosis evaluation should be integrated into comprehensive IC/BPS assessment to optimize treatment strategies.

title: Bingeing as an ADHD-related strategy: a qualitative study of experiences of Neurodivergent and potentially Neurodivergent adults with bulimic-spectrum eating disorders
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study reveals that ADHD and autism are significantly overrepresented in adults with bulimic-spectrum eating disorders and operate through distinct neurobiological mechanisms. ADHD-related factors including impulsivity, emotion regulation difficulties, and reward sensitivity drive binge eating, while interoceptive differences, sensory-seeking behaviors, and hyper-focusing contribute to irregular eating patterns that trigger binge-restriction cycles. Autistic traits such as sensory sensitivities, strong food preferences, and preference for routine promote restrictive eating, with skipped meals subsequently precipitating binge episodes. The study highlights that binge eating serves multiple functions for neurodivergent individuals—managing emotional overwhelm, under-stimulation, and meeting sensory needs—and identifies identity confusion and body-image preoccupation as perpetuating factors in eating disorder maintenance. Notably, participants experienced high rates of comorbid depression, anxiety, and physical health conditions including endometriosis and gastroparesis, while ADHD was associated with poorer treatment outcomes and higher dropout rates in eating disorder services.

title: A computational genetic- and transcriptomics-based study nominates drug repurposing candidates for the treatment of chronic pain
summary: This computational study integrates genome-wide association studies and transcriptome-wide association analysis to identify drug repurposing candidates for chronic pain treatment. Chronic pain demonstrates substantial genetic heritability (10–20% SNP-heritability) and is associated with multiple comorbidities including psychiatric disorders, sleep disturbance, and conditions such as endometriosis, osteoarthritis, and rheumatoid arthritis. The researchers performed TWAS across 17 tissue models spanning blood, skeletal muscle, and central nervous system regions (anterior cingulate cortex, amygdala, hippocampus, and spinal cord) and cross-referenced drug expression signatures from the Connectivity Map to nominate repurposing candidates. Beyond established treatments including opioids, gabapentin, pregabalin, NSAIDs, and non-pharmacological approaches like cognitive behavioral therapy and physical therapy, the study identified novel drug classes—including antidepressants, antipsychotics, cardiovascular agents, and antithrombotic agents—as potential therapeutic options. The findings highlight that chronic pain remains incompletely resolved even with current interventions, as evidenced by persistent symptoms following endometriosis excision surgery, suggesting the need for multimodal treatment approaches combining pharmacological and behavioral strategies.

title: Von Willebrand disease: more than just a bleeding disorder
journal: Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis
summary:
# Summary

Von Willebrand disease (VWD) is a bleeding disorder with complex systemic implications extending far beyond hemostasis. The heavy menstrual bleeding characteristic of VWD increases retrograde menstruation, which significantly elevates the risk of endometriosis—a condition further complicated by the chronic inflammatory state associated with elevated fibrinogen, D-dimer, and plasminogen activator inhibitor-1 levels. Genetic studies reveal that elevated von Willebrand factor plasma levels increase endometriosis risk, while ADAMTS-13 provides protective effects. Beyond reproductive complications, VWD associates with multiple comorbidities including low bone mineral density through altered osteoprotegerin-RANK ligand signaling, leading to increased fracture and osteoporosis risk, as well as joint hemorrhages that develop into arthropathy and pain. The chronic bleeding burden and associated iron deficiency anemia also contribute to migraine pathogenesis alongside elevated von Willebrand factor levels and estrogen fluctuations, while depression and anxiety occur at markedly elevated rates in affected patients. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that VWD requires a comprehensive clinical approach addressing its widespread psychological, skeletal, and reproductive consequences beyond conventional hemostatic management.

title: Exploring the impact of drug decriminalization and legalization policies on mental health outcomes: A scoping review
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review examined the mental health impacts of drug decriminalization and legalization policies, revealing a complex landscape with substantial evidence gaps. The review identified a bidirectional relationship between substance use and mental health conditions, with specific drugs—including cannabis, opioids, stimulants, and alcohol—linked to distinct psychiatric outcomes such as psychosis, depression, anxiety, and suicidality. Notably, the majority of studies found no significant association between cannabis policy changes and mental health outcomes, though approximately one-quarter reported increases in psychosis and depression prevalence following legalization, while a smaller proportion reported improvements in mental health indicators. Significant research limitations exist, particularly regarding the mental health effects of decriminalization policies for non-cannabis drugs, which remain virtually unexplored in the literature. The review underscores that while drug policies have reduced criminal justice burdens and may offer public health benefits, the evidence linking policy reform to specific mental health outcomes remains inconsistent and insufficient to draw definitive conclusions.

title: Association Between Hormonal Contraceptive Use and Lipedema: A Cross-Sectional Study With 637 Brazilian Women
summary: Lipedema, a chronic inflammatory disorder of subcutaneous adipose tissue characterized by symmetrical, painful fat accumulation and edema, is significantly exacerbated by hormonal contraceptive use in Brazilian women. Among 588 contraceptive users, 58.8% reported symptom worsening, and 15.1% reported symptom onset coinciding with contraceptive initiation. The estrogenic components of hormonal contraceptives trigger this exacerbation through multiple mechanisms: activation of mast cells, increased vascular permeability, compromised lymphatic function, and enhanced adipocyte proliferation. Pre-existing symptom severity emerged as the strongest independent predictor of contraceptive-associated worsening, alongside contributing factors including weight gain, mood changes, and early age at menarche, suggesting that individual susceptibility rather than cumulative hormonal exposure determines vulnerability to worsening. Lipedema substantially impairs quality of life, with pain intensity and body mass index emerging as dominant predictors of functional impairment, and comorbidities including hypothyroidism, anxiety/depression, and hypertension frequently co-occurring in affected women. These findings underscore the importance of considering lipedema severity and individual hormonal sensitivity when prescribing contraceptives to women at risk.

title: A systematic review of brain health in adults with chronic pain
summary: This systematic review demonstrates that chronic pain is associated with widespread structural and functional brain alterations that resemble accelerated brain aging. Adults with chronic pain exhibit significantly lower grey matter volumes and reduced white matter integrity, particularly in pain-processing and emotion-regulating regions including the insula, anterior and posterior cingulate cortex, thalamus, hippocampus, and primary motor cortex. Chronic pain is also linked to accelerated brain aging, evidenced by elevated brain age estimates and increased white matter hyperintensities, with specific pain conditions showing marked effects (osteoarthritis: 9.8 years older brain age; trigeminal neuralgia: 6.5 years older). These neurobiological changes overlap with Alzheimer's disease biomarkers, including alterations in serum brain-derived neurotrophic factor and amyloid pathology markers, suggesting a potential mechanistic link between chronic pain and increased dementia risk. Notably, use of non-pharmacological analgesia was associated with reduced brain age acceleration, indicating potential reversibility of some pain-related brain changes. These findings highlight the critical need for early neuroimaging and cognitive assessment in chronic pain populations to identify individuals at higher risk for neurodegeneration and dementia.

title: Brain sensory network activity underlies reduced nociceptive initiated and nociplastic pain via acupuncture in fibromyalgia
summary:
# Summary

Electroacupuncture reduces both nociceptive-initiated and nociplastic pain in fibromyalgia through engagement of somatosensory-insula brain circuits via a bottom-up sensory pathway. The mechanism begins when electroacupuncture activates mechanoreceptors, triggering afferent nerve fibers that activate spinal and supraspinal inhibitory pathways while enhancing endogenous opioids, endocannabinoids, serotonin, dopamine, and GABA—all critical neurotransmitters for pain modulation. Electroacupuncture increases pressure-pain tolerance, which drives heightened activation in the primary somatosensory cortex (S1), anterior insula, posterior midcingulate cortex, and precuneus; this S1 activation then strengthens connectivity between S1 and the right anterior insula, mediating widespread pain reduction. Notably, stronger functional connectivity between left S1 and right anterior insula, combined with decreased precuneus-insula connectivity, significantly mediated the relationship between increased pain tolerance and reduced widespread pain. These findings reveal that electroacupuncture targets the abnormal central pain processing underlying fibromyalgia by reengaging sensory brain networks, offering mechanistic insight into how this intervention can effectively treat both nociceptive and nociplastic pain components.

title: Outcome Assessment in Children and Adolescents With Chronic Pain: An International Clinical Practice Survey
summary: This international survey reveals significant variability in how chronic pain outcomes are assessed in children and adolescents across different healthcare settings. While chronic pain affects a substantial proportion of this population and causes profound disruption to physical, emotional, and social functioning, assessment practices remain inconsistent and often incomplete. Pain intensity and pain interference emerge as the most universally recognized mandatory outcome domains, with 93% of clinicians endorsing their assessment, followed closely by physical functioning at 92%, yet fewer than half of clinicians assess cognitive functioning, role functioning, or economic factors. Assessment practices vary substantially by country income level and professional background, with high-income countries reporting significantly higher rates of comprehensive outcome assessment and greater utilization of patient-reported outcome measures compared to lower-middle-income countries. The 0–10 Numeric Rating Scale dominates as the primary patient-reported outcome measure used globally, though its widespread adoption reflects convenience rather than comprehensive evaluation of the multidimensional impact of chronic pain. These findings highlight substantial gaps between clinical practice and comprehensive assessment standards, suggesting the need for internationally harmonized outcome measurement protocols to better capture the full burden of chronic pain in pediatric populations.

title: What constitutes important change in individual PROMIS-10 global health items in patients with high-impact chronic pain: a qualitative interview study
summary:
# Summary

High-impact chronic pain affects 5–15% of chronic pain patients and is strongly associated with poor health-related quality of life, psychological distress, cognitive difficulties, and significant interference with daily activities. This qualitative study used the PROMIS-10 Global Health instrument to understand what constitutes meaningful change for these patients, as the tool is widely applied in clinical practice to measure outcomes and support shared decision-making. Patients identified three key domains of meaningful improvement: physical function and social participation (Item 1), psychological well-being and everyday participation (Item 2), and physical freedom and energy (Item 3). Importantly, even small improvements in reduced pain intensity, increased energy, improved mood, and fewer negative emotions were perceived as clinically meaningful, as they directly enabled greater engagement in household tasks, work, leisure activities, and social interaction. These findings underscore that meaningful change in chronic pain populations extends beyond pain reduction alone to encompass mental well-being, fatigue reduction, and functional restoration of daily routines.

title: Altered Regional Brain Activity Underlying the Higher Postoperative Analgesic Requirements in Abstinent Smokers: A Prospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Abstinent smokers demonstrate significantly elevated postoperative analgesic requirements and lower pain thresholds compared to nonsmokers, a phenomenon mediated by altered functional activity in key brain regions. Nicotine withdrawal triggers hyperalgesia through multiple mechanisms, including activation of interferon regulatory factor 8, which enhances microglial activation and upregulates P2X purinoceptor 4 and brain-derived neurotrophic factor, while also disrupting inhibitory neurotransmitter systems including GABA, opioid receptors, and endorphins. Neuroimaging reveals that abstinent smokers exhibit reduced activity in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex (a core component of the default mode network involved in pain modulation) and elevated activity in the left calcarine and posterior cingulate cortex. The relationship between abstinence duration and pain threshold is mediated by the calcarine and posterior cingulate cortex, while withdrawal symptom severity predicts postoperative analgesic requirements through dysfunction in the ventromedial prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex. These findings establish that nicotine withdrawal fundamentally alters pain processing networks in the brain, resulting in enhanced pain sensitivity and increased clinical opioid requirements during the perioperative period.

title: Neural basis of intensity-dependent brain activity in response to mechanical stimulation and the level of pain sensitivity
summary: This study reveals how the brain processes mechanical stimuli with intensity-dependent neural responses that distinguish between individuals with different pain sensitivities. Using functional neuroimaging during graded mechanical stimulation, researchers found that low-threshold individuals showed localized activation in somatosensory cortical regions (postcentral gyrus, supramarginal gyrus, parietal operculum) even at mild stimulus intensities, reflecting basic tactile information processing. In contrast, high-threshold individuals demonstrated broader neural recruitment including the anterior insular cortex, frontoparietal network, and prefrontal regions, indicating engagement of attentional and cognitive control mechanisms during the same stimuli. As stimulus intensity increased to painful levels (180g), additional regions activated including the thalamus, cerebellum, and angular gyrus, reflecting the transition from sensory discrimination to pain experience and higher-order cognitive reappraisal. The anterior insular cortex, a key hub of the salience network, showed preferential activation in high-threshold individuals and likely reflects evaluation of stimulus harmfulness and prediction error. These findings suggest that individual differences in pain sensitivity manifest as distinct neural processing strategies, with implications for understanding chronic pain conditions like endometriosis that involve mechanical allodynia and altered sensory processing.

title: Pain in polycystic ovary syndrome: a comprehensive bedside to bench perspective on an underrecognized symptom
summary:
# Summary

Polycystic ovary syndrome is characterized not only by hyperandrogenism and ovulatory dysfunction but also by a frequently underrecognized symptom of chronic pain, including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, abdominal cramping, and dyspareunia. Multiple interconnected biological mechanisms drive PCOS-related pain, including chronic low-grade inflammation mediated by elevated interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor alpha, and C-reactive protein; oxidative stress; insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia; elevated androgens; and altered hormonal cycling that influences nociceptive thresholds. Central neuroimmune processes such as microglial activation and T cell-driven immune signaling contribute to pain hypersensitivity through nociceptor sensitization, while specific molecular pathways—including elevated prostaglandins E2 and F2α driving myometrial contractions, endothelial dysfunction via asymmetric dimethylarginine, and dysregulation of the endogenous opioid system—amplify pain perception. PCOS shows significant clinical and pathophysiological overlap with endometriosis and is associated with comorbid chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, migraines, and irritable bowel syndrome, alongside psychiatric comorbidities such as depression and anxiety that further worsen pain outcomes and reduce quality of life.

title: Neurophysiological, Radiological, and Molecular Biomarkers of Pain-Related Conditions: An Umbrella Review
summary: This umbrella review identifies multiple biomarkers across inflammatory, neuroimaging, and molecular domains that characterize chronic pain conditions. The three pro-inflammatory cytokines—CRP, IL-6, and TNF-α—show the most consistent associations with chronic musculoskeletal pain, particularly in non-specific and chronic low back pain, where they are significantly elevated. Neurophysiological markers demonstrate distinct central nervous system alterations in chronic pain, including reduced gray matter volume in regions such as the left superior frontal gyrus and left insula, coupled with characteristic EEG pattern changes showing increased theta power and decreased alpha and beta power in neuropathic pain states. Notably, resistance and neuromuscular training effectively reduce inflammatory markers in osteoarthritis patients, providing a mechanistic basis for exercise-based pain management. Women experience more severe and widespread pain than men, and chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, diabetic neuropathy, and back pain frequently co-occur with anxiety and psychological comorbidities. Emerging proteomic panels show exploratory diagnostic potential for fibromyalgia, though additional validation is needed, while physiotherapy may modulate underlying neurotrophins, neurotransmitters, and cytokines to ameliorate pain symptoms.

title: In the Light of Healthcare Professionals: Beliefs About Chronic Low Back Pain
summary:
# Summary

Chronic low back pain remains a leading cause of disability in developed countries, with psychological factors including anxiety, depression, pain catastrophizing, and fear-avoidance beliefs significantly contributing to its chronicity. Healthcare professionals, particularly physiotherapists, demonstrate gaps in understanding pain neurophysiology and tend to hold traditional biomedical beliefs about chronic low back pain, despite evidence that younger professionals with greater knowledge of pain mechanisms exhibit substantially lower kinesiophobia. Pain education interventions centered on neurophysiological understanding are highly effective at reducing fear-avoidance behaviors, kinesiophobia, and anxiety while improving treatment outcomes, particularly when delivered by therapists adopting a biopsychosocial approach. The study reveals that age inversely correlates with both pain neurophysiology knowledge and kinesiophobia levels, with older individuals showing stronger traditional biomedical beliefs. Integration of biopsychosocial model training into professional education emerges as critical for improving healthcare providers' capacity to address the complex, multifactorial nature of chronic low back pain and enhance patient outcomes through evidence-based therapeutic approaches.

title: Visual analysis of research hotspots and trends of external therapies in traditional Chinese medicine for chronic pain
summary:
# Summary

Chronic pain is strongly associated with comorbid psychological conditions including depression and anxiety, as well as disrupted sleep patterns, creating a complex clinical challenge requiring multifaceted treatment approaches. Traditional Chinese medicine external therapies, particularly electroacupuncture and tuina therapy, emerge as effective treatments for chronic pain across multiple conditions including low back pain, knee osteoarthritis, and endometriosis. Electroacupuncture demonstrates dual benefits by reducing pain intensity while simultaneously alleviating depression and anxiety symptoms through mechanisms involving inhibition of neuroinflammatory pathways (microglia activation, NLRP3 inflammasome), modulation of dopaminergic signaling, and restoration of glutamatergic circuit function. Additional emerging therapies including cupping, warm acupuncture, and wrist-ankle acupuncture show promise for pain management. Notably, patients receiving electroacupuncture achieve stable long-term efficacy with reduced opioid medication requirements, suggesting these traditional approaches may serve as valuable alternatives to conventional pharmacological pain management while addressing the psychological burden of chronic pain conditions.

title: Association between sleep disorders and rheumatoid arthritis in U.S. adults
summary: This cross-sectional study of U.S. adults identified a significant association between sleep disorders and rheumatoid arthritis, with individuals experiencing sleep disorders having 1.76 times higher odds of RA. The association is mechanistically supported by sleep disturbances triggering systemic inflammation, altered cytokine activity, oxidative stress, and impaired immune regulation—all factors contributing to dysregulation of immune homeostasis and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines characteristic of RA. Multiple sociodemographic and clinical factors independently increased RA risk, including advancing age (≥45 years), female gender, non-Hispanic Black race, obesity, smoking, and comorbidities such as hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia, while higher education and socioeconomic status were protective. The relationship appears bidirectional, with RA-related pain and psychological distress potentially perpetuating sleep disturbances and creating a vicious cycle. Specific sleep disorders, particularly sleep apnea, showed particularly strong associations with RA prevalence. These findings suggest that sleep quality and continuity may represent modifiable risk factors or complications in RA pathogenesis worth targeting therapeutically.

title: Genetic and Phenotypic Associations With Sustained Antidepressant Use in Major Depressive Disorder
summary:
# Summary: Genetic and Phenotypic Associations With Sustained Antidepressant Use in Major Depressive Disorder

This study identified distinct phenotypic profiles and genetic factors underlying sustained antidepressant use patterns in major depressive disorder, revealing that treatment complexity—requiring multiple drug classes—is associated with higher polygenic scores for psychiatric disorders (MDD, ADHD, bipolar disorder, and neuroticism) and numerous comorbid conditions including chronic pain, suicidal ideation, and recurrent depression. Individuals taking SNRIs exhibited markedly different phenotypes compared to SSRI users, including higher rates of chronic pain, endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, atypical depression, and personality disorders, while TCA users showed elevated chronic pain and endometriosis. A genome-wide significant immune-related variant in SLAMF3/LY9—which encodes CD299, a lymphoid cell surface receptor involved in T cell activation—was associated with SSRI use, suggesting immune dysregulation participates in antidepressant treatment response. Treatment complexity was also associated with modifiable lifestyle factors including smoking, lower education, and alcohol consumption, while circulating individual SSRI selection (fluoxetine versus sertraline) related to specific depression subtypes and serotonergic regulation of circadian rhythms. These findings indicate that genetic predisposition, immune function, comorbid pain conditions, and clinical presentation jointly determine antidepressant class selection and treatment patterns in depression.

title: Development and Validation of the Brief Inventory of Treatment Expectations in Chronic Pain (BITEC)
summary:
# Summary

This study presents the development and validation of the Brief Inventory of Treatment Expectations in Chronic Pain (BITEC), a concise instrument designed to systematically assess patient expectations—a critical yet understudied factor shaping therapeutic outcomes in chronic pain management. The research demonstrates that treatment expectations operate through multiple biopsychosocial pathways: positive expectations modulate brain activity and symptom perception, while negative expectations promote distress and maladaptive behaviors. BITEC demonstrated strong construct validity across calibration (n=1127) and validation (n=242) samples, effectively distinguishing expectation categories based on symptom severity, catastrophizing tendencies, and pain phenotypes, with expectation levels varying significantly across conditions (nociceptive pain 56.1% versus fibromyalgia 42.7%). The study highlights that clinicians substantially shape treatment expectations through communication, goal-setting, and shared decision-making approaches, underscoring the importance of person-centered care that explicitly addresses patient expectations. Given that up to 79% of chronic non-cancer pain patients report inadequate pain control despite available treatments, this validated instrument offers clinicians a practical tool for optimizing therapeutic interventions by strategically managing patient expectations to enhance outcomes.

title: Central Nervous System Mechanisms and Treatment Response in Chronic Ocular Surface Pain: Protocol for a Cross-Sectional Observational Phenotyping Study
summary:
# Summary

Chronic ocular surface pain (COSP) represents a heterogeneous condition in which some patients experience central nervous system (CNS) dysfunction-driven symptoms that appear disproportionate to observable ocular signs. This study proposes that increasing symptom-sign discordance correlates with nociplastic pain features—including amplified neural signaling, dysregulated sensory processing, multisite pain, and heightened multisensory hypersensitivity—similar to prototypical nociplastic conditions like fibromyalgia. The research will employ comprehensive quantitative sensory testing (corneal, pressure, thermal, and visual modalities), structural and functional brain imaging, and corneal nerve fiber density assessment to phenotype patients across the discordance spectrum and identify neurobiological predictors of treatment response. Findings aim to distinguish patients who would benefit from peripheral treatments (topical anesthetics) versus CNS-directed pain interventions, ultimately improving outcomes for patients whose COSP causes substantial morbidity including decreased quality of life, visual dysfunction, and elevated suicide risk.

title: Sacral Tarlov cysts and spontaneous persistent genital arousal: 2 unrecognized and underappreciated health conditions with an uncertain relationship
summary:
# Summary

This report highlights the underrecognized relationship between sacral Tarlov cysts and persistent genital arousal disorder (PGAD), two distinct but potentially overlapping conditions. Tarlov cysts, which are cerebrospinal fluid-filled sacs located along sacral nerve roots at the S2-S3 level, can cause nerve compression and impairment leading to pain, neurologic dysfunction, and genital paresthesia or dysesthesia. The compression of the pudendal and other pelvic nerves by these cysts or alternative intrapelvic pathologies appears to trigger PGAD, though other etiologies including small fiber neuropathy, intervertebral disk pathology, and even selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor use contribute to the disorder's heterogeneous presentation. PGAD patients exhibit a distinctive comorbidity profile, including high rates of restless leg syndrome, urinary dysfunction, depression, anxiety, fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, pudendal neuralgia, and significantly compromised quality of life and sleep quality. While mechanical nerve release, surgical decompression of Tarlov cysts, and neuromodulation show promise as interventions, many treatments including clonazepam, pudendal nerve blocks, and local anesthetics have demonstrated limited or temporary efficacy, underscoring the need for better understanding of these conditions' mechanistic relationship and their joint management.

title: Regional homogeneity alterations reflect pain and emotional dysregulation in adenomyosis
summary: Adenomyosis with dysmenorrhea (AMD) is characterized by altered intrinsic brain activity in pain- and emotion-processing regions, reflecting a central nervous system mechanism underlying the condition's chronic pain and affective symptoms. Neuroimaging analysis revealed increased regional homogeneity in the right fusiform gyrus, bilateral angular gyri, right hippocampus, right cerebellum, and frontal regions, while the bilateral supramarginal gyri showed reduced activity in AMD patients compared to healthy controls. The right fusiform gyrus activity correlated significantly with pain severity and anxiety symptoms, while abnormalities in the right hippocampus and cerebellum associated with both anxiety and depression scores. These brain regions collectively participate in pain perception, pain modulation, emotional regulation, and nociceptive processing, suggesting that AMD involves disrupted neural circuits linking pain encoding to affective dysregulation. The findings support inflammation and neurogenic pain mediators contributing to central sensitization and spinal pain hypersensitivity in AMD, with both regional homogeneity measures (static and dynamic ReHo) capable of detecting these aberrant brain function patterns. Current hormonal treatments including gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues, progestins, and oral contraceptives target adenomyosis management, though these neuroimaging biomarkers may help better characterize disease mechanisms and treatment responses.

title: Autonomic dysfunction and its associations with clinical parameters in Familial Mediterranean Fever: a cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates that familial Mediterranean fever (FMF), a chronic autoinflammatory disorder characterized by recurrent febrile episodes and serositis, is significantly associated with autonomic dysfunction that contributes to reduced quality of life and comorbid symptoms. FMF patients exhibited markedly elevated autonomic symptom burden measured by COMPASS-31 scores and showed predominant parasympathetic dysfunction, particularly reduced respiratory rate variability during normal breathing that correlated negatively with inflammatory markers like C-reactive protein. The study revealed strong correlations between autonomic dysfunction severity and multiple comorbidities including fatigue, sleep disturbance, anxiety, depression, and fibromyalgia, indicating that autonomic alterations may be a key mechanism linking FMF's chronic inflammatory burden to these associated conditions. Sympathetic and parasympathetic parameters also correlated with disease severity markers, with gastrointestinal autonomic dysfunction specifically correlating with inflammatory markers and attack frequency. These findings suggest that autonomic dysfunction represents an important and potentially modifiable contributor to the symptom burden and poor quality of life experienced by FMF patients, warranting further investigation into whether targeting autonomic dysfunction could improve clinical outcomes alongside standard FMF treatments such as colchicine and IL-1 inhibitors.

title: Children with suspected anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome (ACNES), it’s not all the abdominal wall
summary:
# Summary

This study examines anterior cutaneous nerve entrapment syndrome (ACNES) in children with chronic abdominal pain, emphasizing that diagnosis and management require more than assessment of the anatomical abdominal wall pathology. While ACNES involves entrapment of the terminal intercostal nerve branch causing localized abdominal pain with characteristic physical findings (positive Carnett sign, pinch test, and response to local anesthetic injection), the authors highlight that psychological and social factors significantly contribute to both the development and persistence of chronic abdominal pain in these patients. Multiple comorbidities and psychosocial stressors—including anxiety, depression, catastrophizing behavior, adverse life events, and school bullying—were prevalent in the cohort and associated with pain maintenance. Applying a comprehensive biopsychosocial model that incorporates psychodiagnostics, psychotherapy, pain education, and cognitive behavioral therapy alongside or before invasive interventions resulted in 64.5% of patients reporting pain reduction or resolution, while emphasizing this holistic approach prevents unnecessary surgical procedures like neurectomy. The study demonstrates that successful management of suspected ACNES requires integrated care addressing biological mechanisms, psychological comorbidities, and social factors to avoid inadequate treatment and improve health-related quality of life.

title: Assessment of Central Sensitivity Syndrome and Sensory Processing Sensitivity: A Systematic Review
journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
summary:
# Summary

Central sensitization, a fundamental neurobiological mechanism, generates central sensitivity syndrome (CSS) through heightened excitability of central neurons, which is orchestrated by a complex interplay between immune activation and neuroendocrine dysfunction. Proinflammatory cytokines, neural growth factors, and histamines released by activated immune cells trigger central sensitization, while the central nervous system reciprocally hyperexcites immune cells, particularly mast cells, establishing a bidirectional neuro-immune-endocrine axis involving the hypothalamic-pituitary system, autonomic nervous system, and stress hormones. Sensory processing sensitivity (SPS), an innate trait characterized by heightened awareness, deep stimulus processing, and emotional reactivity, manifests through a distinct but parallel pathway involving sustained psycho-emotional reactivity that precipitates chronic symptoms. Both CSS and SPS involve genetic predisposition combined with environmental triggers such as stress and chemical exposures, resulting in overlapping presentations including chronic pain, musculoskeletal disorders, and psychological impairments, with environmental hypersensitivity present in both conditions. The review establishes validated assessment tools for each condition (CSI/CSI-9 for CSS; HSPS/HSCS for SPS) and identifies psychological distress as a critical mediator linking sensory hypersensitivity to sustained physical and physiological dysfunction in both syndromes.

title: Severe Pain Phenotype in Hemoglobin C (HbC)/Beta-Zero (β⁰) Thalassemia Without Hemoglobin S (HbS): A Clinical and Diagnostic Challenge
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a patient with HbC/β⁰-thalassemia presenting with severe, persistent diffuse bone pain—an unusual manifestation of this rare hemoglobinopathy. Unlike hemoglobin S in sickle cell disease, HbC does not polymerize under hypoxia and typically does not cause vaso-occlusive pain crises; however, the combination of HbC and β⁰-thalassemia mutations causes chronic hemolysis, anemia requiring transfusion, and extramedullary hematopoiesis with marked marrow expansion, which is the primary hypothesized source of the patient's pain originating in the lower back and hips. Despite treatment with hydroxyurea, which induces fetal hemoglobin, and aggressive opioid therapy with multiple agents, the patient experienced worsening pain potentially complicated by opioid-induced hyperalgesia and central sensitization from chronic high-dose opioid use. The case highlights the diagnostic challenge of HbC/β⁰-thalassemia and the paradoxical risk that escalating opioids without addressing underlying mechanisms may worsen pain outcomes in patients with complex hemolytic anemias.

title: Gaining the Upper Hand? Further Evidence of Pain as a Pleasurable Experience and the Unexpected Relationship Between Sadomasochistic Sexual Preference and Chronic Pain
summary: This study reveals a striking paradox: individuals with sadomasochistic (SM) sexual preferences show significantly elevated rates of chronic pain (47.2% versus lower rates in non-SM populations), yet they demonstrate fundamentally different psychological relationships with pain compared to those with chronic pain alone. While chronic pain patients view pain as an obstacle and experience helplessness, SM-preferring individuals exhibit higher pain fascination, pain acceptance, and challenge-oriented attitudes toward pain, alongside greater sensation seeking and stoicism. The mechanistic basis involves multiple neurobiological systems including dopamine, opioids, cortisol, oxytocin, and endocannabinoids, with submissive practitioners showing elevated cortisol and endocannabinoid levels during SM practice. Notably, SM practitioners with chronic pain report that intentional acute pain exposure provides short-term relief, increases pain tolerance, and restores psychological control through flow-like states, mindfulness, and emotional regulation—suggesting they use controlled pain to counteract uncontrolled chronic pain. These findings suggest that pain perception and response are heavily modulated by psychological framing and learned associations, with SM practice serving as a coping mechanism that transforms pain from a passive threat into an active, empowering experience.

title: Migraine epidemiology, comorbidities and therapeutic landscape: a national population-based study
summary: This population-based study reveals that migraine is a highly comorbid condition with far-reaching associations across multiple disease domains. Patients with migraine demonstrate substantially elevated rates of chronic pain syndromes, including low back pain (2.67-fold higher), fibromyalgia (2.42-fold), and endometriosis (1.86-fold), suggesting shared underlying pain processing mechanisms. Psychiatric comorbidities are also prominent, with anxiety and depression occurring at approximately twofold greater rates in migraine patients compared to controls. Cardiovascular and metabolic associations include increased hypertension, dyslipidemia, cerebrovascular disease, and stroke risk, though notably diabetes mellitus is inversely associated with migraine. Current therapeutic management relies primarily on tricyclic antidepressants, SNRIs, and beta-blockers for prevention, while migraine-specific agents such as CGRP monoclonal antibodies and gepants remain underutilized at less than 1% of patient populations, despite mechanistic evidence implicating neuropeptides including CGRP, PACAP, substance P, neuropeptide Y, and orexins. The condition disproportionately affects females (75.8% of cases) and younger adults, with peak incidence in women aged 18–24, highlighting significant sex-based epidemiological patterns.

title: Phenotypic Characteristics of Chronic Pain and Pain-Related Factors
summary: This study reveals a substantially elevated burden of chronic pain in gender minority individuals, with 40-59% prevalence rates compared to cisgender populations. Transgender men and gender-expansive persons demonstrate particularly high rates of fibromyalgia, temporomandibular disorder, neuropathic pain, and widespread pain conditions, alongside significantly increased pain severity, interference with daily function, sleep disturbances, and fatigue. Sex hormone biology appears to modulate pain sensitivity, with testosterone conferring protective analgesic effects while estrogens and progesterone demonstrate complex bidirectional effects on pain processing. The elevated pain burden in gender minority populations is mechanistically linked to chronic exposure to stigma, discrimination, and identity-related stress, which activates neuroendocrine and inflammatory pathways that enhance pain sensitivity, compounded by higher rates of adverse childhood experiences, trauma, and psychological distress. Hormonal changes associated with gender-affirming therapy, sleep disruption, and developmental hormone exposure (particularly female hormone exposure in utero for transgender men) further contribute to pain vulnerability. These findings suggest that chronic pain in gender minority persons represents a multifactorial condition driven by psychosocial minority stressors, trauma history, and sex hormone-mediated neuroendocrine dysregulation.

title: Nociplastic pain: the prominent role of non-neuronal cells in central and peripheral sensitization
summary:
# Summary

This review establishes non-neuronal cells as central drivers of nociplastic pain through their mediation of both peripheral and central sensitization. Glial cells and circulating immune cells modulate nociceptive processing and synaptic plasticity, key mechanisms underlying chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, irritable bowel syndrome, and complex regional pain syndrome. Viral infections such as COVID-19 trigger nociplastic pain by compromising the blood-brain barrier through matrix metalloproteinases and facilitating immune cell infiltration into the central nervous system, with genetic variations in genes like COMT, TRPV1, and DRD2 conferring susceptibility. Environmental stressors including cold stimulation, alcohol abuse, and gut microbiota dysbiosis contribute to nociplastic pain development, while dysbiotic patterns characterized by reduced Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium and elevated Escherichia coli and Enterobacter are implicated in pain pathology. Hormonal dysfunction involving the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, manifested as reduced ACTH and epinephrine responses, further characterizes fibromyalgia and related nociplastic pain conditions. Therapeutic interventions targeting non-neuronal cell function and neuroimmune signaling, such as macitentan, offer promise for reversing hypersensitivity and chronic pain symptoms.

title: Joint association of chronic pain and sleep patterns with cardiovascular diseases: a prospective study
summary: This prospective study demonstrates that chronic pain and poor sleep patterns are independently associated with significantly increased cardiovascular risk, with hazard ratios ranging from 1.06 to 1.83 across cardiovascular disease, coronary heart disease, and stroke outcomes. Chronic widespread pain carries a higher risk (76% increased risk) compared to chronic localized pain (23% increased risk), and the combination of chronic pain with poor sleep patterns produces additive effects, with coexisting chronic widespread pain and poor sleep elevating CVD risk substantially beyond either factor alone. Both chronic pain conditions and disrupted sleep are associated with common comorbidities including depression, hypertension, and diabetes, with female sex, elevated BMI, higher non-HDL cholesterol, and smoking emerging as shared risk factors. Genetic factors influencing sleep quality and chronic pain show significant correlation, and sleep disorders are highly prevalent in chronic pain populations, with over 40% of individuals experiencing sleep problems reporting chronic pain. Healthy sleep patterns—characterized by early chronotype, 7–8 hours of sleep per night, and absence of insomnia and excessive daytime sleepiness—are protective against cardiovascular disease. These findings underscore the clinical importance of addressing both chronic pain and sleep disturbances as interconnected risk factors for cardiovascular disease.

title: Beyond the Pain: Rethinking Chronic Pain Management Through Integrated Therapeutic Approaches—A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Neuropathic pain arises from maladaptive neuroplastic responses following diverse insults including peripheral nerve injury, central nervous system damage, viral infection, tumors, and metabolic disorders. The pain pathways involve complex neurotransmitter systems where noradrenaline, serotonin, GABA, and endogenous opioids modulate pain through descending pathways, with α2-adrenoceptors providing antinociceptive effects. Central sensitization—characterized by heightened excitation and impaired endogenous pain inhibition—is associated with microglial inflammation, altered GABAergic and glycinergic signaling, and brain-derived neurotrophic factor involvement, and presents with comorbid mood disturbances, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and sleep dysfunction. Psychological factors including early life trauma and emotional stress contribute substantially to centralized pain development. Conditions like fibromyalgia and complex regional pain syndrome exemplify these mechanisms through widespread pain, hyperalgesia, and allodynia driven by neuroinflammatory and neuroplastic alterations. Current pharmacological approaches target these mechanisms through NSAIDs and paracetamol (inhibiting cyclooxygenase pathways), antidepressants (modulating monoamine neurotransmission), anticonvulsants (stabilizing ion channel activity), and emerging biologics like CGRP antagonists (erenumab, galcanezumab) that interrupt neuropeptide-mediated sensitization.

title: Visual hypersensitivity as a transdiagnostic marker of surgical pain response in arthritis and chronic pain syndromes
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies visual hypersensitivity as a transdiagnostic marker of nociplastic pain—pain driven by central nervous system dysfunction rather than peripheral tissue damage—across multiple chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, and chronic pelvic pain including endometriosis. Nociplastic pain involves central sensitization and is associated with widespread sensory hypersensitivity, fatigue, sleep disturbances, cognitive dysfunction, and mood disturbances. The researchers demonstrated that increased visual sensitivity independently predicted poor surgical outcomes following hip arthroplasty or hysterectomy, whereas traditional pressure pain sensitivity measures did not predict surgical response. Visual stimulation activates the insula, a brain region involved in pain processing, and this activation is reduced by pregabalin treatment. These findings establish visual sensitivity as a superior clinical tool for identifying patients with central sensitization mechanisms who are likely to have poor responses to surgical intervention, suggesting that assessment of visual hypersensitivity could guide treatment selection across diverse chronic pain populations.

title: A biopsychosocial perspective on endometriosis: the importance of psychological inflexibility
summary: Endometriosis causes multiple overlapping symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia, while also triggering significant psychiatric comorbidities such as depression and anxiety. While hormone therapy and surgical interventions provide symptom relief for some patients, this study reveals that psychological factors—particularly psychological inflexibility, perceived loss of control, and inadequate social support—emerge as highly central nodes in the symptom network, demonstrating greater importance than biological factors alone in determining disease burden. Pain-related variables (intensity and interference) form interconnected clusters with anxiety and depression, with psychological inflexibility showing strong direct connections to pain interference and catastrophizing contributing to anxiety. The biopsychosocial perspective suggests that standard treatments addressing only biological mechanisms are insufficient, and psychological interventions based on the psychological flexibility model, such as Acceptance and Commitment Therapy, warrant investigation as adjunctive approaches. Enhanced social support and improved perceived control appear as critical intervention targets alongside traditional medical management. This network analysis underscores the need for integrated treatment strategies that address the psychological processes maintaining endometriosis-related suffering.

title: Causal association between periodontitis and systemic diseases: a systematic review and meta-analysis of mendelian randomization studies
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis of Mendelian randomization studies establishes causal relationships between periodontitis and multiple systemic diseases. Periodontitis is a chronic inflammatory disease driven by dysbiotic plaque biofilms, microbial infection, and host immune responses, which progressively destroy periodontal attachment structures. The analysis identified strong causal associations between periodontitis and cardioembolic stroke, depression, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and diabetes, with metabolic factors like elevated triglyceride-glucose index and high systolic blood pressure serving as potential mediating mechanisms. However, the study found no substantial causal associations between periodontitis and several conditions including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, coronary atherosclerosis, rheumatoid arthritis, thyroid disorders, and gastrointestinal diseases. These findings underscore periodontitis as a significant contributor to cardiovascular and metabolic comorbidities, while refuting previously suspected links to neurodegenerative and autoimmune conditions.

title: Associations Between Reproductive Disorders and Specific Physical, Mental, Personality, and Social Characteristics: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines the multifaceted associations between reproductive disorders and physical, mental, personality, and social characteristics. Endometriosis is characterized by a distinct phenotype including lower body mass index, lower waist-to-hip ratio, and greater breast-to-underbreast ratio, alongside physical features such as fair skin, red hair, light eyes, freckles, and high sun sensitivity. Beyond physical manifestations, endometriosis shows strong associations with significant psychological and psychiatric comorbidities including depression, anxiety, stress, affective disorders, panic-agoraphobic disorders, substance use disorders, and eating disorders, with consequent impacts on body image, quality of life, and progressive social isolation including avoidance of intimate relationships. Polycystic ovary syndrome presents with central adiposity and related metabolic features (elevated waist-to-hip ratio, acne, hirsutism), coupled with impaired cognitive performance across memory, executive function, attention, and visuospatial skills, as well as anxiety, depression, eating disorders, and psychosexual dysfunction. Diminished ovarian reserve in younger women and low semen quality are both associated with anxiety, depression, and lower socioeconomic status, while sperm quality shows negative correlation with adiposity measures and certain personality traits. All four reproductive disorders significantly burden quality of life and socioeconomic status through economic impact, productivity loss, and increased healthcare resource utilization.

title: Associations Between Depression and Reduced Quality of Life in Women with Non-Radiographic Axial Spondyloarthritis: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined women with non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (nr-axSpA) and identified robust associations between disease activity and psychological comorbidities. Disease-related variables including the Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index (BASDAI), Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Score (ASDAS), Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Functional Index (BASFI), and visual analog pain scale (VAS) all demonstrated strong positive correlations with depression and anxiety symptoms, while inversely correlating with both physical and mental quality of life components. A family history of spondyloarthritis emerged as an independent risk factor, with affected women showing significantly higher depression scores and reduced mental health outcomes. The study revealed that spondyloarthritis features (including inflammatory back pain, peripheral arthritis, and enthesitis) and overall disease burden collectively contributed to reduced quality of life and elevated psychological distress risk in this patient population. NSAIDs, biologics, and disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs were used to manage the condition, though disease activity remained a dominant driver of mental health and functional impairment. These findings underscore the importance of addressing both inflammatory disease control and comorbid depression and anxiety in the comprehensive management of women with nr-axSpA.

title: Sexually Dimorphic Neuroimmune Pathways in Chronic Pain: A Comprehensive Systematic Review of Cellular and Molecular Mechanisms
summary: This comprehensive systematic review reveals fundamental sex-based differences in neuroimmune mechanisms underlying chronic pain. In males, chronic pain is predominantly driven by microglial activation through P2X4 receptor and pannexin-1 signaling, which elevates proinflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-1β) that enhance neuronal excitability and promote central sensitization via BDNF-mediated neuronal disinhibition and NMDA receptor potentiation. In contrast, females develop chronic pain through adaptive immune processes involving CD4+ and CD8+ T cell infiltration, leptin release, and prolactin receptor signaling in sensory neurons, with distinct chemokine profiles (CCL1, CCL21) and pannexin-1 mechanisms in T cells. Both sexes show glial cell activation, neuroinflammation, and elevated TSPO as neuroimmune markers across chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, migraine, and neuropathic pain; however, these mechanisms manifest through distinct molecular pathways. Women clinically experience higher prevalence and severity of chronic pain conditions, while sex-specific therapeutic approaches—such as TNF receptor inhibition for males—offer promise for improved treatment outcomes. These findings underscore the critical importance of sex-sensitive drug development and personalized medicine strategies in chronic pain management.

title: Prevalence of Poor Sleep Quality and Its Association with Dysmenorrhea Among Female Undergraduate Students at a Health Sciences University in the UAE
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the relationship between sleep quality and dysmenorrhea in female undergraduate students at a UAE health sciences university, revealing substantial bidirectional associations between these conditions. Poor sleep quality, particularly sleep fragmentation and disturbance, showed dose-dependent relationships with severe dysmenorrhea, with moderate and severe sleep problems increasing dysmenorrhea odds by 2- to 3.6-fold respectively. Mechanistically, poor sleep stimulates inflammatory mediators and prostaglandin synthesis while disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility, pathways known to amplify menstrual pain perception. Beyond pain intensity, poor sleep quality independently predicted menstrual-related functional impairment and reduced physical activity, social engagement, and daily productivity, demonstrating that the sleep-dysmenorrhea relationship has substantial clinical consequences affecting student quality of life and academic performance. Sleep disturbance specifically emerged as the strongest sleep component predicting severe dysmenorrhea severity, suggesting that sleep fragmentation rather than duration alone drives this association. These findings underscore sleep quality as a potentially modifiable target for reducing dysmenorrhea burden in this population.

title: Comparing Movement Patterns and Physical Function Between Chronic Low Back Pain Patients With Nociplastic and Nociceptive Pain Categories
summary: This study compared movement patterns and physical function between chronic low back pain patients with nociplastic pain (resulting from altered sensory processing) versus nociceptive pain (associated with tissue damage). Patients with nociplastic pain demonstrated significantly worse clinical outcomes, including greater perceived functional limitations on the PROMIS-PF scale, slower sit-to-stand times, and elevated anxiety and depression scores compared to the nociceptive pain group. The nociplastic pain group was also older and had a higher female predominance, suggesting demographic risk factors for this pain phenotype. Pain category emerged as a significant predictor of perceived physical function, indicating that nociplastic pain classification is more closely associated with functional impairment than tissue-based pain mechanisms. The findings suggest that nociplastic pain responds better to psychologically-informed interventions such as cognitive behavioral therapy rather than tissue-focused treatments, and that poor treatment outcomes are more likely when nociplastic pain is present.

title: Prevalence and related factors of irritable bowel syndrome in women with chronic pelvic pain
summary:
# Summary

The study found a high prevalence of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) in women with chronic pelvic pain (CPP), occurring in 53.15% of cases, with myofascial pain identified as the strongest independent predictor of IBS. Women with IBS showed significantly elevated rates of myofascial pain (84.7%), neuropathic pain (47.5%), and central sensitization compared to those without IBS, reflecting dysregulated pain processing mechanisms. The underlying pathophysiology of IBS involves multifactorial mechanisms including altered gastrointestinal motility, visceral hypersensitivity, microbiota dysbiosis, immune dysfunction, and central nervous system processing abnormalities mediated through disturbed gut-brain axis communication. Beyond gastrointestinal symptoms, IBS in women with CPP was associated with numerous gynecological and systemic conditions including endometriosis, adenomyosis, dyspareunia, pelvic congestion, vulvodynia, and psychological comorbidities such as anxiety and depression. The bidirectional communication disturbance between the gut and central nervous system appears central to the IBS-CPP relationship, positioning IBS within the broader category of Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction that manifest as abdominal pain, bloating, and altered bowel habits.

title: Catastrophizing in Chronic Itch: Associations with Demographic, Clinical and Psychological Factors
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated catastrophizing—the tendency to interpret situations in an overly negative manner—as a key cognitive factor in chronic itch disorders. Catastrophizing was significantly associated with worse itch intensity, quality of life impairment, and heightened vigilance to itch sensations across three chronic pruritus conditions: chronic prurigo, chronic pruritus on nonlesional skin, and chronic spontaneous urticaria. Patients with chronic prurigo demonstrated the highest catastrophizing scores, particularly those with visible skin lesions, while disease control status inversely predicted catastrophizing levels. The study found strong correlations between catastrophizing and anxiety, depression, and stress symptoms, with vigilant attention to itch appearing to amplify catastrophic thinking patterns. Notably, younger age and longer disease duration were associated with higher catastrophizing, and various psychological interventions—including cognitive restructuring, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and acceptance and commitment therapy—showed potential for reducing catastrophizing and improving outcomes in chronic itch patients.

title: Are Positive Mental Health and Social Support Protective Factors in the Development of Suicidal Ideation in Chronic Pain? A Longitudinal Study
summary: This longitudinal study demonstrates that persons living with chronic pain face substantial risk for suicidal ideation through a pathway mediated by feelings of entrapment. Both internal entrapment (feelings of being trapped psychologically) and external entrapment (situational constraints) significantly predict suicidal ideation, with internal entrapment showing the strongest effect. Positive mental health and social support emerge as critical protective factors that buffer against suicidal ideation by reducing the impact of entrapment. Specifically, positive mental health significantly inhibits suicidal ideation and moderates the relationship between both internal and external entrapment and suicidal risk. Additional protective factors identified include hope, resilience, meaning in life, reasons for living, and positive future thinking, which may prevent or reduce suicidal ideation in chronic pain populations. These findings underscore the importance of interventions that enhance positive mental health and social connections while addressing feelings of defeat and entrapment in persons living with chronic pain.

title: Effect of Primary Dysmenorrhea on the Mental Health Quality of Medical Students in Saudi Arabia
summary: Primary dysmenorrhea significantly impairs the mental health and quality of life of female medical students in Saudi Arabia through multiple interconnected physiological and psychological pathways. The condition is strongly associated with depression, anxiety, psychological distress, and stress, with menstruation severity positively correlating with all three mental health disorders. Beyond psychological effects, dysmenorrhea generates substantial physical symptoms including headache, joint pain, nausea, vomiting, and constipation, while also restricting daily activities, social interactions, and work capacity, leading to academic absenteeism. The underlying mechanisms involve elevated estrogen and prostaglandin levels that trigger menstrual pain, while hormonal fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone directly contribute to depressive symptoms through altered neurotransmitter availability, particularly reduced serotonin and norepinephrine. Students with severe to very severe menstrual pain and those requiring regular pain management demonstrate significantly lower mental health quality of life scores compared to those with mild to moderate symptoms. Secondary dysmenorrhea, often linked to endometriosis and uterine fibroids, represents a distinct pathological condition requiring different clinical consideration alongside primary dysmenorrhea's psychological burden.

title: From childhood struggles to adult strains: A systematic review of the impact of adverse childhood experiences on psycho-emotional functioning, pain outcomes, and quality of life in populations experiencing chronic pain
summary: Adverse childhood experiences (ACEs) represent a significant risk factor for the development of chronic pain and mental illness in adulthood, establishing a long-term pathway from early trauma to adult health outcomes. A systematic review of 18 studies demonstrates that childhood trauma is strongly associated with alterations in pain perception and the development of psycho-emotional disorders, suggesting that early adversity fundamentally shapes how individuals process and experience pain later in life. The evidence indicates that ACEs have a broad impact on multiple dimensions of functioning, including compromised quality of life in populations already experiencing chronic pain. These findings underscore the importance of recognizing the psychological and emotional sequelae of childhood trauma as integral components of chronic pain conditions. The cumulative effect of ACEs on psycho-emotional functioning, pain outcomes, and overall quality of life highlights the need for trauma-informed approaches in the assessment and management of chronic pain patients.

title: Factors associated with disability in women with chronic pelvic pain: A cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study identified multiple factors associated with disability in women with chronic pelvic pain, with psychosocial factors emerging as particularly influential. Pain catastrophising and depression were the strongest predictors of disability, with depression showing the largest effect (β = 12.03) followed by pain catastrophising (β = 6.40). Beyond these psychological factors, increased pain intensity, sexual dysfunction, and kinesiophobia were all significantly associated with greater disability. Physical inactivity and poor performance on functional assessments, such as the sit-to-stand test, further correlated with disability outcomes. The findings suggest that psychosocial factors play a dominant role in determining disability levels in chronic pelvic pain, highlighting the importance of addressing pain-related cognitions and mood disturbances alongside physical interventions in clinical management.

title: The Association Between Partner Reactions to Chronic Pain and Pain‐Related Outcomes: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review demonstrates that partner responses to chronic pain significantly influence pain-related outcomes through distinct mechanisms. Solicitous partner responses—characterized by attention and expressions of concern—consistently associate with worse outcomes including increased pain severity, pain behaviors, disability, pain interference, depression, and catastrophizing, likely through reinforcement of pain-focused attention and reduced activity engagement. Conversely, punishing or negative partner responses also produce deleterious effects by increasing disability, pain intensity, catastrophizing, and affective distress. Distracting responses show similar negative associations with pain severity, depression, and disability. In contrast, empathic and validating partner responses promote positive outcomes: validation increases emotional regulation and closeness while reducing pain intensity and helplessness, while empathic accuracy and autonomy support predict better physical function and pain management. Partner criticism and hostility independently associate with heightened pain intensity and catastrophizing. The review highlights that the social context of intimate partnerships fundamentally shapes chronic pain experiences, with interventions encouraging empathic responsiveness and autonomy support while discouraging solicitous or punishing patterns showing promise for improving pain-related outcomes.

title: Optimising Hormonal Therapy Before Declaring Failure in Endometriosis‐Associated Pain
summary: Endometriosis-associated pain encompasses both nociceptive and neuropathic-nociplastic components, functioning as a chronic pain condition requiring multimodal treatment strategies. Current evidence supports initiating hormonal therapy as the primary approach, with first-line options including combined oral contraceptives and progestogens, followed by gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists and antagonists for second-line treatment, though over 40% of patients fail to respond to medical therapy alone. The article emphasizes that hormonal therapy failure should not be declared before amenorrhea is achieved, and recommends a three-month GnRH analogue test to identify patients with predominant neuropathic-nociplastic pain components who require multidisciplinary management rather than surgical intervention. Referral to pain management services, while necessary for some patients, may paradoxically increase anxiety and depression, which themselves worsen nociplastic pain through catastrophizing mechanisms. The authors advocate for optimizing sequential hormonal treatments and assessing pain phenotypes before concluding therapeutic failure, reserving pain management and multidisciplinary approaches for patients with confirmed non-nociceptive pain components.

title: Masculine Identity, Body Image and Illness-Related Shame: Pathways to Psychological Distress in Men with Fibromyalgia
summary:
# Summary

Men with fibromyalgia experience significantly elevated depression and anxiety alongside compromised masculine identity and reduced body appreciation compared to healthy controls. The condition's invisible yet debilitating nature—characterized by widespread pain, fatigue, cognitive dysfunction, and sexual dysfunction—triggers a profound shift in bodily self-perception from strength and reliability to vulnerability and bodily failure. This disruption is particularly damaging because traditional masculine identity is culturally anchored to physical capability, competence, and independence, creating what the study identifies as a credibility and identity crisis. The research reveals that compromised masculine identity and illness-related shame emerge as the strongest psychological pathways to distress in men with fibromyalgia, operating alongside reduced body appreciation and pain-related functional impairment. Notably, body appreciation—which typically protects psychological wellbeing in healthy men—loses its protective association in the fibromyalgia population, suggesting the condition fundamentally disrupts normal psychological buffering mechanisms. These findings highlight how chronic pain conditions in men operate through gender-specific identity and shame mechanisms that warrant targeted psychological interventions addressing masculine identity renegotiation alongside symptom management.

title: Why sleep matters in chronic pain: evidence across the lifespan
summary:
# Sleep and Chronic Pain: Mechanisms Across the Lifespan

Sleep disturbance is nearly universal in chronic pain, affecting approximately three-quarters of adults with chronic pain conditions and potentially contributing to pain persistence rather than merely accompanying it. Poor sleep quality predicts pain emergence in younger populations and is associated with multiple pathophysiological mechanisms, including hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysregulation, elevated nocturnal cortisol, and cognitive dysfunction. Experimental evidence demonstrates that sleep restriction impairs the descending pain inhibitory system while enhancing pain facilitation, and sleep deprivation elevates pro-inflammatory cytokines through NF-κB and STAT pathway activation, directly promoting pain sensitization. At the cellular level, sleep loss reduces GABAergic inhibition in the periaqueductal gray, impairing pain inhibition, while chronic sleep deprivation increases amyloid-beta accumulation in the brain and disrupts neural circuits involving the amygdala and prefrontal cortex that regulate emotion and pain modulation. Specific sleep disruptions such as the alpha-delta electroencephalogram pattern and selective deep sleep disruption induce widespread pain, fatigue, and mood changes in healthy individuals. Cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia shows promise in improving sleep and reducing downstream pain in conditions like fibromyalgia, suggesting that targeting sleep disturbance may provide therapeutic benefits across the chronic pain spectrum.

title: Rural specialty care for Veterans with the chronic overlapping pain conditions: Fibromyalgia, migraine, or irritable bowel syndrome
summary:
# Summary

This study examines specialty care access for rural Veterans with chronic overlapping pain conditions (COPCs)—fibromyalgia, migraine, and irritable bowel syndrome—which affect over 50 million Americans and stem from nociplastic pain mechanisms. Nociplastic pain produces a constellation of central nervous system symptoms including widespread pain, fatigue, gastrointestinal disturbances, headaches, and sleep difficulties, often accompanied by elevated depression and anxiety. Rural Veterans with COPCs face significant healthcare disparities, receiving care predominantly from primary care providers (76.2% of cohort) while having substantially lower specialty care visits compared to urban counterparts, particularly those managed through rural clinics. Specialty care varies by condition type, with migraine patients most likely seen in neurology (29%), IBS patients in gastroenterology (25.9%), and fibromyalgia patients distributed across pain clinics, rheumatology, and mental health services. Rural Veterans with these conditions experience additional clinical complexity with multiple concurrent pain conditions, high symptom severity, and comorbid depression and anxiety, yet remain at increased risk for potentially risky prescribing practices like long-term opioids due to limited access to comprehensive multidisciplinary pain management. These findings highlight critical barriers to specialty care access in rural settings that contribute to suboptimal pain management for this vulnerable population.

title: Reimagining pain: the role of mental imagery in pain experience
summary:
# Summary

Mental imagery plays a critical role in amplifying pain experience and comorbid psychological distress, particularly in conditions like endometriosis. Negative pain-related imagery—characterized by high frequency, vividness, and emotional intensity—drives pain perception through neural simulation of pain experiences and threat-related interpretations, activating key pain-processing brain regions including the anterior insula, anterior cingulate cortex, and primary somatosensory cortex. The emotional evaluation and vivid quality of pain imagery, combined with negative appraisals of its meaning, predict greater pain severity, interference, anxiety, and depression. Conversely, therapeutic interventions harnessing imagery mechanisms show promise: guided imagery reduces various pain types, imagery rescripting decreases pain intensity and distress, and imagery-enhanced cognitive-behavioral approaches facilitate pain management by engaging emotional processing. These findings suggest that targeting pain-related mental imagery through cognitive and behavioral techniques represents a novel therapeutic pathway for managing chronic pain conditions in endometriosis and beyond.

title: Fear-induced hyperalgesia in quiescent inflammatory bowel disease
summary: This study demonstrates that pain-related fear learning significantly shapes pain perception in patients with quiescent inflammatory bowel disease, independent of active inflammation. Compared to healthy controls, IBD patients exhibited substantially enhanced pain intensity and unpleasantness following experimental pain exposure, with pain unpleasantness serving as the key mediator between fear learning and increased pain intensity. The research identifies fear-induced hyperalgesia as a central mechanism contributing to persistent abdominal pain in IBD, suggesting that maladaptive psychological responses play a critical role in pain chronification beyond the direct effects of visceral inflammation. This finding highlights the importance of the gut-brain axis and anticipatory processes in modulating pain perception, indicating that emotional reactivity and fear-avoidance behaviors perpetuate chronic pain even during periods of clinical remission. The study measured inflammatory biomarkers including TNF-α, IL-6, C-reactive protein, fecal calprotectin and lactoferrin, alongside stress markers such as salivary cortisol and alpha-amylase. These results suggest that psychological interventions targeting fear learning, such as exposure therapy, may be valuable therapeutic approaches for managing persistent pain in IBD patients.

title: Transcranial direct current stimulation of primary motor cortex reduces thermal pain
summary: This study demonstrates that anodal transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) of the primary motor cortex significantly reduces thermal pain perception in healthy subjects, with effect sizes ranging from 0.85 to 0.93, while cathodal stimulation paradoxically increases pain ratings relative to sham control. The analgesic mechanism involves M1-tDCS-induced opioid release and modulation of the sensory-discriminative aspects of pain processing. Notably, anodal stimulation produced a modest increase in reaction time in the ipsilateral hand, likely reflecting interhemispheric inhibitory effects, though this motor effect was independent of the analgesic response. Beyond this experimental demonstration, tDCS targeting the motor cortex has shown clinical promise across multiple chronic pain conditions including fibromyalgia, migraine, neuropathic pain, and pelvic pain. These findings establish the primary motor cortex as a viable non-invasive target for pain modulation and support further investigation of tDCS as a potential therapeutic approach for conditions where traditional pain management is inadequate.

title: Diminished anticipatory pleasure predicts anhedonia in women with endometrisois: a case-control study
summary: This case-control study reveals that women with uterine-deep endometriosis (UD endometriosis) exhibit significantly elevated anhedonia—the inability to experience pleasure—with diminished anticipatory pleasure being a key predictor of this psychiatric symptom. The chronic pain associated with endometriosis, particularly menstrual and pelvic pain, appears to drive disruption in reward processing through overlapping neural circuits, creating a neurobiological pathway linking pain to anhedonia. Importantly, the study identified a specific impairment in anticipatory pleasure (the "wanting" component) rather than consummatory pleasure (the "liking" component), suggesting disruption in motivational processes mediated by the anterior cingulate cortex and ventral striatum.
